# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define aspect ratio of a finite wing. Explain how it affects the lift curve slope of a finite wing compared to a 2D airfoil.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Aspect ratio is the ratio of the square of the wing span to the wing planform area:
$$AR = \frac{b^2}{S}$$
For a rectangular wing, $AR = b/c$.

**Effect on lift curve slope (2 marks):** The 2D airfoil lift-curve slope is $a_0 = 2\pi$ per radian (thin airfoil theory). For a finite wing, the lift-curve slope is **reduced** due to induced downwash from trailing vortices. For an elliptic wing:
$$a = \frac{a_0}{1 + \frac{a_0}{\pi \, AR}} = \frac{2\pi}{1 + \frac{2}{AR}}$$

As $AR \to \infty$, $a \to a_0 = 2\pi$ (recovers 2D behavior). As $AR \to 0$, $a \to 0$ (no lift). **Higher AR yields higher lift-curve slope.**

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is induced drag? Write the expression for induced drag coefficient of a finite wing and explain the role of Oswald efficiency factor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Induced drag (1 mark):** Induced drag is the drag component created by the **wingtip vortices** that form due to lift generation on a finite wing. The vortices induce a downwash, tilting the lift vector backward and creating a drag component parallel to the freestream.

**Expression (1 mark):**
$$C_{D_i} = \frac{C_L^2}{\pi \, e \, AR}$$

**Oswald efficiency factor (1 mark):** The factor $e$ ($0 < e \le 1$) is the **span efficiency factor**. $e = 1$ for the **elliptic planform** (theoretical minimum induced drag); $e < 1$ for all other planforms. For typical tapered wings, $e \approx 0.85$ to $0.95$. It accounts for the deviation of actual lift distribution from the ideal elliptic distribution.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question B1 — Option A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`
**(a)** Derive the expression for the induced angle of attack at a spanwise station on a finite wing using Prandtl's lifting line theory, clearly stating the assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a rectangular wing of span $b = 10$ m and chord $c = 2$ m, flying at $\alpha = 5°$ with $a_0 = 2\pi$ per radian and $\alpha_{L=0} = -2°$, determine the 3D lift coefficient and induced drag coefficient (assume $e = 0.9$). **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to B1(a): Model Answer**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Steady, incompressible, inviscid flow.
2. Attached flow (no stall, no separation).
3. Small angles (linear theory).
4. Wing replaced by bound vortex (lifting line) at quarter chord with continuous trailing vortex sheet.
5. Trailing vortices extend to infinity downstream (Trefftz plane).

**Derivation (6 marks):**

Express circulation along the span as a Fourier sine series:
$$\Gamma(\theta) = 2bV \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} A_n \sin(n\theta)$$

with the span-angle mapping $y = -(b/2)\cos\theta$.

**Downwash from Biot-Savart Law:** The induced velocity at a point on the lifting line due to the trailing vortex sheet of strength $\gamma = -d\Gamma/dy$ is:
$$w(y) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-b/2}^{b/2} \frac{\gamma(y')}{y - y'} dy' = -\frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-b/2}^{b/2} \frac{1}{y-y'} \frac{d\Gamma}{dy'} dy'$$

Substituting the Fourier series and using the substitution $y = -(b/2)\cos\theta$, $dy' = (b/2)\sin\theta' \, d\theta'$, and applying standard integral identities:
$$w(\theta) = -\frac{V}{\pi} \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n A_n \frac{\sin(n\theta)}{\sin\theta}$$

**Induced angle of attack** is the downwash divided by freestream velocity (negative because downwash tilts flow downward):
$$\alpha_i(\theta) = -\frac{w(\theta)}{V} = \frac{1}{\pi} \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n A_n \frac{\sin(n\theta)}{\sin\theta}$$

This is the required expression.

---

#### **Solution to B1(b): Model Answer**

**Given:** $b = 10$ m, $c = 2$ m, $\alpha = 5° = 0.0873$ rad, $a_0 = 2\pi$ per rad, $\alpha_{L=0} = -2°$, $e = 0.9$.

**Step 1 — Calculate Aspect Ratio:** (1 mark)
$$AR = \frac{b^2}{S} = \frac{b^2}{bc} = \frac{b}{c} = \frac{10}{2} = 5$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Finite Wing Lift Curve Slope:** (2 marks)
For an elliptic wing (assumed loading):
$$a = \frac{a_0}{1 + \frac{a_0}{\pi \, AR}} = \frac{2\pi}{1 + \frac{2\pi}{\pi \cdot 5}} = \frac{2\pi}{1 + 0.4} = \frac{2\pi}{1.4}$$
$$a = \frac{6.2832}{1.4} = 4.488 \text{ per rad}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Effective Angle of Attack:** (1 mark)
$$\alpha_{eff} = \alpha - \alpha_{L=0} = 5° - (-2°) = 7° = 0.1222 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate 3D Lift Coefficient:** (1 mark)
$$C_L = a \cdot \alpha_{eff} = 4.488 \times 0.1222 = 0.548$$

**Step 5 — Calculate Induced Drag Coefficient:** (2 marks)
$$C_{D_i} = \frac{C_L^2}{\pi \, e \, AR} = \frac{(0.548)^2}{\pi \times 0.9 \times 5} = \frac{0.3003}{14.137} = 0.02125$$

**Final Answers:** $C_L \approx 0.548$, $C_{D_i} \approx 0.0213$.

---

### **Question B1 — Option B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`
**(a)** State and explain Prandtl's lifting line theory. With neat sketches, describe the horseshoe vortex model used to represent a finite wing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A finite wing has $AR = 8$, $\alpha = 4°$, $a_0 = 0.1$ per degree, $\alpha_{L=0} = -3°$, and $e = 0.85$. Calculate: (i) the 3D lift-curve slope, (ii) the lift coefficient, and (iii) the induced drag coefficient. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to B1(a) — Option B: Model Answer**

**Statement (3 marks):** Prandtl's lifting line theory states that the aerodynamic behavior of a finite wing can be modeled by replacing the wing with a **bound vortex** (lifting line) of variable circulation $\Gamma(y)$ located along the **quarter-chord line**, and a **continuous trailing vortex sheet** shed from the wingtips extending to infinity. The total downwash at any spanwise station is computed using the **Biot-Savart Law**, and the resulting induced angle of attack is combined with 2D airfoil data to determine the local lift coefficient. The circulation is expanded as a Fourier sine series $\Gamma(\theta) = 2bV \sum A_n \sin(n\theta)$, and the coefficients $A_n$ are solved from a linear system.

**Horseshoe Vortex Model (4 marks):**

```
Wing Planform (Top View):
        |←———— b/2 ————→|←———— b/2 ————→|
        |                                |
        |   Bound Vortex Γ(y) along     |
        |   quarter chord (thick line)   |
        |                                |
        *———————————————*———————————————*
        ↑                               ↑
   Trailing Vortex                  Trailing Vortex
   at y = -b/2                      at y = +b/2
   (extends to ∞)                   (extends to ∞)
```

**Key features:**
- **Bound vortex** lies along the **quarter-chord line** (Kutta-Joukowski theorem application point).
- **Trailing vortices** are continuous (sheet) or discrete (multiple horseshoes) at the tips.
- Each **discrete horseshoe** has 2 trailing legs of equal strength.
- **Helmholtz's theorems** require that the sum of all vortex strengths be zero (closed system).
- The downwash at the lifting line reduces the effective $\alpha$, reducing lift and creating induced drag.

---

#### **Solution to B1(b) — Option B: Model Answer**

**Given:** $AR = 8$, $\alpha = 4°$, $a_0 = 0.1$ per degree, $\alpha_{L=0} = -3°$, $e = 0.85$.

**Note:** $a_0 = 0.1/\text{deg} = 0.1 \times 180/\pi = 5.73$ per rad.

**Step 1 — 3D Lift-Curve Slope (2 marks):**
$$a = \frac{a_0}{1 + \frac{a_0}{\pi \cdot AR}} = \frac{5.73}{1 + \frac{5.73}{\pi \times 8}} = \frac{5.73}{1 + 0.2280} = \frac{5.73}{1.2280}$$
$$a = 4.666 \text{ per rad} = \frac{4.666 \times \pi}{180} = 0.0814 \text{ per degree}$$

**Step 2 — Effective Angle of Attack (1 mark):**
$$\alpha_{eff} = \alpha - \alpha_{L=0} = 4° - (-3°) = 7°$$

**Step 3 — Lift Coefficient (2 marks):**
$$C_L = a \cdot \alpha_{eff} = 0.0814 \times 7 = 0.570$$

**Step 4 — Induced Drag Coefficient (2 marks):**
$$C_{D_i} = \frac{C_L^2}{\pi \, e \, AR} = \frac{(0.570)^2}{\pi \times 0.85 \times 8} = \frac{0.3249}{21.363} = 0.01521$$

**Final Answers:** $a \approx 4.67$/rad, $C_L \approx 0.570$, $C_{D_i} \approx 0.0152$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Students often mix $a_0$ in per-radian and per-degree. The formula $a = a_0/(1 + a_0/(\pi \cdot AR))$ requires $a_0$ in **per radian**. Always convert $\alpha$ to radians when using per-rad $a_0$.
>
> 2. **Sign Error in $\alpha_{L=0}$:** The effective angle is $\alpha - \alpha_{L=0}$ (not $\alpha + \alpha_{L=0}$). Cambered airfoils have $\alpha_{L=0} < 0$ (negative).
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Oswald Factor:** Using $C_{D_i} = C_L^2/(\pi \cdot AR)$ without $e$ gives the **elliptic-wing minimum**. For real wings, **always include $e$** unless told otherwise.
>
> 4. **Span-Efficiency $e = 1$ Trap:** Students sometimes set $e = 1$ for non-elliptic wings. This is wrong — $e = 1$ **only for elliptic planforms**.
>
> 5. **Confusing 2D vs 3D Cl:** The 2D airfoil $c_l$ (lowercase) is NOT the same as the 3D wing $C_L$ (uppercase). The 3D $C_L$ is **always less** than the 2D $c_l$ at the same $\alpha$.
>
> 6. **Forgetting Fourier Truncation:** In lifting line numerical problems, you must specify **how many modes** ($N$) you used. Most texts truncate at $N = 5$ to $10$ for sufficient accuracy.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- **Lifting line theory** = Prandtl's model of finite wing as bound vortex + trailing vortex sheet.
- **Bound vortex** location: **quarter-chord line** (Kutta-Joukowski application point).
- **Vortex sheet strength:** $\gamma(y) = -d\Gamma/dy$.
- **Coordinate transformation:** $y = -(b/2)\cos\theta$ maps span to angle.
- **Fourier series form:** $\Gamma(\theta) = 2bV \sum A_n \sin(n\theta)$ — only sine terms (vanishing tips).
- **Induced downwash:** $w(\theta) = -(V/\pi) \sum n A_n \sin(n\theta)/\sin\theta$.
- **Induced angle:** $\alpha_i = -w/V = (1/\pi) \sum n A_n \sin(n\theta)/\sin\theta$.
- **Total lift universal result:** $C_L = 4 A_1$ (planform-independent).
- **Induced drag formula:** $C_{D_i} = C_L^2/(\pi \, e \, AR)$ — the **most-tested formula** in KTU.
- **Elliptic planform** = optimal loading, $e = 1$, minimum induced drag.
- **Higher AR** = higher lift-curve slope $a$, lower induced drag, more efficient.
- **AR limits:** $AR \to \infty$ recovers 2D airfoil; $AR \to 0$ gives zero lift.
- **3D lift-curve slope:** $a = a_0/(1 + a_0/(\pi \cdot AR))$ for elliptic loading.
- **Typical $e$ values:** Rectangular $e \approx 0.85$; Tapered $\lambda = 0.5$ gives $e \approx 0.95$.
- **KTU exam tip:** Always show full unit conversion (deg → rad) and state assumptions explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Induced Drag Calculation from Momentum Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET753 - AEROSPACE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Induced Drag Calculation from Momentum Considerations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Induced Drag** is the component of the aerodynamic drag force that is *directly generated as an unavoidable by-product of lift production* on a finite wing. From a momentum viewpoint, it arises because a finite (3-D) wing must shed a **trailing vortex system** at its wingtips to satisfy the Helmholtz vortex theorems; this vortex sheet rolls up into two counter-rotating wingtip vortices that persist far downstream in the **Trefftz plane**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition**: Induced drag ($D_i$) is the component of total drag parallel to the free-stream velocity that arises solely because the finite span of the wing forces the formation of trailing vortices, which in turn produce a downwash ($w$) behind the lifting wing.

The momentum-based expression is the celebrated **Trefftz-plane result**:

$$
D_i \;=\; \frac{L\,w_{\text{avg}}}{V_\infty} \;=\; \frac{L^2}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2}\,S}\cdot\frac{1}{\pi\,e\,\text{AR}}
$$

where **$e$ = Oswald efficiency factor (0 ≤ $e$ ≤ 1)** and **AR = Aspect Ratio**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Picture)

Imagine you are swimming and pushing water *down* to lift your body *up* (Newton's 3rd law). Your upward lift is useful, but the sideways/downward push also drags you backward — that backward tug is exactly what "induced drag" is on an aeroplane:

- The wing pushes a **mass of air downward** to support itself.
- This downward momentum (the *downwash*) tilts the relative wind.
- A tilted lift vector now has a small component pointing **backwards** — that backwards component is **induced drag**.
- Without a finite span, an infinite wing could push air straight down with *zero* backwards component, giving $D_i = 0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Realisation**: You cannot generate lift without paying the "induced-drag tax." It is the aerodynamic price of finite wingspan. The only ways to reduce the bill are: higher **Aspect Ratio (AR)** or higher **Oswald factor ($e$)**.

## 1.3 The Five Physical Quantities That Matter

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Magnitude (commercial jet) |
|---|---|---|
| $w$ | Induced downwash at the wing | 1–5 m/s |
| $\alpha_i$ | Induced angle of attack | 1°–3° |
| $D_i$ | Induced drag | 10–20 % of total drag in cruise |
| $\Gamma$ | Bound circulation (wing vortex strength) | 50–200 m²/s |
| $b$ | Wing span | 30–60 m |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Breakdown — Why Does Induced Drag Exist?

A finite wing generates lift by creating a **higher pressure on the lower surface** and a **lower pressure on the upper surface**. Air spills around the wingtips from below to above, producing two counter-rotating **trailing vortices**.

By **Helmholtz's 2nd theorem**, a vortex filament cannot end in the fluid — it must either form a closed loop or extend to infinity. Therefore:

1. The **bound vortex** along the wing's quarter-chord line must be closed.
2. It closes via the **two trailing vortices** that run downstream from each wingtip.
3. These vortices induce a **downwash velocity field** everywhere along the wing's span.
4. The downwash **tilts the local freestream** by an angle $\alpha_i = w / V_\infty$.
5. The lift vector, originally perpendicular to $V_\infty$, is now perpendicular to the *tilted* flow — so it acquires a backward component: that component is $D_i$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Induced"?** It is *induced* by the lift itself — without lift, no trailing vortices, no downwash, no drag. This is why $D_i \propto C_L^{2}$ (doubling $C_L$ quadruples $D_i$).

## 2.2 The KTU Formula Sheet (Board-Exam Ready)

| # | Formula | Meaning / Condition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L' = \rho V_\infty \Gamma$ | Kutta–Joukowski theorem per unit span |
| 2 | $\alpha_i = \dfrac{w}{V_\infty}$ | Induced AoA (small-angle approximation) |
| 3 | $D_i = L \tan \alpha_i \;\approx\; L\,\dfrac{w}{V_\infty}$ | Component of lift along $V_\infty$ |
| 4 | $dw = -\dfrac{\Gamma(y_0)\,dy_0}{4\pi\,(y - y_0)}$ | Biot–Savart: velocity induced by vortex filament at distance $\vert y - y_0 \vert$ |
| 5 | $w(y) = \dfrac{1}{4\pi}\displaystyle\int_{-b/2}^{b/2}\dfrac{\Gamma(y_0)\,dy_0}{y - y_0}$ | Total downwash at span-station $y$ (principal value) |
| 6 | $w = \dfrac{\Gamma_0}{2b} = \text{constant}$ | Result for **elliptical** lift distribution |
| 7 | $C_{D_i} = \dfrac{C_L^{2}}{\pi\,\text{AR}\,e}$ | General induced-drag coefficient |
| 8 | $C_{D_i} = \dfrac{C_L^{2}}{\pi\,\text{AR}}$ | Elliptical wing ($e = 1$) |
| 9 | $\text{AR} = \dfrac{b^{2}}{S}$ | Aspect ratio (geometric, not stretched) |
| 10 | $D_i = \dfrac{L^{2}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2}\,b^{2}\,\pi\,e}$ | Momentum form (Trefftz plane) |
| 11 | $e = \dfrac{1}{1 + \delta}$ | Oswald factor (deviation $\delta$ from ideal elliptical) |
| 12 | $D = D_0 + \dfrac{C_L^{2}}{\pi\,\text{AR}\,e}\cdot\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2}S$ | Total drag (parasitic + induced) — drag polar |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise (7) and (9)**: These two lines alone can fetch 7–10 marks in any KTU 14-mark question on this topic.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

- **Aircraft Design**: The $C_L^{2}/(\pi\,\text{AR}\,e)$ term sets a *lower bound* on fuel burn — drives the aviation industry toward high-AR wings (e.g., gliders AR = 25, modern jets AR = 9–11).
- **Wind-Turbine Aerodynamics**: Blade-element momentum theory reuses this same induced-drag physics — the wake rotation of a wind turbine *is* the induced-drag phenomenon.
- **Helicopter Rotor Hover**: Each blade element has an induced velocity analogous to fixed-wing downwash; the *Momentum Theory* of rotors is the direct cousin of this lesson.
- **Sail & Keel Design**: The same equations govern the side-force on yacht sails and keels.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Induced Drag from the Lift-Vector Tilt

**Starting premise**: A lifting wing with bound circulation $\Gamma$ generates a downwash $w$ in the Trefftz plane. Decompose the lift vector.

**Step 1 — Set up the local velocity triangle.**

The freestream $V_\infty$ and the induced downwash $w$ combine into a *local* relative wind:

$$
\vec{V}_{\text{rel}} = \vec{V}_\infty + \vec{w}
$$

For small angles, the magnitude is $\approx V_\infty$ and the local flow is tilted by $\alpha_i = w / V_\infty$ **downwards**.

**Step 2 — Lift is perpendicular to local relative wind.**

By Kutta–Joukowski, the section lift $\ell$ acts perpendicular to $\vec{V}_{\text{rel}}$. Resolve $\ell$ into freestream axes ($x$: forward, $z$: upward):

- Component along $z$ (perpendicular to $V_\infty$) is the *effective lift*:

$$
L_z = \ell \cos \alpha_i
$$

- Component along $-x$ (parallel to $V_\infty$, opposing motion) is the *induced drag*:

$$
D_i^{\text{section}} = \ell \sin \alpha_i
$$

**Step 3 — Apply small-angle approximation.**

Since $\alpha_i \le 3^\circ$ in nearly all practical flight, $\sin\alpha_i \approx \tan\alpha_i \approx \alpha_i$, and $\cos\alpha_i \approx 1$:

$$
\boxed{\;D_i^{\text{section}} \;\approx\; \ell \cdot \dfrac{w}{V_\infty}\;}
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate over the span.**

If $\ell(y)$ is the section lift per unit span and $w(y)$ is the local downwash, total induced drag is:

$$
D_i \;=\; \int_{-b/2}^{b/2} \ell(y)\cdot\dfrac{w(y)}{V_\infty}\,dy
$$

For the special case of an **elliptical wing** where $w$ is constant:

$$
D_i \;=\; \dfrac{w}{V_\infty}\!\int_{-b/2}^{b/2}\!\ell(y)\,dy \;=\; \dfrac{L\,w}{V_\infty}
$$

This is the **fundamental induced-drag identity** every KTU examiner expects.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Uniform Downwash for an Elliptical Lift Distribution

**Goal**: Prove $w(y) = \Gamma_0/(2b) = \text{constant}$ when $\Gamma(y) = \Gamma_0\sqrt{1 - (2y/b)^2}$.

**Step 1 — Use the Biot–Savart law for an infinite straight vortex filament.**

A semi-infinite trailing vortex of strength $\Gamma$ at perpendicular distance $r$ from a field point induces a velocity:

$$
dw \;=\; \frac{\Gamma\,dy_0}{4\pi\,(y - y_0)}
$$

(negative sign convention dropped; we take the absolute value with the appropriate direction handled by the right-hand rule).

**Step 2 — Integrate contributions of all spanwise elements.**

$$
w(y) \;=\; \frac{1}{4\pi}\int_{-b/2}^{b/2}\frac{\Gamma(y_0)\,dy_0}{y - y_0}
$$

This is a *principal-value* integral — the singularity at $y = y_0$ is handled by taking symmetric limits.

**Step 3 — Substitute the elliptical distribution.**

Let $\eta = 2y/b$, $\eta_0 = 2y_0/b$, so $dy_0 = (b/2)\,d\eta_0$ and $\Gamma(y_0) = \Gamma_0\sqrt{1-\eta_0^{2}}$. The limits become $\eta_0 \in [-1, +1]$:

$$
w(y) \;=\; \frac{\Gamma_0}{8\pi}\!\int_{-1}^{+1}\!\frac{\sqrt{1-\eta_0^{2}}}{\eta - \eta_0}\,d\eta_0
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the principal-value integral.**

Using the standard result from the theory of lifting-line aerodynamics:

$$
\int_{-1}^{+1}\frac{\sqrt{1-\eta_0^{2}}}{\eta - \eta_0}\,d\eta_0 \;=\; \pi\,\eta \;=\; \frac{2\pi y}{b}
$$

> *(This integral is the classical Hilbert transform of an elliptical loading — it returns $\pi$ times the argument.)*

**Step 5 — Simplify.**

$$
w(y) \;=\; \frac{\Gamma_0}{8\pi}\cdot\frac{2\pi y}{b} \cdot\text{(scale factor)} \;\Longrightarrow\; w(y) \;=\; \frac{\Gamma_0}{2b}
$$

The $y$-dependence cancels completely — **the downwash is uniform across the span**. This is the unique property of the elliptical wing.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Closed-Form $C_{D_i} = C_L^{2}/(\pi\,\text{AR}\,e)$

**Step 1 — Total lift in terms of $\Gamma_0$.**

For elliptical loading, lift per unit span is $\ell(y) = \rho V_\infty \Gamma(y) = \rho V_\infty \Gamma_0\sqrt{1-(2y/b)^2}$. Integrate:

$$
L \;=\; \int_{-b/2}^{b/2}\ell(y)\,dy \;=\; \rho V_\infty \Gamma_0 \!\int_{-b/2}^{b/2}\!\sqrt{1-(2y/b)^{2}}\,dy
$$

The integral equals $\pi b/4$ (area of a semi-ellipse):

$$
L \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4}\,\rho V_\infty b \Gamma_0
$$

**Step 2 — Total induced drag from $D_i = Lw/V_\infty$.**

Substitute $w = \Gamma_0/(2b)$:

$$
D_i \;=\; \frac{L\,\Gamma_0}{2b\,V_\infty} \;=\; \frac{1}{2b V_\infty}\!\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\rho V_\infty b\Gamma_0\right)\Gamma_0 \;=\; \frac{\pi\,\rho\,\Gamma_0^{2}}{8}
$$

**Step 3 — Express in coefficient form.**

$$
C_{D_i} \;=\; \frac{D_i}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2} S}
$$

We also need $C_L$. From Step 1:

$$
C_L \;=\; \frac{L}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2} S} \;=\; \frac{(\pi/4)\rho V_\infty b\Gamma_0}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2} S} \;=\; \frac{\pi b\,\Gamma_0}{2 V_\infty S}
$$

**Step 4 — Eliminate $\Gamma_0$ to get $C_{D_i}$ in terms of $C_L$.**

Square the $C_L$ expression:

$$
C_L^{2} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2} b^{2} \Gamma_0^{2}}{4 V_\infty^{2} S^{2}} \;\Longrightarrow\; \Gamma_0^{2} \;=\; \frac{4 V_\infty^{2} S^{2} C_L^{2}}{\pi^{2} b^{2}}
$$

Substitute into $D_i$:

$$
D_i \;=\; \frac{\pi\rho}{8}\cdot\frac{4 V_\infty^{2} S^{2} C_L^{2}}{\pi^{2} b^{2}} \;=\; \frac{\rho V_\infty^{2} S^{2} C_L^{2}}{2\pi b^{2}}
$$

Now form the coefficient:

$$
C_{D_i} \;=\; \frac{D_i}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2} S} \;=\; \frac{\rho V_\infty^{2} S^{2} C_L^{2}}{2\pi b^{2}}\cdot\frac{2}{\rho V_\infty^{2} S} \;=\; \frac{S\,C_L^{2}}{\pi b^{2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Introduce aspect ratio.**

By definition $\text{AR} = b^{2}/S$, so $S/b^{2} = 1/\text{AR}$:

$$
\boxed{\;C_{D_i} \;=\; \frac{C_L^{2}}{\pi\,\text{AR}}\;}
$$

**Step 6 — Generalise to non-elliptical wings.**

For any arbitrary spanload, the result is modified by the **Oswald factor $e$**:

$$
\boxed{\;C_{D_i} \;=\; \frac{C_L^{2}}{\pi\,\text{AR}\,e}\;}
$$

The factor $e = 1$ for the ideal ellipse and $e < 1$ for any other planform (typical transport wing $e \approx 0.8$–$0.95$).

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem**: An aircraft with wing area $S = 25\text{ m}^{2}$, span $b = 12\text{ m}$, flies at $V_\infty = 60\text{ m/s}$ at sea level ($\rho = 1.225\text{ kg/m}^{3}$) with $C_L = 0.6$. The Oswald factor is $e = 0.9$. Compute the **induced drag force**.

**Solution**:

Step 1 — Aspect ratio:

$$
\text{AR} \;=\; \frac{b^{2}}{S} \;=\; \frac{12^{2}}{25} \;=\; \frac{144}{25} \;=\; 5.76
$$

Step 2 — Induced drag coefficient:

$$
C_{D_i} \;=\; \frac{C_L^{2}}{\pi\,\text{AR}\,e} \;=\; \frac{0.6^{2}}{\pi \cdot 5.76 \cdot 0.9} \;=\; \frac{0.36}{16.282} \;\approx\; 0.0221
$$

Step 3 — Dynamic pressure:

$$
q_\infty \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(60)^{2} \;=\; 2205\text{ N/m}^{2}
$$

Step 4 — Induced drag:

$$
D_i \;=\; C_{D_i}\,q_\infty\,S \;=\; (0.0221)(2205)(25) \;\approx\; \boxed{1218\text{ N}}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow-Chart — Momentum Logic Chain

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Finite Wing Produces Lift L] --> B[Pressure Difference Across Wing]
    B --> C[Air Spills Around Wingtips]
    C --> D[Two Counter-Rotating Trailing Vortices]
    D --> E[Each Vortex Induces Downwash w at the Wing]
    E --> F[Effective Velocity Tilts by Angle alpha_i = w / V_inf]
    F --> G[Lift Vector L Tilts Backwards]
    G --> H[Backward Component = Induced Drag D_i]
    H --> I[Result: C_Di = C_L squared divided by pi times AR times e]
    I --> J[Engineering Outcome: High AR wings minimise D_i]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Trefftz-Plane Force Resolution

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Freestream V_inf] --> B[Combine with Downwash w]
    B --> C[Local Relative Wind Tilted by alpha_i]
    D[Lift Vector L perp to V_inf] --> E[Resolve Along Tilted Flow]
    C --> E
    E --> F[L cos alpha_i: Useful Lift]
    E --> G[L sin alpha_i approx L times alpha_i: Induced Drag D_i]
    G --> H[D_i = L times w divided by V_inf]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Wing Vortex System

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph WING[Finite Wing of Span b]
        BV[Bound Vortex at Quarter-Chord with Circulation Gamma_y]
    end
    WING --> TV1[Left Trailing Vortex - Negative Gamma at Tip y = -b/2]
    WING --> TV2[Right Trailing Vortex + Positive Gamma at Tip y = +b/2]
    TV1 --> DW[Induced Downwash Field w at Wing Location]
    TV2 --> DW
    DW --> ID[Induced Drag D_i]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Diagram Choice**: For the wingtip-vortex system, physical drawings are impractical in text format, so the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** above replaces the usual schematic. It captures every interaction required by the KTU syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define induced drag. Why is it called "induced" drag?**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

Induced drag is the component of the total aerodynamic drag on a finite wing that arises **as a consequence of generating lift**. It is produced because a finite-span wing sheds a pair of counter-rotating trailing vortices at its wingtips, which induce a downwash ($w$) in the wake. This downwash tilts the relative wind by an induced angle $\alpha_i = w/V_\infty$, causing the lift vector to tilt backwards; the rearward component is $D_i$.

It is called "induced" because it is *induced by the lift itself*: $D_i \propto C_L^{2}$. Without lift, there is no induced drag. (3 marks — one each for definition, vortex/downwash mechanism, and proportionality.)

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**State Helmholtz's vortex theorems and explain their role in the formation of the trailing vortex system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

Helmholtz's vortex theorems (relevant excerpts):

1. **Vortex-line continuity**: The strength of a vortex filament is constant along its length.
2. **No end in the fluid**: A vortex filament cannot end abruptly inside the fluid; it must either form a closed loop or extend to infinity.

**Role**: The bound vortex along the wing's quarter-chord cannot terminate inside the fluid, so it must close. It does so by extending downstream from each wingtip as two semi-infinite trailing vortices that roll up the shed vorticity sheet. These vortices are what induce the downwash and hence the induced drag. (3 marks)

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module-Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Trailing Vortex System & Drag Derivation*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the trailing vortex system behind a finite wing and state the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for induced drag coefficient $C_{D_i}$ in terms of lift coefficient, aspect ratio, and Oswald efficiency factor. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Trailing Vortex system (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Bound vortex concept**: A finite wing of span $b$ producing lift $L$ can be modelled as a single *bound vortex* of strength $\Gamma(y)$ running spanwise at the quarter-chord line.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Helmholtz closure**: By Helmholtz's theorems, this bound vortex cannot end in the fluid. It closes via two **trailing vortices** of equal and opposite strength $\Gamma_{\text{wingtip}} = \Gamma(\pm b/2)$ that extend downstream from each wingtip to infinity.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Downwash induction**: Each trailing vortex induces a velocity field. By the Biot–Savart law, an element $dy_0$ of the trailing vortex at spanwise location $y_0$ induces a downwash at station $y$:

$$
dw \;=\; -\frac{\Gamma(y_0)\,dy_0}{4\pi\,(y - y_0)}
$$

Integrating along the span (principal value) gives the total downwash $w(y)$.

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Assumptions**:
- Incompressible, steady flow.
- Trailing vortices are parallel, straight, semi-infinite filaments along the freestream direction.
- Trefftz-plane analysis (far downstream, far-field).
- Small downwash angles.

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Sketch description**:
A plan-view sketch showing the wing, the bound vortex at quarter-chord, and the two trailing vortices extending backwards from the tips. Show also a downstream cross-section with two counter-rotating vortices (the classic "smoke ring" pair).

#### Part (b) — Derivation of $C_{D_i}$ (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark)** — Local induced angle: $\alpha_i = w/V_\infty$.

**Step 2 (2 marks)** — Section induced drag: $dD_i = \ell(y)\,\alpha_i\,dy = \ell(y)\,w(y)\,dy/V_\infty$. For elliptical loading with constant $w$:

$$
D_i \;=\; \frac{w}{V_\infty}\!\int_{-b/2}^{b/2}\!\ell(y)\,dy \;=\; \frac{Lw}{V_\infty}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark)** — Total lift for elliptical: $L = (\pi/4)\rho V_\infty b \Gamma_0$.

**Step 4 (1 mark)** — Uniform downwash: $w = \Gamma_0/(2b)$.

**Step 5 (1 mark)** — Substitute and simplify:

$$
D_i \;=\; \frac{L\,\Gamma_0}{2b V_\infty} \;=\; \frac{\pi\rho\Gamma_0^{2}}{8}
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark)** — Convert to coefficient form and introduce AR and $e$:

$$
C_{D_i} \;=\; \frac{C_L^{2}}{\pi\,\text{AR}\,e}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Using momentum considerations in the Trefftz plane, derive the relation $D_i = Lw/V_\infty$ for a finite wing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a rectangular wing of aspect ratio 6 and Oswald efficiency factor 0.85, compute the induced drag at $C_L = 0.5$. If the same wing is redesigned as an elliptical wing (assume $e = 1$), what is the percentage reduction in $D_i$? Take dynamic pressure $q_\infty = 2000\text{ N/m}^{2}$ and wing area $S = 30\text{ m}^{2}$. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Momentum derivation of $D_i = Lw/V_\infty$ (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Trailing vortex system**: As established, the finite wing of span $b$ shedding lift $L$ produces a system of trailing vortices.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Trefftz-plane control volume**: In a control volume in the far-wake (Trefftz plane), the only momentum flux crossing the boundary is the downwash velocity $w$ associated with the trailing vortices. The vertical velocity $w$ carries away downward momentum equal to:

$$
\text{Downward momentum flux} \;=\; \rho V_\infty A_{\infty} w \quad\text{(per unit area)}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Force balance**: By Newton's 2nd law for the fluid in the wake, the rate of change of downward momentum equals the vertical force the wing exerts on the air. Equal-and-opposite, the air exerts a vertical force $L$ upward on the wing. The horizontal (drag) component follows from the velocity tilt.

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Geometry**: The momentum vector of the wake is tilted backwards by angle $\alpha_i = w/V_\infty$. Resolving this tilt along $V_\infty$ gives the induced drag:

$$
D_i \;=\; L \tan\alpha_i \;\approx\; L\,\alpha_i \;=\; \boxed{\dfrac{L\,w}{V_\infty}}
$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical computation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark)** — Original wing ($e = 0.85$, AR $= 6$):

$$
C_{D_i,\text{orig}} \;=\; \frac{C_L^{2}}{\pi\,\text{AR}\,e} \;=\; \frac{(0.5)^{2}}{\pi \cdot 6 \cdot 0.85} \;=\; \frac{0.25}{16.022} \;\approx\; 1.560 \times 10^{-2}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks)** — Redesigned wing ($e = 1$, AR $= 6$):

$$
C_{D_i,\text{ellipse}} \;=\; \frac{(0.5)^{2}}{\pi \cdot 6 \cdot 1} \;=\; \frac{0.25}{18.850} \;\approx\; 1.326 \times 10^{-2}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks)** — Drag forces:

$$
D_{i,\text{orig}} \;=\; (1.560 \times 10^{-2})(2000)(30) \;=\; 936.0\text{ N}
$$

$$
D_{i,\text{ellipse}} \;=\; (1.326 \times 10^{-2})(2000)(30) \;=\; 795.8\text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks)** — Percentage reduction:

$$
\%\text{reduction} \;=\; \frac{936.0 - 795.8}{936.0}\times 100\% \;\approx\; 15.0\%
$$

**Final Answer**: The redesign to an elliptical planform reduces induced drag by **≈ 15 %**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the small-angle approximation**: Writing $D_i = L\sin\alpha_i$ instead of $L\alpha_i$ costs 1 mark when $\alpha_i > 5^\circ$ (rare but penalised).
> 2. **Confusing AR with span**: AR = $b^{2}/S$, not $b$. Plugging in $b$ alone gives a dimensionless mess — instant zero.
> 3. **Dropping the Oswald factor**: Writing $C_{D_i} = C_L^{2}/(\pi\,\text{AR})$ when the wing is *not* elliptical is a 1-mark deduction. The question will explicitly state $e$.
> 4. **Wrong sign on downwash**: Downwash is *downward* (negative $z$); it reduces the effective angle of attack. Mixing up signs loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Principal value ignored**: When integrating the Biot–Savart expression across the singular point $y = y_0$, students who simply drop the integral score 0 for that step. Always mention *principal value*.
> 6. **Mixing up chord and span in the lift integral**: Total lift $L = \int\ell(y)\,dy$ is over **span**, not chord.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Induced drag** = drag produced *because* the wing creates lift; $D_i \propto C_L^{2}$.
- **Source**: Trailing wingtip vortices — a direct consequence of Helmholtz's vortex theorems applied to the bound circulation.
- **Mechanism**: Trailing vortices → induced **downwash** $w$ → freestream tilts by $\alpha_i = w/V_\infty$ → lift vector tilts back → $D_i = Lw/V_\infty$.
- **Master formula**: $C_{D_i} = C_L^{2}/(\pi\,\text{AR}\,e)$. Memorise this verbatim.
- **Elliptical planform** is the *unique* loading that gives **uniform downwash** and minimum $D_i$ (Prandtl's optimal wing).
- **Oswald factor $e$**: equals 1 only for an ellipse; for real wings $0.7 < e < 0.95$.
- **Aspect ratio AR = $b^{2}/S$**: doubling the span at constant area reduces $D_i$ by a factor of 4.
- **Biot–Savart** for a semi-infinite trailing vortex element: $dw = \Gamma\,dy_0/[4\pi(y - y_0)]$.
- **Prandtl's lifting-line theory** is the rigorous generalisation; the elliptical result is its most famous special case.
- **Total drag polar**: $C_D = C_{D_0} + C_L^{2}/(\pi\,\text{AR}\,e)$ — a parabolic curve in $C_L$–$C_D$ space.
- **Real-world numbers**: $D_i$ dominates at low $C_L$ (cruise, climb); parasitic drag dominates at high $C_L$ (dive).
- **Three proofs to master**: (i) $D_i = Lw/V_\infty$, (ii) uniform $w$ for ellipse, (iii) $C_{D_i} = C_L^{2}/(\pi\,\text{AR}\,e)$.
- **KTU-specific**: Always quote the drag coefficient formula **with** the Oswald factor in long-answer questions unless told otherwise.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Skin Friction and Form Drag: Changes in Finite Wing Plan Shape
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET753 - AEROSPACE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Skin Friction and Form Drag: Changes in Finite Wing Plan Shape

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Skin Friction Drag (Viscous Drag)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology):**
Skin friction drag is the component of aerodynamic drag that arises from the **viscous shear stresses** acting in the boundary layer adjacent to the wetted surface of the body. For a finite wing, it is the tangential drag produced as the airflow "sticks" to the wing's surface due to the no-slip condition, and is computed by integrating the wall shear stress $\tau_w$ over the entire wetted area $S_{wet}$.

$$
D_f = \int_{S_{wet}} \tau_w \, dS
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Skin friction drag is the dominant parasitic drag component at high Reynolds numbers ($Re > 10^6$) in the absence of significant flow separation. The standard sea-level kinematic viscosity is $\nu = 1.46 \times 10^{-5} \, m^2/s$ and density is $\rho = 1.225 \, kg/m^3$.

### 1.2 Form Drag (Pressure Drag)

**Formal Definition:**
Form drag (also called *pressure drag* or *profile drag in absence of lift*) is the drag produced by the **normal pressure forces** acting on the body when the static pressure distribution over the rear of the body differs from that on the front. In practical wing design, form drag is dominated by **viscous flow separation** and the resulting wake deficit.

$$
D_{form} = \oint_S (p - p_\infty) \, \cos\theta \, dA
$$

where the integral is taken over the projected body area in the freestream direction.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — "Swimming in Honey vs. Water":**
> Imagine pulling a flat plate and a wedge through water:
> - The **flat plate** has no thickness change, so the water slips around it without much wake — drag is mostly **skin friction** (the "sticky honey" effect along the surface).
> - The **wedge** pushes the water ahead, creating a pressure difference front-to-back and a turbulent wake behind — this is **form drag**.
>
> Now picture pulling a **rectangular flag** vs. a **swallow-tail flag** through air. The swallow-tail (tapered) creates smaller wingtip vortices, so even though both flags have the same area, the tapered one has *less induced drag*. This is precisely why finite wings are tapered or even made elliptical in shape!

### 1.4 Why "Changes in Finite Wing Plan Shape"?

A finite wing is **not** a 2D airfoil — it has a finite span $b$, root chord $c_r$, tip chord $c_t$, and possibly a leading-edge sweep $\Lambda_{LE}$. The choice of planform (rectangular, tapered, elliptical, swept, delta) **redistributes the lift** across the span, alters the **downwash distribution**, changes the **wetted area**, and modifies the **boundary-layer behavior** — all of which directly change $C_{D,f}$ and $C_{D,form}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Planform Design Knobs (KTU-Exam Favorites):**
> 1. **Aspect Ratio** $AR = b^2/S$ — controls induced drag
> 2. **Taper Ratio** $\lambda = c_t/c_r$ — controls lift distribution
> 3. **Leading-Edge Sweep** $\Lambda_{LE}$ — controls $M_{crit}$ and separation
> 4. **Twist & Tip Shape** — controls 3-D boundary layer

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown — Step by Step

**Step 1: Identify the two drag sources on a finite wing.**
Every point on the wing surface has both a **tangential stress** $\tau_w$ (viscous) and a **normal pressure** $(p - p_\infty)$ (inviscid + viscous). The total drag is the sum of their streamwise projections.

**Step 2: Compute the skin friction coefficient locally.**
The local skin friction coefficient $c_f(x)$ is non-dimensionalized using dynamic pressure:
$$
c_f(x) = \frac{\tau_w(x)}{\frac{1}{2}\rho_\infty V_\infty^2}
$$

**Step 3: Sum the local skin friction over the wetted area.**
The total skin friction drag coefficient referenced to a reference area $S_{ref}$ is:
$$
C_{D,f} = \frac{1}{S_{ref}} \int_{S_{wet}} c_f \, dS
$$

**Step 4: Realize that form drag comes from pressure asymmetry.**
For a finite wing at angle of attack, inviscid theory would predict **zero** form drag (D'Alembert's paradox). All form drag is therefore a **viscous phenomenon** — it requires boundary-layer separation and wake formation.

**Step 5: Change the planform and observe:**
- Increasing **AR** $\Rightarrow$ reduces induced drag (the strongest effect).
- Taper ratio $\lambda = 1$ (rectangular) $\Rightarrow$ elliptical lift distribution requires $\lambda \approx 0.4$ for untwisted wings (Helmbold equation).
- Adding **sweep** $\Rightarrow$ reduces the effective chord Reynolds number $Re_c = V_\infty c \cos\Lambda / \nu$, thickens the boundary layer, may increase form drag at low speeds.
- An **elliptical planform** uniquely yields a uniform downwash and minimum induced drag for a given $AR$ and lift.

**Step 6: Combine into total drag (parabolic drag polar).**
$$
C_D = C_{D,0} + \frac{C_L^2}{\pi \cdot AR \cdot e}
$$
where $C_{D,0}$ is the zero-lift drag (form + skin friction) and $e$ is the **Oswald efficiency factor** ($0 < e \leq 1$, with $e=1$ for the ideal elliptical wing).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas verified from classical Anderson, Houghton & Carpenter, and KTU syllabus text. Memorize these for the ESE.**

| Symbol | Definition | Formula / Expression | Reference Quantity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $C_f$ | Local skin friction coefficient (turbulent) | $C_f = \dfrac{0.455}{(\log_{10} Re_x)^{2.58}} \quad \text{(Prandtl-Schlichting)}$ | Local $Re_x = \rho V x / \mu$ |
| $C_{D,f}$ | Skin friction drag coefficient | $C_{D,f} = \dfrac{C_f \cdot S_{wet}}{S_{ref}}$ | $S_{ref}$ (planform area) |
| $C_{D,form}$ | Form drag coefficient (projected area) | $C_{D,form} = \dfrac{1}{c} \displaystyle\int \Delta C_p \, dy$ | $S_{ref}$ |
| $AR$ | Aspect Ratio | $AR = b^2 / S = b / \bar{c}$ | — |
| $\lambda$ | Taper Ratio | $\lambda = c_t / c_r$ | — |
| $S_{wet}$ | Wetted area (rectangular wing) | $S_{wet} = 2 S (1 + 0.2\,t/c)$ | — |
| $S_{wet}$ | Wetted area (tapered wing) | $S_{wet} = 2 \int_0^{b/2} c(y) \, dy$ | — |
| $\bar{c}$ | Mean Aerodynamic Chord | $\bar{c} = \dfrac{2}{S} \displaystyle\int_0^{b/2} c^2(y) \, dy$ | — |
| $e$ | Oswald efficiency factor | $e = \dfrac{1}{1 + \delta}$ | $\delta$ = induced drag factor |
| $C_{D,i}$ | Induced drag coefficient | $C_{D,i} = \dfrac{C_L^2}{\pi \, AR \, e}$ | $S_{ref}$ |
| $C_D$ | Total drag coefficient | $C_D = C_{D,0} + \dfrac{C_L^2}{\pi \, AR \, e}$ | $S_{ref}$ |
| $\Lambda_{LE}$ | Leading edge sweep | $\tan \Lambda_{LE} = \dfrac{(c_r - c_t)/2}{b/2}$ | — |
| $S$ | Planform area | $S = b \cdot \bar{c} = \dfrac{b(c_r + c_t)}{2}$ | — |

### 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Commercial Aviation:** Modern Boeing 787 / Airbus A350 wings use a *moderately swept, highly tapered* planform (with winglets). This reduces $C_{D,i}$ by $30\%$–$40\%$ compared to a rectangular wing, saving fuel.
- **Fighter Aircraft (e.g., F-22):** Use a *trapezoidal cropped-delta* planform that balances low form drag at supersonic speeds with high lift at low speeds.
- **Sailplanes (Gliders):** Use *high aspect ratio* ($AR > 30$) wings with elliptical taper to minimize both skin friction and induced drag.
- **UAVs / Drones:** Use *rectangular low-AR* wings where maneuverability and structural simplicity outweigh drag penalties.

> [!TIP]
> **Why elliptical is the "Gold Standard":** Prandtl's lifting-line theory proves that for an *untwisted* finite wing, the elliptical planform is the only shape that produces a purely elliptic spanwise loading — and that this loading yields the **minimum possible induced drag** for the given lift and span.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Mean Aerodynamic Chord and Wetted Area of a Tapered Wing

Let a finite wing have root chord $c_r$, tip chord $c_t$, span $b$, and taper ratio $\lambda = c_t/c_r$.

**Step 1: Express the chord as a linear function of the spanwise coordinate $y$.**
With $y = 0$ at the root and $y = b/2$ at the tip:
$$
c(y) = c_r + \frac{c_t - c_r}{b/2}\,y = c_r\left[1 + 2(\lambda - 1)\,\frac{y}{b}\right]
$$

**Step 2: Compute the planform area $S$.**
$$
S = \int_{-b/2}^{b/2} c(y)\,dy = 2\int_0^{b/2} c_r\left[1 + 2(\lambda-1)\frac{y}{b}\right]dy
$$

$$
= 2c_r\left[y + (\lambda-1)\frac{y^2}{b}\right]_0^{b/2}
$$

$$
= 2c_r\left[\frac{b}{2} + (\lambda-1)\frac{b}{4}\right]
$$

$$
\boxed{S = \frac{b\,c_r}{2}(1 + \lambda)}
$$

This is the classic "average chord times span" formula for a trapezoidal wing.

**Step 3: Compute the Mean Aerodynamic Chord (MAC).**
By definition:
$$
\bar{c} = \frac{2}{S}\int_0^{b/2} c^2(y)\,dy
$$

Square the chord function:
$$
c^2(y) = c_r^2\left[1 + 2(\lambda-1)\frac{y}{b}\right]^2
$$

Expand the square:
$$
c^2(y) = c_r^2\left[1 + 4(\lambda-1)\frac{y}{b} + 4(\lambda-1)^2\frac{y^2}{b^2}\right]
$$

Integrate from $0$ to $b/2$:
$$
\int_0^{b/2} c^2(y)\,dy = c_r^2\left[y + 2(\lambda-1)\frac{y^2}{b} + \frac{4}{3}(\lambda-1)^2\frac{y^3}{b^2}\right]_0^{b/2}
$$

$$
= c_r^2\left[\frac{b}{2} + 2(\lambda-1)\frac{b}{4} + \frac{4}{3}(\lambda-1)^2\frac{b}{8}\right]
$$

$$
= \frac{b\,c_r^2}{2}\left[1 + (\lambda-1) + \frac{(\lambda-1)^2}{3}\right]
$$

$$
= \frac{b\,c_r^2}{2}\left[\frac{\lambda^2 + \lambda + 1}{3}\right]
$$

Substitute into the MAC definition:
$$
\bar{c} = \frac{2}{S}\cdot \frac{b\,c_r^2}{2}\cdot \frac{\lambda^2 + \lambda + 1}{3} = \frac{b\,c_r^2\,(\lambda^2 + \lambda + 1)}{3S}
$$

Using $S = b\,c_r(1+\lambda)/2$:
$$
\bar{c} = \frac{b\,c_r^2(\lambda^2 + \lambda + 1)}{3 \cdot \frac{b\,c_r(1+\lambda)}{2}} = \frac{2c_r(\lambda^2 + \lambda + 1)}{3(1+\lambda)}
$$

$$
\boxed{\bar{c} = \frac{2c_r}{3}\cdot\frac{\lambda^2 + \lambda + 1}{1 + \lambda}}
$$

**Verification:** For a rectangular wing ($\lambda = 1$): $\bar{c} = \dfrac{2c_r}{3}\cdot\dfrac{3}{2} = c_r$ ✓

**Step 4: Compute the wetted area (assuming thin airfoil of thickness ratio $t/c$).**
The wetted area of a thin wing is approximately $S_{wet} \approx 2S(1 + 0.2\,t/c)$ for a uniform section, but for a linearly tapered wing we integrate:

$$
S_{wet} = 2\int_0^{b/2} c(y)\left(1 + 0.2\,\frac{t}{c}\right)dy
$$

If thickness ratio $t/c$ is constant:
$$
S_{wet} = 2\left(1 + 0.2\,\frac{t}{c}\right)\int_0^{b/2}c(y)\,dy = 2S\left(1 + 0.2\,\frac{t}{c}\right)
$$

So the wetted area is the **same** as for a rectangular wing of equivalent area — taper ratio alone does not change $S_{wet}$ at constant $t/c$. This is a subtle KTU favorite!

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Induced Drag from a Prandtl Lifting-Line Analysis

The downwash at the wing $w(y)$ produces an induced angle of attack:
$$
\alpha_i(y) = \frac{w(y)}{V_\infty}
$$

The induced drag per unit span is the streamwise component of the local lift:
$$
dD_i = L(y)\,\sin\alpha_i \approx L(y)\,\alpha_i = \frac{L(y)\,w(y)}{V_\infty}
$$

Integrating over the span:
$$
D_i = \frac{1}{V_\infty}\int_{-b/2}^{b/2} L(y)\,w(y)\,dy
$$

For an elliptic loading $L(y) = L_0\sqrt{1-(2y/b)^2}$, the downwash is **uniform** $w(y) = w_0$, and $L_0 = 2L/\pi$. The integral evaluates to:
$$
D_i = \frac{L\,w_0}{V_\infty} = \frac{2L^2}{\pi \rho V_\infty^2 b^2}
$$

Since $C_L = L/(\frac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 S)$ and $AR = b^2/S$:
$$
C_{D,i} = \frac{D_i}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 S} = \frac{1}{\pi AR}C_L^2
$$

Introducing the Oswald factor $e \leq 1$ for non-elliptical wings:
$$
\boxed{C_{D,i} = \frac{C_L^2}{\pi\,AR\,e}}
$$

**Engineering Consequence:** Doubling $AR$ halves $C_{D,i}$. A wing of $AR = 8$ at $C_L = 0.5$ has $C_{D,i} = 0.0125$, while $AR = 4$ has $C_{D,i} = 0.025$ — a 100% increase.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Oswald Efficiency Factor $e$ for a Tapered Wing

For a linearly tapered, untwisted wing, the loading is *not* perfectly elliptic. Helmbold's empirical result (used in KTU 2024 syllabus):

$$
e = \frac{1}{1 + \delta}
$$
where
$$
\delta = \frac{0.0345}{\cos\Lambda_{LE}}\cdot\frac{1 + \lambda - 2\lambda^2 + 2\lambda^2 \ln\lambda}{(1+\lambda)^2}
$$

**Example:** For $\lambda = 0.4$ and $\Lambda_{LE} = 30°$:

First compute the numerator factor:
$$
1 + 0.4 - 2(0.16) + 2(0.16)\ln(0.4) = 1.4 - 0.32 + 0.32(-0.916)
$$
$$
= 1.08 - 0.293 = 0.787
$$

Denominator: $(1.4)^2 = 1.96$

$$
\frac{0.787}{1.96} = 0.402
$$

Then:
$$
\delta = \frac{0.0345}{\cos 30°}\cdot 0.402 = \frac{0.0345}{0.866}\cdot 0.402 = 0.0398 \cdot 0.402 = 0.016
$$

$$
\boxed{e = \frac{1}{1.016} = 0.984}
$$

This is very close to the ideal $e = 1$, confirming that $\lambda \approx 0.4$ is "near-elliptical" — a key KTU design insight.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Drag Decomposition for a Finite Wing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Drag D on Finite Wing] --> B[D Parasitic Zero Lift]
    A --> C[D Induced Lift Dependent]
    B --> B1[Skin Friction Drag Df]
    B --> B2[Form Drag Dform]
    C --> C1[Driven by Lift L]
    C --> C2[Driven by Planform AR]
    B1 --> D1[Wetted Area Swet]
    B1 --> D2[Reynolds Number Re]
    B1 --> D3[Laminar vs Turbulent BL]
    B2 --> E1[Boundary Layer Separation]
    B2 --> E2[Leading Edge Sweep LambdaLE]
    B2 --> E3[Thickness Ratio t over c]
    C1 --> F1[Spanwise Loading L of y]
    C1 --> F2[Downwash Distribution w of y]
    C2 --> G1[Aspect Ratio AR]
    C2 --> G2[Taper Ratio Lambda]
    C2 --> G3[Oswald Factor e]
    D2 --> H1[Higher Re lowers Cf per unit area]
    E2 --> H2[Higher sweep thickens BL]
    G1 --> H3[Higher AR lowers CDi]
    G3 --> H4[Lambda approx 0.4 maximizes e]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Planform Shape Comparison Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Rectangular Wing] --> P1A[Lambda = 1]
    P1A --> P1B[Non elliptic loading]
    P1B --> P1C[e approx 0.85 to 0.95]
    P2[Tapered Wing] --> P2A[Lambda approx 0.4]
    P2A --> P2B[Near elliptic loading]
    P2B --> P2C[e approx 0.95 to 0.99]
    P3[Elliptical Wing] --> P3A[Continuous taper]
    P3A --> P3B[Elliptic loading ideal]
    P3B --> P3C[e = 1.0 minimum induced drag]
    P4[Swept Wing] --> P4A[LambdaLE approx 25 to 35 deg]
    P4A --> P4B[Effective Re reduced]
    P4B --> P4C[Higher Cf but lower M critical]
    P5[Delta Wing] --> P5A[High LambdaLE approx 60 deg]
    P5A --> P5B[Strong leading edge vortex]
    P5B --> P5C[Form drag rises low speed]
    P1C --> Z1[Result Higher CDi at same AR]
    P2C --> Z1
    P3C --> Z1
    P4C --> Z1
    P5C --> Z1
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — How Planform Geometry Maps to Drag

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Finite Wing Planform] --> B[Geometric Inputs]
    B --> B1[b Span]
    B --> B2[S Planform Area]
    B --> B3[cr Root Chord]
    B --> B4[ct Tip Chord]
    B --> B5[LambdaLE Sweep]
    A --> C[Derived Parameters]
    C --> C1[AR = b squared / S]
    C --> C2[Lambda = ct / cr]
    C --> C3[Mac Bar c]
    C --> C4[e Oswald factor]
    A --> D[Drag Outputs]
    D --> D1[CDf Skin friction]
    D --> D2[CDform Form drag]
    D --> D3[CDi Induced drag]
    D --> D4[CD Total drag]
    C1 --> D3
    C2 --> D4
    C4 --> D3
    B1 --> D1
    B3 --> D1
    B4 --> D1
    B5 --> D2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Trace how geometric inputs (span, root/tip chord, sweep) feed derived parameters (AR, taper ratio, MAC, $e$) which in turn determine the three drag components. Any change in the planform propagates through this entire chain. This is the most commonly tested concept in KTU ESE Module 3.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024 expected]`

**Define skin friction drag and form drag. With the help of neat sketches, explain how each arises on a finite wing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Skin Friction Drag (1½ marks):** It is the drag produced by the **tangential viscous shear stresses** in the boundary layer. It exists even on a perfectly streamlined body and is given by:
$$
D_f = \int_{S_{wet}} \tau_w \, dS
$$
- **Form Drag (1½ marks):** It is the drag produced by the **asymmetric normal pressure distribution** caused by boundary-layer separation and the resulting wake. It is zero in inviscid flow (D'Alembert's paradox).

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** Mention **D'Alembert's paradox** to get full marks — examiners love this phrase.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model 2024]`

**What is the significance of the aspect ratio in determining the total drag of a finite wing? How does taper ratio modify this?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- The **aspect ratio** $AR = b^2/S$ appears in the induced-drag formula:
$$
C_{D,i} = \frac{C_L^2}{\pi\,AR\,e}
$$
Increasing $AR$ reduces $C_{D,i}$ proportionally. (1½ marks)
- The **taper ratio** $\lambda = c_t/c_r$ controls the spanwise lift distribution. For untwisted wings, $\lambda \approx 0.4$ produces a near-elliptical loading and $e \to 1$, giving **minimum induced drag**. (1½ marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, 2024 retest]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **mean aerodynamic chord (MAC)** of a linearly tapered wing with root chord $c_r$, tip chord $c_t$ and span $b$. Show that for $\lambda = 1$ the result reduces to the root chord. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Spanwise chord distribution (1 mark):
$$
c(y) = c_r\left[1 + 2(\lambda-1)\frac{y}{b}\right], \quad 0 \leq y \leq b/2
$$

**Step 2:** Definition of MAC (1 mark):
$$
\bar{c} = \frac{2}{S}\int_0^{b/2} c^2(y)\,dy
$$

**Step 3:** Planform area (1 mark):
$$
S = \int_{-b/2}^{b/2} c(y)dy = \frac{bc_r}{2}(1+\lambda)
$$

**Step 4:** Square and integrate (2 marks):
$$
\int_0^{b/2}c^2(y)dy = \frac{bc_r^2}{2}\cdot\frac{\lambda^2+\lambda+1}{3}
$$

**Step 5:** Combine (1 mark):
$$
\boxed{\bar{c} = \frac{2c_r}{3}\cdot\frac{\lambda^2+\lambda+1}{1+\lambda}}
$$

**Step 6:** Check rectangular case (1 mark):
For $\lambda = 1$:
$$
\bar{c} = \frac{2c_r}{3}\cdot\frac{3}{2} = c_r \quad \checkmark
$$

---

**(b)** A finite wing has span $b = 12$ m, root chord $c_r = 2$ m, taper ratio $\lambda = 0.5$, and thickness ratio $t/c = 0.12$. The aircraft flies at $V_\infty = 60$ m/s at sea level ($\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.789 \times 10^{-5}$ kg/m·s). Calculate:
   (i) the **planform area** $S$,
   (ii) the **wetted area** $S_{wet}$,
   (iii) the **skin friction drag coefficient** $C_{D,f}$,
   (iv) the **skin friction drag** $D_f$ in Newtons.

Take $C_f \approx 0.003$ (turbulent flat-plate value at typical $Re$).

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**(i) Planform area (2 marks):**
$$
S = \frac{bc_r}{2}(1+\lambda) = \frac{12 \times 2}{2}(1+0.5) = 12 \times 1.5 = 18 \; m^2
$$

**(ii) Wetted area (2 marks):**
$$
S_{wet} = 2S\left(1 + 0.2\,\frac{t}{c}\right) = 2(18)(1 + 0.2 \times 0.12) = 36(1.024) = 36.86 \; m^2
$$

**(iii) Skin friction drag coefficient (1.5 marks):**
$$
C_{D,f} = \frac{C_f \cdot S_{wet}}{S_{ref}} = \frac{0.003 \times 36.86}{18} = 0.00614
$$

**(iv) Skin friction drag (1.5 marks):**
$$
q_\infty = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 3600 = 2205 \; N/m^2
$$
$$
D_f = C_{D,f} \cdot q_\infty \cdot S = 0.00614 \times 2205 \times 18 = 243.7 \; N
$$

---

#### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2022, retest 2024]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **effect of wing planform shape** on (i) skin friction drag and (ii) form drag. Compare rectangular, tapered, elliptical, and swept wings with neat supporting equations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer Outline:**

**(i) Effect on Skin Friction Drag (3.5 marks):**
- $C_{D,f} = C_f S_{wet}/S$. For the same planform area $S$ and thickness ratio $t/c$, **wetted area is approximately the same** for any planform (since $S_{wet} \approx 2S(1+0.2t/c)$).
- However, the **local Reynolds number** varies:
$$
Re_{c}(y) = \frac{\rho V_\infty c(y)}{\mu}
$$
A rectangular wing has *uniform* $Re_c$ across the span, while a tapered wing has *lower* $Re_c$ near the tip. Lower $Re_c \Rightarrow$ higher $C_f$ locally (since $C_f \propto Re^{-1/5}$ for turbulent BL), so the *integrated* $C_{D,f}$ is slightly higher for tapered wings at constant $S$.
- Swept wings reduce the **effective chord** $c_{eff} = c\cos\Lambda_{LE}$, lowering $Re_{c,eff}$ and **increasing** $C_{D,f}$.

**(ii) Effect on Form Drag (3.5 marks):**
- Sweep moves the leading edge stagnation point and can **delay or promote** separation depending on the airfoil and Re.
- An **elliptical** wing has the smoothest spanwise loading and the cleanest 3-D pressure recovery — minimum form drag for a clean wing.
- A **rectangular** wing has tip separation (3-D effect) and slightly higher form drag.
- A **delta** wing uses leading-edge vortices to maintain attached flow at high $\alpha$ — low form drag at high angle of attack, but high form drag at low $\alpha$.

---

**(b)** A rectangular wing and a tapered wing have the **same planform area** $S = 20 \; m^2$, **same span** $b = 10$ m, and same airfoil section with $C_{L,\max} = 1.4$. The taper ratio is $\lambda = 0.5$. Determine:
   (i) the **chord lengths** $c_r, c_t$ for the tapered wing,
   (ii) the **aspect ratios** of both wings,
   (iii) the **induced drag coefficient** at $C_L = 0.6$ for both, assuming $e = 0.9$ (rectangular) and $e = 0.98$ (tapered).
   (iv) Comment on the **percentage reduction in induced drag** due to tapering. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**(i) Chord lengths (2 marks):**
- Rectangular wing: $c = S/b = 20/10 = 2$ m. So $c_r = c_t = 2$ m.
- Tapered wing: $S = \frac{bc_r}{2}(1+\lambda) \Rightarrow 20 = \frac{10 \cdot c_r}{2}(1.5) = 7.5\,c_r$
$$
c_r = \frac{20}{7.5} = 2.667 \; m, \quad c_t = \lambda c_r = 0.5 \times 2.667 = 1.333 \; m
$$

**(ii) Aspect ratios (1.5 marks):**
Both have $AR = b^2/S = 100/20 = 5$ (same since same $b$ and $S$).

**(iii) Induced drag coefficients (2.5 marks):**
- Rectangular:
$$
C_{D,i}^{rect} = \frac{C_L^2}{\pi\,AR\,e} = \frac{0.6^2}{\pi \times 5 \times 0.9} = \frac{0.36}{14.137} = 0.0255
$$
- Tapered:
$$
C_{D,i}^{taper} = \frac{0.6^2}{\pi \times 5 \times 0.98} = \frac{0.36}{15.394} = 0.0234
$$

**(iv) Percentage reduction (1 mark):**
$$
\Delta C_{D,i} = 0.0255 - 0.0234 = 0.0021
$$
$$
\text{Reduction} = \frac{0.0021}{0.0255} \times 100\% = 8.2\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't confuse $S$ and $S_{wet}$.** $S$ is the *planform* area; $S_{wet}$ is the *wetted* area (roughly $2S$ for thin wings). Mixing them up will cost you 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Always state assumptions** — laminar vs. turbulent BL, $t/c$ constant, $C_f$ value, $e$ value. Examiners award method marks when assumptions are explicit.
> 3. **Aspect ratio formula** — use $AR = b^2/S$, **not** $b/c$. The ratio $b/c$ only equals $AR$ for rectangular wings. This is the #1 error in KTU answer sheets.
> 4. **Induced drag vs. form drag** — induced drag exists *only* with lift. Form drag is part of $C_{D,0}$. Don't conflate them.
> 5. **Units in MAC derivations** — keep $b, c_r, c_t$ in metres throughout. The formula $\bar{c} = \frac{2c_r(\lambda^2+\lambda+1)}{3(1+\lambda)}$ is dimensionless-correct only if all chords share the same unit.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Skin Friction & Form Drag in Planform Changes):**

- **Drag Decomposition:** $C_D = C_{D,0} + C_{D,i}$, where $C_{D,0} = C_{D,f} + C_{D,form}$.
- **Skin Friction Drag:** Viscous shear, exists even on a streamlined body, $C_{D,f} = C_f S_{wet}/S_{ref}$.
- **Form Drag:** Pressure asymmetry from separation, **zero** in inviscid theory, dominant in bluff or separated flows.
- **Aspect Ratio:** $AR = b^2/S$ — primary driver of induced drag.
- **Taper Ratio:** $\lambda = c_t/c_r$ — $\lambda \approx 0.4$ gives near-elliptical loading (untwisted).
- **Elliptical Wing:** Theoretically minimum induced drag; $e = 1$.
- **Swept Wing:** Reduces $M_{crit}$ and effective $Re_c$ but **increases** $C_{D,f}$.
- **Delta Wing:** Uses LE vortex for high-$\alpha$ lift; high form drag at low $\alpha$.
- **Parabolic Drag Polar:** $C_D = C_{D,0} + C_L^2/(\pi AR e)$ — memorize and apply.
- **MAC Formula:** $\bar{c} = \frac{2c_r}{3}\cdot\frac{\lambda^2+\lambda+1}{1+\lambda}$ — derived from first principles.
- **Wetted Area:** $S_{wet} \approx 2S(1 + 0.2\,t/c)$ — independent of taper ratio at constant $t/c$.
- **Prandtl-Schlichting:** $C_f = 0.455/(\log_{10} Re)^{2.58}$ for turbulent BL.
- **Helmbold Equation:** Use to compute $e$ for tapered, swept wings.
- **Practical Takeaway:** Tapering alone reduces $C_{D,i}$ by $\sim 8\%$ at $AR = 5$; combined with high AR and sweep, the total drag reduction can exceed 30% versus a rectangular wing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Propeller as a Thrust-Producing Device

A **propeller** is a rotating, blade-mounted aero-mechanical device that converts the **rotational mechanical power** supplied by an engine (shaft power) into a **forward thrust force** by accelerating a large mass of air rearward (Newton's Third Law). It is the most widely used propulsion device for subsonic, fixed-wing aircraft and is the foundational concept behind all modern turboprop and rotorcraft (helicopter) design.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A propeller is treated as a thrust-producing device using two complementary analytical frameworks:
> 1. **Momentum Theory (Froude Theory / Actuator Disk Theory)** – Treats the propeller as an idealized *actuator disk* and applies control-volume conservation of mass, momentum, and energy.
> 2. **Blade Element Theory (BET)** – Treats each radial strip of the blade as an independent 2-D *airfoil section*, summing forces over all strips to obtain total propeller thrust and torque.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are standing on a skateboard holding a garden hose:

- **Momentum Theory Viewpoint:** Don't worry about *how* the water leaves the hose. Just track the *change in momentum* of the water slug between the nozzle and where it eventually settles. From the impulse imparted, you can deduce the thrust pushing you backwards. The propeller behaves identically: it accelerates air from velocity $V_\infty$ to a wake velocity $V_w$ through an invisible "actuator disk," and the rate of momentum change equals thrust.

- **Blade Element Theory Viewpoint:** Now zoom into a single thin ring (annular strip) of one propeller blade. That strip behaves exactly like a tiny **wing in combined translation and rotation**. It has an angle of attack, generates lift and drag, and the resolved components produce thrust and torque. Sum all rings → total propeller performance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Both Theories?**
> - Momentum theory gives **global performance** (thrust, power, efficiency) but **cannot predict blade geometry**.
> - Blade element theory gives **local aerodynamic loading** but requires empirical 2-D airfoil data.
> - Combined, they form the foundation of **Blade Element Momentum Theory (BEMT)**, the industry standard for propeller and rotor design.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Air density:** $\rho = 1.225 \,\text{kg/m}^3$ (ISA Sea Level)
- **Propeller diameter:** $D$ (m)
- **Propeller radius:** $R = D/2$ (m)
- **Rotational speed:** $n$ (revolutions per second) or $N$ (RPM)
- **Angular velocity:** $\Omega = 2\pi n$ (rad/s)
- **Freestream velocity:** $V_\infty$ (m/s)
- **Inflow velocity at disk:** $V_i$ (m/s)
- **Far-wake velocity:** $V_w$ (m/s)
- **Induced velocity factor:** $a$ (dimensionless, often denoted $\alpha$)
- **Advance ratio:** $J$ or $\lambda = V_\infty / (nD)$ (dimensionless)
- **Thrust coefficient:** $C_T = T/(\rho n^2 D^4)$
- **Power coefficient:** $C_P = P/(\rho n^3 D^5)$
- **Propulsive efficiency:** $\eta_p$ (dimensionless)

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Froude Momentum Theory — Operating Principle

The propeller is replaced by an **actuator disk** — a porous, frictionless surface across which a pressure jump $\Delta p$ exists but through which the air velocity is continuous. The control volume extends from far upstream ($\infty$) to far downstream (wake).

### Assumptions (Critical for KTU Board Exams):

1. The propeller is a **thin, frictionless actuator disk** of area $A = \pi R^2 = \pi D^2/4$.
2. **Incompressible, steady, inviscid flow** (no drag, no heat transfer).
3. The flow is **axisymmetric and uniform** across each cross-section.
4. The far-wake velocity $V_w$ is **uniform** across the wake cross-section (a discontinuous jump from $V_\infty$).
5. The **velocity at the disk** is the average of upstream and wake velocities: $V_i = (V_\infty + V_w)/2$.
6. **No external work** other than the shaft power transferred across the disk.

### Step-by-Step Logic:

- **Mass conservation:** $\dot{m} = \rho A V_\infty = \rho A V_i = \rho A_{w} V_w$. Since the stream-tube area must contract in the wake to conserve mass with a higher velocity, $A_w < A$.
- **Axial momentum equation:** Thrust equals the rate of change of axial momentum across the stream-tube.
- **Energy equation (Euler's Turbomachinery Equation):** Shaft power $P = T \cdot V_i$ because the force $T$ acts through the disk at the intermediate velocity $V_i$.

### Defining the Induced Velocity Factor:

$$a = \frac{V_i - V_\infty}{V_\infty} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad V_i = V_\infty(1 + a)$$

From continuity and momentum: $V_w = V_\infty(1 + 2a)$.

## 2.2 Blade Element Theory (BET) — Operating Principle

The blade is sliced at radius $r$ into a series of thin annular strips (blade elements), each of chord $c(r)$, width $dr$, and local geometric pitch $\beta(r)$.

### Key Local Quantities at Radius $r$:

- **Blade section velocity (perpendicular to plane of rotation):** $\Omega r = 2\pi n r$.
- **Resultant local airspeed:** $U = \sqrt{V_\infty^2 + (\Omega r)^2}$.
- **Inflow (advance) angle:** $\phi = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{V_\infty}{\Omega r}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{\lambda D}{2\pi r}\right)$.
- **Local angle of attack:** $\alpha = \beta - \phi$.

### Force Resolution on a Blade Element:

The airfoil-section generates $dL$ (lift) and $dD$ (drag) perpendicular and parallel to $U$ respectively. Resolving into propeller axes:

- **Thrust contribution:** $dT = dL \cos\phi - dD \sin\phi$.
- **Torque contribution:** $dQ = r(dL \sin\phi + dD \cos\phi)$.
- **Power contribution:** $dP = \Omega \, dQ$.

For an **ideal (thin-airfoil, inviscid) blade**, $C_D \approx 0$ and $dT = dL\cos\phi$, $dQ = r\,dL\sin\phi$.

### Integration:

Total thrust $T$, torque $Q$, and power $P$ are obtained by integrating from hub radius $r_h$ to tip radius $R$, and multiplying by the number of blades $B$:

$$T = B \int_{r_h}^{R} dT, \qquad Q = B \int_{r_h}^{R} dQ, \qquad P = \Omega Q = B \int_{r_h}^{R} \Omega \, dQ$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **most essential cheat sheet** for solving KTU Module 4 propeller problems. Memorize every row.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Disk area | $A = \pi R^2 = \pi D^2 / 4$ | $\text{m}^2$ |
| 2 | Mass flow rate | $\dot{m} = \rho A V_i$ | kg/s |
| 3 | Inflow velocity | $V_i = V_\infty(1 + a)$ | m/s |
| 4 | Far-wake velocity | $V_w = V_\infty(1 + 2a)$ | m/s |
| 5 | Thrust (Momentum) | $T = \dot{m}(V_w - V_\infty) = 2\rho A V_\infty^2 \, a(1+a)$ | N |
| 6 | Static thrust ($V_\infty = 0$) | $T_0 = 2\rho A V_i^2$ | N (a = 1 in static case) |
| 7 | Shaft power | $P = T \cdot V_i = 2\rho A V_\infty^3 \, a(1+a)^2$ | W |
| 8 | Propulsive efficiency | $\eta_p = \dfrac{T V_\infty}{T V_i} = \dfrac{V_\infty}{V_i} = \dfrac{1}{1+a}$ | Dimensionless |
| 9 | Froude ideal efficiency | $\eta_F = \dfrac{2}{1 + \sqrt{1 + C_T/2}}$ — using $C_T = T/(\rho n^2 D^4)$ | Dim-less |
| 10 | Advance ratio | $J = \dfrac{V_\infty}{nD}$ | Dim-less |
| 11 | Local inflow angle | $\tan\phi = \dfrac{V_\infty}{\Omega r} = \dfrac{1}{\pi x} \cdot J$ where $x = r/R$ | radians |
| 12 | Local resultant speed | $U = \sqrt{V_\infty^2 + (\Omega r)^2}$ | m/s |
| 13 | Element lift force | $dL = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 c \, C_L \, dr$ | N |
| 14 | Element drag force | $dD = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 c \, C_D \, dr$ | N |
| 15 | Element thrust | $dT = dL\cos\phi - dD\sin\phi$ | N |
| 16 | Element torque | $dQ = r(dL\sin\phi + dD\cos\phi)$ | N·m |
| 17 | Thrust coefficient | $C_T = \dfrac{T}{\rho n^2 D^4}$ | Dim-less |
| 18 | Power coefficient | $C_P = \dfrac{P}{\rho n^3 D^5}$ | Dim-less |
| 19 | Ideal blade twist (no-drag) | $\tan\beta_i = \dfrac{J}{\pi x} \cdot \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{C_T / (x^2 \cdot \text{const.})}}$ — use $C_{L_i}$ relation | degrees |
| 20 | Figure of merit (helicopter analog) | $\eta_F = \dfrac{C_T^{3/2}}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{C_P}$ | Dim-less |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aircraft Preliminary Design:** Designers use $C_T$ and $C_P$ vs. $J$ charts (computed using these theories) to select the optimal propeller diameter and RPM for a given airframe and engine.
- **Wind-Turbine Aerodynamics:** The BEM equations are *identical* in form to propeller equations — only the sign conventions and reference frames change. Modern wind-turbine design codes (e.g., AeroDyn, BEMRosetta) are direct descendants of this theory.
- **Helicopter Rotor Design:** Momentum theory gives hover thrust ($T = 2\rho A V_i^2$) and induced power; blade element theory gives rotor blade twist and chord distributions. Together they form the **Blade Element Momentum Theory (BEMT)** used universally in rotorcraft industry.
- **Marine Propellers and Ducted Fans:** The same theory extends to ship propellers with the addition of a duct (Shrank, Kort nozzles) and to high-bypass turbofan engines where the fan is essentially a ducted propeller.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Froude Momentum Theory — Complete Derivation

We establish a cylindrical control volume bounded upstream by station $\infty$ (velocity $V_\infty$, area $A_\infty$) and downstream by the far wake (velocity $V_w$, area $A_w$). The propeller is modelled as the actuator disk at station $i$ (velocity $V_i$, area $A = \pi R^2$).

### Step 1 — Mass Conservation

Because the flow is steady and incompressible, the mass flow rate is constant across all three stations:

$$\dot{m} = \rho A_\infty V_\infty = \rho A V_i = \rho A_w V_w$$

### Step 2 — Define the Induced Velocity Factor

Define $a$ such that the velocity at the disk equals:

$$V_i = V_\infty(1 + a)$$

From mass conservation: $A_\infty V_\infty = A V_i \Rightarrow A = A_\infty / (1+a)$, so the stream-tube contracts upstream of the disk.

### Step 3 — Far-Wake Velocity (Momentum + Kinetic Energy)

For an inviscid actuator disk, the **Bernoulli equation** applied on a streamline between $\infty$ and the far wake gives:

$$p_\infty + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 = p_w + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_w^2$$

The pressure jump across the disk $\Delta p = p_{i^+} - p_{i^-}$ does equal work on the air. Applying Bernoulli on either side of the disk:

$$p_\infty + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 = p_{i^-} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_i^2$$

$$p_{i^+} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_i^2 = p_w + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_w^2$$

Adding the two and using $p_w = p_\infty$:

$$\Delta p = p_{i^+} - p_{i^-} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho(V_w^2 - V_\infty^2)$$

Now apply the momentum theorem. The thrust equals the net force on the air = pressure jump $\times$ disk area:

$$T = \Delta p \cdot A = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A (V_w^2 - V_\infty^2)$$

But $T$ also equals the rate of change of axial momentum:

$$T = \dot{m}(V_w - V_\infty) = \rho A V_i (V_w - V_\infty)$$

Equating the two expressions for $T$:

$$\tfrac{1}{2}\rho A(V_w^2 - V_\infty^2) = \rho A V_i (V_w - V_\infty)$$

Factor $(V_w - V_\infty)$ from the left side:

$$\tfrac{1}{2}\rho A(V_w - V_\infty)(V_w + V_\infty) = \rho A V_i (V_w - V_\infty)$$

Cancel $\rho A (V_w - V_\infty)$ (non-zero during thrust production):

$$\tfrac{1}{2}(V_w + V_\infty) = V_i$$

This yields the **classic Froude relation**:

$$\boxed{V_i = \frac{V_\infty + V_w}{2}}$$

This justifies assumption (5) above: the velocity at the disk is the arithmetic mean of upstream and wake velocities.

### Step 4 — Express Thrust in Terms of $a$

From $V_i = V_\infty(1+a)$ and $V_i = (V_\infty + V_w)/2$:

$$V_w = 2V_i - V_\infty = 2V_\infty(1+a) - V_\infty = V_\infty(1 + 2a)$$

Substitute into $T = \dot{m}(V_w - V_\infty)$:

$$T = \rho A V_i \cdot (V_w - V_\infty) = \rho A \cdot V_\infty(1+a) \cdot V_\infty(2a)$$

$$\boxed{T = 2\rho A V_\infty^2 \, a(1+a)}$$

### Step 5 — Shaft Power

Power = Force × Velocity-at-which-force-acts = $T \cdot V_i$:

$$P = T \cdot V_i = 2\rho A V_\infty^2 \, a(1+a) \cdot V_\infty(1+a)$$

$$\boxed{P = 2\rho A V_\infty^3 \, a(1+a)^2}$$

### Step 6 — Useful (Propulsive) Power and Efficiency

Useful power = Thrust × Freestream velocity:

$$P_\text{useful} = T \cdot V_\infty = 2\rho A V_\infty^3 \, a(1+a)$$

$$\boxed{\eta_p = \frac{P_\text{useful}}{P_\text{shaft}} = \frac{T V_\infty}{T V_i} = \frac{V_\infty}{V_i} = \frac{1}{1+a}}$$

### Step 7 — Static Case ($V_\infty = 0$)

At hover / static thrust, $V_\infty \to 0$. From $T = 2\rho A V_\infty^2 a(1+a)$, we get $T = 0/0$. Take the limit by using $T = \Delta p \cdot A$ with $\Delta p = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho(V_w^2 - V_\infty^2)$ and noting $V_w = 2V_i$ when $V_\infty = 0$:

$$\Delta p = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_w^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho (2V_i)^2 = 2\rho V_i^2$$

$$\boxed{T_0 = 2\rho A V_i^2}$$

Static efficiency $\eta_p = V_\infty/V_i = 0$, indicating that **all power is wasted in accelerating the wake** — a critical limitation of propellers (and helicopter rotors) in hover.

### Step 8 — Solving for $a$ in Terms of Thrust (Crucial for BEMT Coupling)

From $T = 2\rho A V_\infty^2 \, a(1+a)$, rearranging as a quadratic in $a$:

$$2\rho A V_\infty^2 \, a^2 + 2\rho A V_\infty^2 \, a - T = 0$$

Using the quadratic formula and selecting the **physical root** ($a \geq 0$):

$$\boxed{a = \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\sqrt{1 + \frac{2T}{\rho A V_\infty^2}} - 1\right)}$$

This is the **exact** induced velocity factor used in BEMT iterations.

---

## 3.2 Blade Element Theory — Complete Derivation

### Step 1 — Kinematics of a Blade Element at Radius $r$

Each blade element is a 2-D airfoil of chord $c(r)$ mounted at geometric pitch angle $\beta(r)$ relative to the propeller plane. The blade rotates at $\Omega = 2\pi n$, so the **tangential velocity of the section** is:

$$V_\theta = \Omega r$$

The freestream $V_\infty$ is axial. The **resultant relative velocity** seen by the section is:

$$U = \sqrt{V_\infty^2 + (\Omega r)^2}$$

The **inflow angle** $\phi$ between $U$ and the propeller plane of rotation:

$$\tan\phi = \frac{V_\infty}{\Omega r}$$

Using the **non-dimensional radial coordinate** $x = r/R$ and advance ratio $J = V_\infty/(nD)$:

$$\tan\phi = \frac{V_\infty}{2\pi n R x} = \frac{J}{2\pi x}$$

The **local angle of attack** is:

$$\alpha = \beta - \phi$$

### Step 2 — Forces on the Element

The 2-D lift and drag on an element of width $dr$ are:

$$dL = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 \, c(r) \, C_L(\alpha) \, dr$$

$$dD = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 \, c(r) \, C_D(\alpha) \, dr$$

where $C_L$ and $C_D$ are obtained from the airfoil polar at the local Reynolds number $\text{Re} = Uc/\nu$.

### Step 3 — Resolution into Thrust and Torque

The forces act perpendicular and parallel to $U$, not along the propeller axes. Resolve:

$$dT_\text{elem} = dL\cos\phi - dD\sin\phi$$

$$dQ_\text{elem} = r(dL\sin\phi + dD\cos\phi)$$

For an **ideal (inviscid) blade** with $C_D = 0$:

$$dT_\text{elem} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 \, c \, C_L \cos\phi \, dr$$

$$dQ_\text{elem} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 \, c \, C_L \sin\phi \, r \, dr$$

### Step 4 — Integration Over the Blade

The total thrust and torque are obtained by integrating along the blade radius from hub $r_h$ to tip $R$, and multiplying by the number of blades $B$:

$$T = \frac{B}{2}\int_{r_h}^{R} \rho U^2 \, c(r) \, [C_L\cos\phi - C_D\sin\phi] \, dr$$

$$Q = \frac{B}{2}\int_{r_h}^{R} \rho U^2 \, c(r) \, r\,[C_L\sin\phi + C_D\cos\phi] \, dr$$

### Step 5 — Ideal (Minimum-Energy) Blade Twist

For minimum induced loss at a single design advance ratio $J$, set $C_D = 0$ and choose $C_L = \text{const} = C_{L,\text{design}}$. From the radial equilibrium of forces, every element must contribute thrust proportional to its annulus area. The result, derived by equating $dT_\text{elem}$ with the local ideal thrust distribution $dT^* = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2(1+a)^2 \cdot 4\pi r\,dr \cdot ...$, gives the **ideal twist distribution**:

$$\boxed{\tan\beta_i(r) = \tan\phi + \frac{4\pi r \, V_i}{B \, c(r) \, U \, C_{L,\text{design}}}}$$

Substituting $V_i = V_\infty(1+a)$:

$$\boxed{\beta_i(r) = \arctan\!\left[\frac{V_\infty}{\Omega r} + \frac{4\pi r\,V_\infty(1+a)}{B\,c(r)\,U\,C_{L,\text{design}}}\right]}$$

This is the **optimum helical twist** that maximizes propeller efficiency.

### Step 6 — Expressing in Non-Dimensional Form ($C_T$ and $C_P$)

Using $C_T = T/(\rho n^2 D^4)$ and $C_P = P/(\rho n^3 D^5)$, the integrated BET expressions become:

$$C_T = \frac{\pi J^2}{2}\int_{x_h}^{1} \frac{c(x)}{D}\,C_L\!\left(\frac{J^2 + \pi^2 x^2}{J^2 \pi^2 x^2}\right)^{1/2}\!x^2\left[\theta - \frac{C_D}{C_L}\right]dx$$

where $\theta = (J^2 + \pi^2 x^2)^{1/2} \cdot \pi x / J$, and similarly for $C_P$. (Detailed expansion is suppressed here for brevity but is available in standard propeller texts.)

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Momentum vs. Blade Element Theory

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is **exam-favorite**. KTU frequently asks to "compare momentum and blade element theories" in 7-mark sub-parts.

| Property | Momentum Theory (Froude) | Blade Element Theory (BET) |
|----------|--------------------------|----------------------------|
| Modeling basis | Actuator disk (control volume) | 2-D airfoil section per radial strip |
| Inputs required | Thrust $T$, $V_\infty$, $\rho$, $A$ | Blade geometry $c(r),\beta(r)$, $C_L,C_D$ polars |
| Outputs | $T$, $P$, $\eta_p$ | $T$, $Q$, $P$, blade loading distribution |
| Predicts blade shape? | **No** | **Yes** (gives twist, chord) |
| Includes rotation? | **No** (only axial momentum) | **Yes** (rotation explicit in $\Omega r$) |
| Accounts for 3-D effects? | No | No (corrections via tip-loss factor $B^{1.5}/...$) |
| Computational cost | Very low (algebraic) | High (numerical integration) |
| Empirical data needed | None | Airfoil $C_L$ vs $\alpha$ and $C_D$ vs $\alpha$ |
| Typical accuracy for $C_T$ | Within 5–10% | Within 2–5% when combined with BEMT |
