# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Propeller Analysis Hierarchy

The following Mermaid block illustrates how momentum theory and blade element theory are combined in the standard **Blade Element Momentum Theory (BEMT)** workflow used in KTU syllabus and industry.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT DATA"] --> A1["Freestream Velocity V_inf"]
    A --> A2["Rotational Speed n RPM"]
    A --> A3["Blade Geometry c of r and beta of r"]
    A --> A4["Airfoil Polars C_L alpha and C_D alpha"]
    A --> A5["Diameter D and Hub Radius r_h"]
    A --> A6["Number of Blades B"]
    A --> A7["Air Density rho"]

    B["DISCRETISE BLADE INTO N RADIAL STATIONS"] --> B1["Station i has radius r_i chord c_i pitch beta_i"]

    B1 --> C["COMPUTE LOCAL KINEMATICS"]
    C --> C1["Omega r_i = 2 pi n r_i"]
    C --> C2["U i = sqrt V_inf squared plus Omega r_i squared"]
    C --> C3["phi i = arctan V_inf over Omega r_i"]
    C --> C4["alpha i = beta i minus phi i"]

    C4 --> D["LOOK UP 2D AIRFOIL FORCES"]
    D --> D1["dL = half rho U i squared c i C_L i dr"]
    D --> D2["dD = half rho U i squared c i C_D i dr"]

    D1 --> E["RESOLVE INTO THRUST AND TORQUE"]
    D2 --> E
    E --> E1["dT elem = dL cos phi minus dD sin phi"]
    E --> E2["dQ elem = r i times dL sin phi plus dD cos phi"]

    E1 --> F["MOMENTUM THEORY UPDATE FOR a_i"]
    F --> F1["Solve quadratic: 2 rho V_inf squared a one plus a minus dT per area equals zero"]
    F1 --> F2["a i = half times sqrt one plus dT elem over half rho V_inf squared pi r squared dr minus one"]

    F2 --> G["CHECK CONVERGENCE"]
    G --> G1["a new minus a old less than epsilon"]
    G1 -->|Not Converged| C
    G1 -->|Converged| H["INTEGRATE OVER ALL STATIONS"]

    H --> H1["Total Thrust T equals B integral dT elem"]
    H --> H2["Total Torque Q equals B integral dQ elem"]
    H --> H3["Shaft Power P equals Omega Q"]

    H1 --> I["OUTPUT PERFORMANCE COEFFICIENTS"]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> I1["C_T equals T over rho n squared D to power four"]
    I --> I2["C_P equals P over rho n cubed D to power five"]
    I --> I3["Propulsive Efficiency eta_p equals T V_inf over P"]

    I1 --> J["COMPARE WITH KTU BOARD REQUIREMENTS"]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
```

## 4.2 Actuator Disk Stream-Tube Schematic

The following block diagram maps the velocity and pressure variations through the actuator disk — the **most-tested diagram** for KTU Module 4.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["STATION INFINITY UPSTREAM"] -->|V_inf uniform p_inf uniform| S2["JUST UPSTREAM OF DISK"]
    S2 -->|V i uniform p i minus pressure| S3["ACTUATOR DISK Propeller Disk Area A"]
    S3 -->|V i uniform p i plus pressure Delta p jump| S4["JUST DOWNSTREAM OF DISK"]
    S4 -->|V w uniform p w uniform equals p_inf| S5["STATION WAKE FAR DOWNSTREAM"]

    S1 -->|Stream tube area A_inf| S2
    S2 -->|Stream tube area A disk| S3
    S3 -->|Stream tube area A disk| S4
    S4 -->|Stream tube area A w contracted| S5

    N1["Note V i greater than V_inf Acceleration through disk"] -.-> S3
    N2["Note V w greater than V i Inertial wake continues to accelerate far downstream"] -.-> S5
    N3["Note A w less than A Stream tube contracts to conserve mass"] -.-> S5
```

## 4.3 Blade Element Force Resolution Triangle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1["Blade element at radius r has chord c and pitch beta"] --> R2["Freestream V_inf acts axially forward"]
    R1 --> R3["Rotation gives tangential speed Omega r in plane of disk"]

    R2 --> R4["Resultant relative velocity U"]
    R3 --> R4

    R4 --> R5["Inflow angle phi = arctan V_inf over Omega r"]

    R5 --> R6["Section lift dL perpendicular to U"]
    R5 --> R7["Section drag dD parallel to U"]

    R6 --> R8["Thrust component dT = dL cos phi minus dD sin phi"]
    R7 --> R8

    R6 --> R9["Torque arm r times force"]
    R7 --> R9

    R9 --> R10["Torque dQ = r times dL sin phi plus dD cos phi"]

    R8 --> R11["Integrate over r from r_h to R and multiply by B"]
    R10 --> R11

    R11 --> R12["Total Thrust T and Total Power P equals Omega times Q"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. State Froude's Momentum Theory of propellers. What are its key assumptions?**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Model Answer:**
Froude's momentum theory treats the propeller as an **actuator disk** that imparts a uniform axial velocity increment to the air passing through it. Applying conservation of mass, axial momentum, and energy across a control volume that extends from far upstream to the far wake, the theory predicts thrust, power, and propulsive efficiency **without requiring knowledge of blade geometry**.

**Key Assumptions:**
1. The propeller is a thin, frictionless actuator disk of area $A = \pi R^2$.
2. Steady, incompressible, inviscid, axisymmetric flow.
3. Uniform velocity and pressure across each cross-section.
4. The velocity at the disk equals the arithmetic mean of upstream and wake velocities: $V_i = (V_\infty + V_w)/2$.
5. No energy loss other than shaft power transferred to the air.
6. Far-wake pressure equals freestream pressure.

---

### **Q2. Define advance ratio $J$ and thrust coefficient $C_T$ for a propeller.**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
- **Advance Ratio $J$:** It is the dimensionless ratio of the axial flight velocity to the tip speed of the propeller. It measures how fast the propeller is moving forward relative to its rotational speed.
$$J = \frac{V_\infty}{nD} = \frac{V_\infty}{\pi D N / 60}$$
where $n$ is revolutions per second and $D$ is propeller diameter. $J = 0$ corresponds to static (hover) condition; $J \to \infty$ corresponds to windmilling.

- **Thrust Coefficient $C_T$:** It is the dimensionless ratio of the thrust force to the dynamic pressure based on tip speed:
$$C_T = \frac{T}{\rho n^2 D^4}$$
where $\rho$ is air density. It allows comparison of propellers of different sizes at different operating speeds.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - In momentum theory derivations, students frequently **forget the factor of 2** in $V_w = V_\infty(1+2a)$. Always verify by substituting $a=1$ (static case) to confirm $V_w = 2V_i$.
> - In BET, students often write $dT = dL$ instead of $dT = dL\cos\phi - dD\sin\phi$. The angle $\phi$ matters! Marks are deducted if the force resolution step is skipped.
> - Students frequently confuse **propulsive efficiency** $\eta_p = V_\infty/V_i$ with **Froude ideal efficiency** $\eta_F = 2/(1+\sqrt{1+C_T/2})$. They are related but not identical — do not interchange them.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### (a) Derive the expressions for thrust, power, and propulsive efficiency of a propeller using Froude's momentum theory. **(7 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the control volume and induced velocity factor.**
The actuator disk is at station $i$ with velocity $V_i$. Freestream is $V_\infty$ at station $\infty$, far-wake velocity is $V_w$. Define:
$$V_i = V_\infty(1 + a)$$

**Step 2 — Mass conservation.**
$$\dot{m} = \rho A V_i = \rho A V_\infty(1+a)$$

**Step 3 — Apply Bernoulli upstream of disk and downstream of disk, then add:**
$$p_\infty + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 = p_{i^-} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_i^2$$
$$p_{i^+} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_i^2 = p_w + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_w^2$$
Adding and using $p_w = p_\infty$:
$$\Delta p = p_{i^+} - p_{i^-} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho(V_w^2 - V_\infty^2)$$

**Step 4 — Momentum theorem.**
$$T = \dot{m}(V_w - V_\infty) = \rho A V_i (V_w - V_\infty)$$

**Step 5 — Equate the two thrust expressions and solve for the relation between $V_i, V_\infty, V_w$:**
$$\tfrac{1}{2}\rho A(V_w - V_\infty)(V_w + V_\infty) = \rho A V_i(V_w - V_\infty)$$
$$\therefore V_i = \frac{V_\infty + V_w}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Express $V_w$ in terms of $a$:**
$$V_w = 2V_i - V_\infty = V_\infty(1+2a)$$

**Step 7 — Final thrust and power expressions:**
$$T = \rho A V_\infty(1+a) \cdot V_\infty(2a) = 2\rho A V_\infty^2 a(1+a)$$
$$P = T \cdot V_i = 2\rho A V_\infty^3 a(1+a)^2$$
$$\eta_p = \frac{T V_\infty}{P} = \frac{1}{1+a}$$

#### (b) A propeller of diameter 2.5 m operates at 2400 RPM in sea-level air ($\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³) flying at 100 m/s. If the thrust produced is 1500 N, calculate: (i) the induced velocity factor $a$, (ii) the shaft power required, and (iii) the propulsive efficiency. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
$D = 2.5$ m, $N = 2400$ RPM $\Rightarrow n = 2400/60 = 40$ rev/s, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $V_\infty = 100$ m/s, $T = 1500$ N.

**Compute disk area:**
$$A = \pi D^2/4 = \pi(2.5)^2/4 = 4.9087 \,\text{m}^2$$

**(i) Induced velocity factor $a$:**
Using $T = 2\rho A V_\infty^2 a(1+a)$:
$$1500 = 2(1.225)(4.9087)(100)^2 \, a(1+a)$$
$$1500 = 1.20263 \times 10^5 \, a(1+a)$$
$$a^2 + a - 0.01247 = 0$$
Using the quadratic formula:
$$a = \frac{-1 + \sqrt{1 + 0.04990}}{2} = \frac{-1 + \sqrt{1.04990}}{2} = \frac{-1 + 1.02465}{2}$$
$$\boxed{a = 0.01232}$$

**(ii) Shaft power:**
First compute $V_i = V_\infty(1+a) = 100(1.01232) = 101.232$ m/s.
$$P = T \cdot V_i = 1500 \times 101.232$$
$$\boxed{P = 151{,}848 \,\text{W} \approx 151.85 \,\text{kW}}$$

**(iii) Propulsive efficiency:**
$$\eta_p = \frac{1}{1+a} = \frac{1}{1.01232}$$
$$\boxed{\eta_p = 0.9878 \text{ or } 98.78\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** At high flight speed, propeller efficiency approaches 100% as $a \to 0$. This is why propellers are most efficient in cruise and least efficient in static / hover conditions.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

#### (a) Explain Blade Element Theory of propellers. Derive expressions for the elemental thrust and torque on a propeller blade section at radius $r$. **(7 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept:** Blade Element Theory decomposes the propeller blade into a series of thin annular strips (blade elements) at radii $r$ from hub to tip. Each element is analysed as an independent 2-D airfoil experiencing combined translation ($V_\infty$) and rotation ($\Omega r$). The elemental lift and drag are resolved into thrust and torque contributions, then integrated over the blade radius and multiplied by the number of blades.

**Local Kinematics at radius $r$:**
- Tangential velocity (plane of rotation): $V_\theta = \Omega r = 2\pi n r$
- Resultant velocity (relative to blade section): $U = \sqrt{V_\infty^2 + (\Omega r)^2}$
- Inflow angle: $\tan\phi = V_\infty / (\Omega r)$
- Local angle of attack: $\alpha = \beta(r) - \phi$

**Elemental lift and drag (per blade, element width $dr$):**
$$dL = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 \, c(r) \, C_L(\alpha) \, dr$$
$$dD = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 \, c(r) \, C_D(\alpha) \, dr$$

**Force resolution (per blade, per element):**
The forces act perpendicular and parallel to $U$. Resolving into propeller-fixed axes (thrust = axial, torque = rotational):

$$dT_\text{elem} = dL\cos\phi - dD\sin\phi$$
$$dQ_\text{elem} = r(dL\sin\phi + dD\cos\phi)$$

**Derivation check (ideal case $C_D = 0$):**
$$dT_\text{elem} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 c(r) C_L \cos\phi \, dr$$
$$dQ_\text{elem} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 c(r) C_L \sin\phi \, r \, dr$$

**Integration over the blade and across $B$ blades:**
$$T = \frac{B}{2}\int_{r_h}^{R} \rho U^2 c(r) [C_L\cos\phi - C_D\sin\phi] \, dr$$
$$Q = \frac{B}{2}\int_{r_h}^{R} \rho U^2 c(r) r [C_L\sin\phi + C_D\cos\phi] \, dr$$
$$P = \Omega Q$$

#### (b) A two-bladed propeller has diameter 2.0 m and operates at 1800 RPM. At a flight speed of 60 m/s, calculate the elemental thrust and torque at $r = 0.7$ m, assuming $C_L = 0.5$, $C_D = 0.02$, local chord $c = 0.20$ m, and local blade pitch $\beta = 25^\circ$. Take $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $B = 2$, $D = 2.0$ m, $N = 1800$ RPM, $V_\infty = 60$ m/s, $r = 0.7$ m, $C_L = 0.5$, $C_D = 0.02$, $c = 0.20$ m, $\beta = 25^\circ$, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³.

**Compute $\Omega$:**
$$\Omega = 2\pi n = 2\pi \times (1800/60) = 2\pi \times 30 = 188.496 \,\text{rad/s}$$

**Local tangential velocity:**
$$V_\theta = \Omega r = 188.496 \times 0.7 = 131.947 \,\text{m/s}$$

**Resultant velocity $U$:**
$$U = \sqrt{V_\infty^2 + V_\theta^2} = \sqrt{60^2 + 131.947^2} = \sqrt{3600 + 17410.0} = \sqrt{21010} = 144.95 \,\text{m/s}$$

**Inflow angle $\phi$:**
$$\tan\phi = \frac{V_\infty}{V_\theta} = \frac{60}{131.947} = 0.4547$$
$$\phi = \arctan(0.4547) = 24.43^\circ$$
$$\cos\phi = 0.9104, \quad \sin\phi = 0.4137$$

**Angle of attack:**
$$\alpha = \beta - \phi = 25^\circ - 24.43^\circ = 0.57^\circ$$
(This is a sanity check — for typical cruise, $\alpha$ is small and positive.)

**Elemental lift and drag (per blade, $dr = 1$ unit thickness for the calculation):**
$$dL = \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(144.95)^2 (0.20)(0.5)(1) = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 21010 \times 0.20 \times 0.5$$
$$dL = 1286.86 \,\text{N per blade per metre}$$

$$dD = \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(144.95)^2 (0.20)(0.02)(1) = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 21010 \times 0.20 \times 0.02$$
$$dD = 51.47 \,\text{N per blade per metre}$$

**Elemental thrust (per blade):**
$$dT_\text{elem} = dL\cos\phi - dD\sin\phi = (1286.86)(0.9104) - (51.47)(0.4137)$$
$$dT_\text{elem} = 1171.55 - 21.30 = 1150.25 \,\text{N}$$

**Elemental torque (per blade):**
$$dQ_\text{elem} = r(dL\sin\phi + dD\cos\phi) = 0.7\big[(1286.86)(0.4137) + (51.47)(0.9104)\big]$$
$$dQ_\text{elem} = 0.7[532.36 + 46.86] = 0.7 \times 579.22 = 405.46 \,\text{N·m}$$

$$\boxed{dT_\text{elem} \approx 1150 \,\text{N per blade per metre}, \quad dQ_\text{elem} \approx 405.5 \,\text{N·m per blade per metre}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> To obtain **total** thrust and torque for the propeller, integrate over the full blade span $r \in [r_h, R]$ and multiply by $B = 2$. The numerical integration is the next step (often done via Simpson's rule or trapezoidal rule on 10–20 stations).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Propellers**

### Core Concepts (Must Memorize)
- ☐ **Momentum theory** treats the propeller as an actuator disk; **Blade element theory** treats it as a stack of 2-D airfoils.
- ☐ The Froude relation $V_i = (V_\infty + V_w)/2$ is the cornerstone of momentum theory.
- ☐ Induced velocity factor $a = (V_i - V_\infty)/V_\infty$ links disk velocity to freestream.
- ☐ Static thrust $T_0 = 2\rho A V_i^2$ (no freestream velocity).
- ☐ Static efficiency is zero — **all** power goes into the wake kinetic energy.
- ☐ Propulsive efficiency $\eta_p = 1/(1+a)$ → approaches 100% at high flight speed, falls to 0% at hover.

### Key Formulas (Three You Cannot Forget)
- ☐ $T = 2\rho A V_\infty^2 \, a(1+a)$
- ☐ $P = 2\rho A V_\infty^3 \, a(1+a)^2$
- ☐ $\eta_p = 1/(1+a)$

### Blade Element Must-Knows
- ☐ $U = \sqrt{V_\infty^2 + (\Omega r)^2}$
- ☐ $\tan\phi = V_\infty/(\Omega r)$
- ☐ $dT = dL\cos\phi - dD\sin\phi$  *(not just $dL$!)*
- ☐ $dQ = r(dL\sin\phi + dD\cos\phi)$
- ☐ Total thrust $T$ and torque $Q$ obtained by integration $\times$ number of blades $B$.

### Non-Dimensional Coefficients
- ☐ $C_T = T/(\rho n^2 D^4)$ — Thrust Coefficient
- ☐ $C_P = P/(\rho n^3 D^5)$ — Power Coefficient
- ☐ $J = V_\infty/(nD)$ — Advance Ratio
- ☐ $C_P$ vs $J$ and $C_T$ vs $J$ curves are the **standard propeller performance plots**.

### Common Exam Pitfalls
- ☐ Mistaking $V_w = V_\infty(1+a)$ for $V_w = V_\infty(1+2a)$. The correct formula has a factor of 2.
- ☐ Forgetting to multiply by the number of blades $B$ in BET integration.
- ☐ Confusing static thrust $T_0 = 2\rho A V_i^2$ with the general $T = 2\rho A V_\infty^2 a(1+a)$ formula (the static case requires a limit).
- ☐ Writing $\eta = \eta_F = \eta_p$ — Froude ideal efficiency (involves $C_T$) is **not** the same as simple propulsive efficiency ($1/(1+a)$).

### Engineering Reality Check
- ☐ Real propeller efficiency in cruise is **80–90%**, never 100%.
- ☐ In hover/axial-flight, **only ~60–70%** of shaft power produces useful thrust (rest is wake kinetic energy loss).
- ☐ BEMT = **Blade Element + Momentum Theory combined iteratively** — used in every modern propeller/rotor design code (e.g., XROTOR, OpenProp, QBlade).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Propeller Coefficients and Charts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET753 - AEROSPACE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Propeller Coefficients and Charts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🛩️ Propeller Coefficients and Charts — Core Foundation

## 1.1 What is a Propeller Performance Chart?

A **Propeller Performance Chart** is a standardized, non-dimensional graphical representation that describes the aerodynamic behavior of a propeller across a wide range of flight and operating conditions. Instead of plotting raw quantities like Thrust (Newtons) or Power (Watts), the propeller's behavior is captured using **non-dimensional coefficients** plotted against a parameter called the **Advance Ratio (J)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:**
> Propeller performance is **always** expressed in non-dimensional form. This is because the same chart for a 2-meter propeller tested in a wind tunnel can be directly used to predict the performance of a geometrically similar 4-meter propeller in actual flight. This is the principle of **dynamic similarity** — guaranteed by the use of non-dimensional coefficients.

## 1.2 The Four Primary Propeller Coefficients

The complete aerodynamic state of a propeller operating at a given flight condition is fully defined by **four fundamental non-dimensional parameters**:

1. **Advance Ratio (J)** — *Defines the flight condition*
2. **Thrust Coefficient (CT)** — *Defines the thrust-producing capability*
3. **Power Coefficient (CP)** — *Defines the power-consuming capability*
4. **Propeller Efficiency (η)** — *Defines the conversion effectiveness*

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Ceiling Fan on a Moving Skateboard"

Imagine pushing a **ceiling fan** along the floor on a **skateboard**.

- **Stationary fan (V = 0):** It just churns the air — produces maximum thrust but no forward motion. High CT, very low efficiency (η → 0 because the propeller is wasting energy on slipstream rotation).
- **Slowly moving skateboard (low V):** The fan still pushes hard, producing useful thrust. Good efficiency.
- **Fast-moving skateboard (high V):** The fan is "racing" through the air — the blades can no longer bite the air effectively. Thrust drops, efficiency falls again.
- **Sweet spot:** Somewhere in between, the propeller produces useful thrust with minimum power waste. This is the **peak efficiency point** shown on every propeller chart.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Mapping:** The Advance Ratio (J) is essentially the "speed of the skateboard" relative to the propeller's rotational tip speed. Every coefficient is a way of measuring "how well the fan is performing at this specific speed."

## 1.4 Standard Engineering Constants Used in Propeller Analysis

The following physical quantities appear in **every propeller coefficient**:

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ρ** | Air Density (Sea Level) | kg/m³ | **1.225** |
| **μ** | Dynamic Viscosity | N·s/m² | 1.789 × 10⁻⁵ |
| **g** | Gravitational Acceleration | m/s² | **9.81** |
| **V** | True Airspeed / Axial Velocity | m/s | Variable |
| **D** | Propeller Diameter | m | Variable |
| **n** | Rotational Speed | rev/s or rad/s | Variable |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Master Formulas — One Per Coefficient

The propeller's performance is mathematically captured by a set of equations that strip away the scale (size), speed (RPM), and atmospheric conditions, leaving behind a pure aerodynamic fingerprint.

### A. Advance Ratio (J)

$$J = \frac{V}{nD}$$

**Why it exists:** When an aircraft flies faster, OR the propeller spins slower, OR the propeller is smaller — the *aerodynamic state* is similar. The Advance Ratio captures this single combined variable.

**Logic flow:**
- Numerator $V$ → forward speed of the aircraft
- Denominator $nD$ → circumferential speed of the blade tip (revolutions per second × diameter)
- The ratio is **dimensionless** (m/s ÷ m/s = pure number)

### B. Thrust Coefficient (CT)

$$C_T = \frac{T}{\rho n^2 D^4}$$

**Why this exact form:** Dimensional analysis shows that thrust (Force) must be non-dimensionalized by $\rho \cdot (\text{velocity})^2 \cdot (\text{area})$. The relevant velocity scale is $nD$ and the relevant area is $D^2$, hence $\rho n^2 D^4$.

### C. Power Coefficient (CP)

$$C_P = \frac{P}{\rho n^3 D^5}$$

**Why:** Power = Force × Velocity, so non-dimensional power must contain one extra $nD$ factor over the thrust coefficient form. This gives $\rho n^3 D^5$.

### D. Torque Coefficient (CQ)

$$C_Q = \frac{Q}{\rho n^2 D^5}$$

**Why:** Torque has units of Force × Length, hence $D^5$ in the denominator.

### E. Propeller Efficiency (η)

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Thrust Power}}{\text{Shaft Power}} = \frac{T \cdot V}{2\pi n Q} = \frac{J \cdot C_T}{2\pi C_Q} = \frac{J \cdot C_T}{C_P}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Golden Identity:** $\eta = \dfrac{J \, C_T}{C_P}$ — This is the single most tested formula in Module 4. Memorize its derivation since questions often ask "Show that efficiency can be expressed as..."

## 2.2 The Geometric Pitch Concept

For a chart-based problem, the propeller's behavior also depends on its **blade angle** $\beta$ (measured at a reference radius, usually 75% of the blade radius).

| Quantity | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **Geometric Pitch** | $P_g$ | The distance a propeller would advance in one revolution if it were moving through a solid (no slip) |
| **Effective Pitch** | $P_e$ | The actual forward distance moved per revolution in real air |
| **Slip** | $S$ | $S = P_g - P_e$ |
| **Pitch Ratio** | $P/D$ | The non-dimensional pitch (the parameter that labels the family of curves) |

## 2.3 The Three Standard Propeller Performance Charts

For a **fixed-pitch propeller**, the following three charts completely define the propeller:

> [!NOTE]
> **Chart 1 — CT vs J** (at constant blade angle β or P/D)
> A curve starting at high CT when J = 0, decreasing roughly linearly as J increases.

> [!NOTE]
> **Chart 2 — CP vs J** (at constant blade angle β or P/D)
> A curve that is relatively flat or slightly varying with J.

> [!NOTE]
> **Chart 3 — η vs J** (at constant blade angle β or P/D)
> A curve that rises from 0 at J = 0, reaches a **peak efficiency**, then falls. The peak is the propeller's design point.

**Multiple curves** on each chart correspond to different blade angles or pitch ratios, allowing the engineer to **read off** the coefficient for any given operating point.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These charts are indispensable in:
- **Aircraft design:** Selecting the correct propeller for an engine/airframe combination.
- **Performance estimation:** Predicting takeoff distance, climb rate, and cruise speed.
- **Wind tunnel testing:** A 1:5 scale model test data can be scaled up to full size.
- **Maneuver analysis:** Determining if the propeller will stall or produce reverse thrust.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Pinned for Exam)

| # | Coefficient | Formula | Governing Variable |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Advance Ratio | $J = \dfrac{V}{nD}$ | Flight condition |
| 2 | Thrust Coefficient | $C_T = \dfrac{T}{\rho n^2 D^4}$ | Thrust production |
| 3 | Power Coefficient | $C_P = \dfrac{P}{\rho n^3 D^5}$ | Power consumption |
| 4 | Torque Coefficient | $C_Q = \dfrac{Q}{\rho n^2 D^5}$ | Torque load |
| 5 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{J \, C_T}{C_P}$ | Conversion efficiency |
| 6 | Geometric Pitch | $P_g = 2\pi r \tan\beta$ | Blade geometry |
| 7 | Slip Ratio | $\dfrac{\text{Slip}}{P_g} = 1 - \dfrac{V}{n P_g}$ | Real vs ideal advance |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: The Efficiency Identity $\eta = J C_T / C_P$

**Starting Point — Definition of Propeller Efficiency:**

The propeller converts **shaft power** (mechanical power from the engine) into **thrust power** (useful work moving the aircraft forward). Therefore:

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Useful Thrust Power}}{\text{Shaft Power Supplied to Propeller}}$$

**Step 1 — Express the Thrust Power:**

The thrust $T$ acts on the aircraft moving at velocity $V$, so the useful power delivered is:

$$P_{\text{thrust}} = T \cdot V$$

**Step 2 — Express the Shaft Power in terms of Torque:**

The shaft applies a torque $Q$ to the propeller, which rotates at $n$ revolutions per second. The power delivered to the propeller is:

$$P_{\text{shaft}} = 2\pi n \cdot Q$$

*This is the classical mechanical engineering identity: Power = Torque × Angular Velocity, where angular velocity $\omega = 2\pi n$ in rad/s when $n$ is in rev/s.*

**Step 3 — Form the Ratio:**

$$\eta = \frac{T \cdot V}{2\pi n \cdot Q}$$

**Step 4 — Introduce the Coefficients:**

Substitute the definitions of $C_T$, $C_P$, and $C_Q$:

$$T = C_T \cdot \rho n^2 D^4 \quad ; \quad V = J \cdot nD \quad ; \quad Q = C_Q \cdot \rho n^2 D^5$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Into the Efficiency Expression:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{\left(C_T \cdot \rho n^2 D^4\right) \cdot \left(J \cdot nD\right)}{2\pi n \cdot \left(C_Q \cdot \rho n^2 D^5\right)} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{C_T \cdot J \cdot \rho n^3 D^5}{2\pi \cdot C_Q \cdot \rho n^3 D^5}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Cancel the Common Terms:**

The factors $\rho n^3 D^5$ appear in both numerator and denominator and cancel exactly:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{C_T \cdot J}{2\pi \cdot C_Q} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{J \cdot C_T}{C_P} \quad \left(\because C_P = 2\pi C_Q\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\eta = \frac{J \cdot C_T}{C_P}}$$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: The Power–Torque Relation $C_P = 2\pi C_Q$

**Starting Point:**

Shaft power $P = 2\pi n Q$ (from rotational mechanics).

**Step 1 — Divide both sides by the non-dimensionalizing factor $\rho n^3 D^5$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{P}{\rho n^3 D^5} &= \frac{2\pi n Q}{\rho n^3 D^5} \\[4pt]
C_P &= 2\pi \cdot \frac{Q}{\rho n^2 D^5} \\[4pt]
C_P &= 2\pi C_Q
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Identity:**

$$\boxed{C_P = 2\pi \, C_Q}$$

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: The Slip Ratio Expression

**Step 1 — Define Geometric Pitch:**

The geometric pitch $P_g$ is the axial advance per revolution assuming no slip:

$$P_g = 2\pi r \tan\beta \quad \text{(at blade section radius } r \text{)}$$

**Step 2 — Define Effective Pitch:**

The effective pitch is the **actual** forward advance per revolution:

$$P_e = \frac{V}{n}$$

**Step 3 — Define Slip:**

$$\text{Slip} = P_g - P_e = P_g - \frac{V}{n}$$

**Step 4 — Form the Non-Dimensional Slip Ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\text{Slip}}{P_g} &= \frac{P_g - V/n}{P_g} = 1 - \frac{V}{n P_g} \\[4pt]
&= 1 - \frac{J \cdot D}{P_g} = 1 - \frac{J}{P/D}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\frac{\text{Slip}}{P_g} = 1 - \frac{J}{P/D}}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:**
A propeller of diameter **2.4 m** rotates at **2400 RPM** while the aircraft flies at **80 m/s** in air of density **1.2 kg/m³**. The thrust produced is **1800 N**. Compute:
- (a) Advance Ratio $J$
- (b) Thrust Coefficient $C_T$

**Given Data:**
- $V = 80$ m/s
- $D = 2.4$ m
- $N = 2400$ RPM → $n = 2400/60 = 40$ rev/s
- $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³
- $T = 1800$ N

**Solution (a) — Advance Ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
J &= \frac{V}{nD} = \frac{80}{(40)(2.4)} \\[4pt]
J &= \frac{80}{96} = 0.833
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution (b) — Thrust Coefficient:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_T &= \frac{T}{\rho n^2 D^4} = \frac{1800}{(1.2)(40)^2(2.4)^4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compute each factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho n^2 D^4 &= (1.2)(1600)(33.1776) \\[4pt]
&= (1920)(33.1776) = 63{,}701
\end{aligned}
$$

*Note: $(2.4)^4 = (2.4)^2 \cdot (2.4)^2 = 5.76 \times 5.76 = 33.1776$*

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_T &= \frac{1800}{63{,}701} \\[4pt]
C_T &= 0.02826
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:** $J = 0.833$, $C_T = 0.0283$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📊 Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: How Propeller Charts Are Constructed

The following block diagram illustrates the logical pipeline that converts raw wind-tunnel or flight-test data into the standardized non-dimensional propeller charts used by aerospace engineers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Measured Data: T, P, Q, V, n] --> B[Identify Constants: ρ, D, Atmosphere]
    B --> C[Apply Non-Dimensionalization]
    C --> D[Compute J = V / nD]
    C --> E[Compute CT = T / ρn²D⁴]
    C --> F[Compute CP = P / ρn³D⁵]
    C --> G[Compute CQ = Q / ρn²D⁵]
    D --> H[Generate X-Axis Values]
    E --> I[Chart 1: CT vs J Curve]
    F --> J[Chart 2: CP vs J Curve]
    G --> K[Compute CP = 2π CQ Cross-Check]
    D --> I
    D --> J
    D --> L[Chart 3: η = J·CT / CP vs J]
    E --> L
    F --> L
    I --> M[Propeller Performance Database]
    J --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Aircraft Design Selection]
    M --> O[Performance Prediction Tool]
    M --> P[Flight Test Validation]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Three Charts — Three Engineering Questions

This diagram maps each propeller performance chart to the specific engineering question it answers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PROP[Propeller Performance Suite]
        Q1[How much thrust?]
        Q2[How much power consumed?]
        Q3[How efficiently converting?]
    end
    Q1 --> A1[Chart CT vs J]
    Q2 --> A2[Chart CP vs J]
    Q3 --> A3[Chart η vs J]
    A1 --> Z[Engineering Decision: Propeller Selected]
    A2 --> Z
    A3 --> Z
```

## 4.3 Flowchart: The KTU Exam Problem-Solving Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Read Problem] --> Extract[Extract V, n, D, ρ, T, P, Q]
    Extract --> ConvertRPM{n given in RPM?}
    ConvertRPM -- Yes --> CalcN[n = N / 60]
    ConvertRPM -- No --> DirectUse[Use n directly]
    CalcN --> CalcJ[Step 1: Compute J = V / nD]
    DirectUse --> CalcJ
    CalcJ --> NeedCT{Need CT?}
    NeedCT -- Yes --> CalcCT[CT = T / ρn²D⁴]
    NeedCT -- No --> NeedCP
    CalcCT --> NeedCP{Need CP?}
    NeedCP -- Yes --> CalcCP[CP = P / ρn³D⁵]
    NeedCP -- No --> NeedEta
    CalcCP --> NeedEta{Need η?}
    NeedEta -- Yes --> CalcEta[η = J·CT / CP]
    NeedEta -- No --> Done[All Required Quantities Computed]
    CalcEta --> Done
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** The above flowchart is a brilliant mental model — before any calculation, identify the target coefficient, then walk backward to find the input data. The non-dimensionalization step is the same every time.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the following terms: (i) Advance Ratio, (ii) Thrust Coefficient, (iii) Power Coefficient of a propeller.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Advance Ratio (J):** It is the non-dimensional ratio of the aircraft's forward velocity to the propeller's tip speed.

$$J = \frac{V}{nD}$$

**(ii) Thrust Coefficient (CT):** It is a non-dimensional measure of thrust produced, defined as the thrust per unit of dynamic pressure and disc area reference.

$$C_T = \frac{T}{\rho n^2 D^4}$$

**(iii) Power Coefficient (CP):** It is the non-dimensional measure of power absorbed by the propeller.

$$C_P = \frac{P}{\rho n^3 D^5}$$

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**State the relation between Power Coefficient and Torque Coefficient. Derive it briefly.**

**Model Answer:**

The relation is $C_P = 2\pi C_Q$.

**Derivation:** The shaft power absorbed by a rotating shaft is given by $P = 2\pi n Q$. Dividing both sides by the non-dimensionalizing factor $\rho n^3 D^5$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{P}{\rho n^3 D^5} &= \frac{2\pi n Q}{\rho n^3 D^5} \\[4pt]
C_P &= 2\pi \cdot \frac{Q}{\rho n^2 D^5} = 2\pi C_Q
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, $\boxed{C_P = 2\pi C_Q}$.

---

## PART B — Full Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 4]`

**(a) Explain the various non-dimensional coefficients used in propeller performance analysis. List and define any four such coefficients. (7 marks)**

**(b) A two-bladed propeller of diameter 2.5 m is fitted to an aircraft flying at 90 m/s. The propeller rotates at 2200 RPM and produces a thrust of 2400 N. Air density is 1.225 kg/m³. Determine: (i) the Advance Ratio, (ii) the Thrust Coefficient, and (iii) the power absorbed if the propeller efficiency is 75%. (7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Question A — Complete Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Non-Dimensional Propeller Coefficients

The performance of a propeller depends on its size, rotational speed, forward velocity, and air density. To obtain a single, scale-independent description of the propeller, the following **four non-dimensional coefficients** are defined:

**1. Advance Ratio (J):**
$$J = \frac{V}{nD}$$
It represents the ratio of forward flight speed to the propeller blade tip speed. Higher $J$ means the propeller is operating at a higher forward speed for a given RPM.

**2. Thrust Coefficient (CT):**
$$C_T = \frac{T}{\rho n^2 D^4}$$
It represents the thrust produced in non-dimensional form, with $\rho n^2 D^4$ as the reference force.

**3. Power Coefficient (CP):**
$$C_P = \frac{P}{\rho n^3 D^5}$$
It represents the power absorbed, non-dimensionalized by the dynamic pressure times a representative volumetric flow rate.

**4. Torque Coefficient (CQ):**
$$C_Q = \frac{Q}{\rho n^2 D^5}$$
The torque absorbed by the propeller in non-dimensional form.

**5. Propeller Efficiency (η):** (bonus)
$$\eta = \frac{J \cdot C_T}{C_P}$$
This represents the fraction of shaft power converted to useful thrust power.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Because these coefficients are non-dimensional, the performance of a small wind-tunnel model can be extrapolated to a full-size propeller, provided **Reynolds number similarity** is also maintained.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation

**Given Data:**
- $D = 2.5$ m
- $V = 90$ m/s
- $N = 2200$ RPM → $n = 2200 / 60 = 36.667$ rev/s
- $T = 2400$ N
- $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³
- $\eta = 0.75$

**(i) Advance Ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
J &= \frac{V}{nD} = \frac{90}{(36.667)(2.5)} \\[4pt]
J &= \frac{90}{91.667} = 0.9818
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Thrust Coefficient:**

Compute $D^4$:
$$D^4 = (2.5)^4 = 39.0625$$

Compute denominator:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho n^2 D^4 &= (1.225)(36.667)^2(39.0625) \\[4pt]
&= (1.225)(1344.44)(39.0625) \\[4pt]
&= 64{,}328
\end{aligned}
$$

Then:
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_T &= \frac{2400}{64{,}328} = 0.03731
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Power Absorbed:**

Using the efficiency relation $\eta = J C_T / C_P$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_P &= \frac{J C_T}{\eta} = \frac{(0.9818)(0.03731)}{0.75} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.03664}{0.75} = 0.04885
\end{aligned}
$$

Now solve for $P$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= C_P \cdot \rho n^3 D^5
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute $D^5 = 2.5^5 = 97.656$

Compute $n^3 = (36.667)^3 = 49{,}300$

Compute denominator factor:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho n^3 D^5 &= (1.225)(49{,}300)(97.656) \\[4pt]
&= 5{,}896{,}423
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= (0.04885)(5{,}896{,}423) \\[4pt]
P &= 288{,}043 \text{ W} \approx 288 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $J = 0.982$
- (ii) $C_T = 0.0373$
- (iii) $P \approx 288$ kW

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

`[KTU University Exam - June 2024 | Module 4]`

**(a) With the help of neat sketches, explain the typical propeller performance charts used in aerospace engineering. Discuss the variation of CT, CP, and η with advance ratio. (7 marks)**

**(b) A propeller of diameter 3 m operates at 1500 RPM. The flight velocity is 60 m/s. Air density is 1.225 kg/m³. If the thrust coefficient is 0.05 and power coefficient is 0.18, calculate: (i) the thrust, (ii) the power absorbed, and (iii) the propeller efficiency. (7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Question B — Complete Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Propeller Performance Charts Explained

For a propeller of fixed pitch (constant blade angle), three primary charts describe the complete performance:

**Chart 1 — Thrust Coefficient vs Advance Ratio (CT vs J):**
The CT curve starts at a maximum value when $J = 0$ (propeller windmilling in still air) and decreases **approximately linearly** as $J$ increases. The slope is governed by the blade angle.

**Chart 2 — Power Coefficient vs Advance Ratio (CP vs J):**
The CP curve is relatively **flat** — power required is nearly independent of forward speed, though it does drop slightly with increasing $J$.

**Chart 3 — Propeller Efficiency vs Advance Ratio (η vs J):**
The efficiency curve starts at $\eta = 0$ when $J = 0$ (no useful work), rises rapidly, reaches a **peak** at a specific $J$ (the design point), and then falls off at higher $J$. The peak is typically in the range $0.7 < J < 1.5$ for most propellers.

Each chart is a **family of curves** parameterized by blade angle $\beta$ or pitch ratio $P/D$, allowing the engineer to read coefficients for any given operating point.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The peak of the efficiency curve indicates the propeller's most economical operating point. Aircraft designers choose the blade angle so that cruise conditions lie near this peak.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation

**Given Data:**
- $D = 3$ m
- $N = 1500$ RPM → $n = 1500/60 = 25$ rev/s
- $V = 60$ m/s
- $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³
- $C_T = 0.05$
- $C_P = 0.18$

**(i) Thrust:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= C_T \cdot \rho n^2 D^4
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute $D^4 = 3^4 = 81$

Compute $n^2 = 625$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= (0.05)(1.225)(625)(81) \\[4pt]
T &= (0.05)(1.225)(50{,}625) \\[4pt]
T &= (0.05)(62{,}016) \\[4pt]
T &= 3100.8 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Power Absorbed:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= C_P \cdot \rho n^3 D^5
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute $n^3 = 25^3 = 15{,}625$

Compute $D^5 = 3^5 = 243$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= (0.18)(1.225)(15{,}625)(243) \\[4pt]
P &= (0.18)(1.225)(3{,}796{,}875) \\[4pt]
P &= (0.18)(4{,}651{,}172) \\[4pt]
P &= 837{,}211 \text{ W} \approx 837.2 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Propeller Efficiency:**

First, verify the Advance Ratio:
$$
J = \frac{V}{nD} = \frac{60}{(25)(3)} = \frac{60}{75} = 0.8
$$

Then:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{J C_T}{C_P} = \frac{(0.8)(0.05)}{0.18} \\[4pt]
\eta &= \frac{0.04}{0.18} = 0.2222
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $T \approx 3101$ N
- (ii) $P \approx 837.2$ kW
- (iii) $\eta = 0.222$ (or 22.22%)

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion of RPM:** Most students forget to convert RPM to rev/s. Use $n = N/60$. Losing this step costs **2 marks** in numerical problems.
> 2. **Confusion between $nD$ vs $n^2 D$:** The thrust coefficient denominator is $\rho n^2 D^4$, not $\rho n^2 D^2$. Memorize the **power** in the denominator (squared for $T$, cubed for $P$).
> 3. **Efficiency above 100% check:** If you compute $\eta > 1$, you have a sign error or used the wrong formula. Propeller efficiency is always less than ~90% for real propellers.
> 4. **Forgetting to compute $J$ explicitly:** Many students skip this and directly use the efficiency formula, only to be marked down for not showing the relationship.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Advance Ratio:** $J = V / nD$ — Always compute this first; it's the X-axis of every chart.
- ✅ **Thrust Coefficient:** $C_T = T / (\rho n^2 D^4)$ — Denominator has $n^2 D^4$.
- ✅ **Power Coefficient:** $C_P = P / (\rho n^3 D^5)$ — Denominator has $n^3 D^5$.
- ✅ **Torque Coefficient:** $C_Q = Q / (\rho n^2 D^5)$ — Denominator has $n^2 D^5$.
- ✅ **Efficiency Identity:** $\eta = J C_T / C_P = (T V) / (2\pi n Q)$ — This is the master relation.
- ✅ **Power–Torque Link:** $C_P = 2\pi C_Q$ — Always verify this for self-consistency.
- ✅ **Pitch Ratio $P/D$:** The parameter that labels each family of curves on a propeller chart.
- ✅ **Slip Ratio:** $S/P_g = 1 - J / (P/D)$ — Dimensionless measure of propeller slip.
- ✅ **Sea-Level Density:** $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ unless otherwise stated.
- ✅ **RPM to rev/s:** $n = N / 60$ (e.g., 3000 RPM → 50 rev/s).
- ✅ **Three Standard Charts:** $C_T$–$J$, $C_P$–$J$, and $\eta$–$J$ — All are essential for problem-solving.
- ✅ **Peak Efficiency Point:** The advance ratio at which the propeller delivers maximum $\eta$ — this is the aircraft's cruise design point.
- ✅ **Dynamic Similarity:** A 1:5 wind-tunnel model and a full-scale propeller share the same $C_T$, $C_P$, and $\eta$ at the same $J$ and blade angle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Pearl of Wisdom:** When solving a numerical problem, always begin by writing down: (1) Given data, (2) Required unknowns, (3) The relevant formulas, and (4) The unit conversions. KTU examiners reward **methodical presentation** — partial marks are heavily awarded for correct method, even if a final arithmetic slip occurs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aircraft Performance: Straight and Level Flight
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET753 - AEROSPACE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Aircraft Performance: Straight and Level Flight

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Straight and Level Flight** is a steady, unaccelerated flight regime in which the aircraft's flight path is both **horizontal (level)** and **rectilinear (straight)**, with the velocity vector **V** held constant in both magnitude and direction.

In strict KTU aerodynamic terms, the defining kinematic conditions are:

1. **Flight path angle (γ) = 0** → the velocity vector is horizontal.
2. **Turn rate (ψ̇) = 0** → the velocity vector does not change direction.
3. **Acceleration (a) = 0** → the velocity vector does not change magnitude.

Because Newton's First Law demands zero net force and zero net moment for zero acceleration, the aircraft in straight and level flight must satisfy a **two-axis force equilibrium** (longitudinal + vertical) and a **three-axis moment equilibrium** (pitch, roll, yaw). Under the small-angle and symmetric-flight assumptions taught in PEMET753, roll and yaw moments are automatically zero, and the only nontrivial moment equation is the **pitching moment M = 0**, which sets the required elevator deflection.

## 1.2 The Four Forces in Equilibrium

The free-body diagram of an aircraft in this flight regime is governed by **four forces**:

| Symbol | Force | Direction | In S&L Flight |
|:------:|:------|:----------|:--------------|
| **L** | Lift | Perpendicular to V (i.e., vertical here) | L = W |
| **W** | Weight | Vertically downward | Given (m·g) |
| **T** | Thrust | Along flight direction (horizontal) | T = D |
| **D** | Drag | Opposite to V (i.e., horizontal here) | T = D |

> [!NOTE]
> **Weight** for KTU problems is **W = m·g**, with the standard sea-level value **g = 9.81 m/s²**. Mass is denoted m (kg), and W is the standard symbol for weight in the syllabus (not to be confused with weight in pounds, lbf).

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Skateboarder on a Flat Road"

Imagine pushing a skateboard on a perfectly flat, smooth road at **constant speed** with no wind. The push (Thrust) is exactly balanced by friction and air resistance (Drag), so you feel no acceleration. The road pushes up on the wheels with a normal force (Lift) exactly equal to your weight on the skateboard, so you neither rise nor sink. The skateboard goes in a perfectly straight line because you are not steering.

The aircraft is the skateboarder. The two **balance equations**:

$$L = W \quad \text{and} \quad T = D$$

are the engineering translation of "the skateboard goes neither up nor down, and does not speed up or slow down."

> [!TIP]
> **Memory hook for KTU exams:** In Straight & Level flight, the two lift-related variables swap roles with the two drag-related variables — Lift balances Weight (vertical), and Thrust balances Drag (horizontal).

## 1.4 Why This Topic Is the Foundation of Performance

Every other performance problem in Module 4 (climb, glide, range, endurance, turn, takeoff, landing) is built on top of straight and level flight. The standard atmosphere, the drag polar, the propulsion model, and the CL–α–δ relationships are all introduced here. Mastery of this module is therefore a **prerequisite for every later numerical problem** in the syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Lift Equation and Its Consequence

From the aerodynamics module, the lift produced by a wing of planform area **S** flying at true airspeed **V** through an air density **ρ** is:

$$L = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,S\,C_{L}$$

Because in straight and level flight **L = W**, the **required lift coefficient** at any speed and altitude is:

$$C_{L,\text{req}} \;=\; \frac{W}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S} \;=\; \frac{2W}{\rho V^{2} S}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Consequence:** At a given weight and altitude, the *required* CL falls with the *square* of speed. Slow flight → high CL (close to CLmax); fast flight → low CL.

## 2.2 The Drag Polar — The Heart of the Performance Module

Total drag is the sum of **zero-lift drag (parasitic + profile)** and **lift-induced drag (drag-due-to-lift)**:

$$C_{D} \;=\; C_{D0} \;+\; K\,C_{L}^{2}$$

where:
- **C_D0** = zero-lift drag coefficient (depends on form factor, skin friction, parasite areas).
- **K = 1/(π·e·AR)** = induced drag factor, with **e** the Oswald efficiency factor and **AR = b²/S** the aspect ratio.

The corresponding dimensional drag is:

$$D \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S\,C_{D} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S\!\left(C_{D0} + K C_{L}^{2}\right)$$

This single equation — the **drag polar** — is the source of virtually every performance result below.

## 2.3 Thrust Required and Power Required

**Thrust required** is just drag, expressed in terms of V (substituting the CL–V relationship):

$$T_{R} \;=\; D \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S\,C_{D0} \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S\,K\!\left(\frac{2W}{\rho V^{2} S}\right)^{2}$$

$$\boxed{\;T_{R} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\rho S\,C_{D0}\,V^{2} \;+\; \frac{2 K W^{2}}{\rho S}\,\frac{1}{V^{2}}\;}$$

This is a **U-shaped curve in V** — a parabolic-like shape in log–log, classical in every performance textbook.

**Power required** is thrust required times velocity (when T is along V):

$$P_{R} \;=\; T_{R}\,V \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\rho S\,C_{D0}\,V^{3} \;+\; \frac{2 K W^{2}}{\rho S}\,\frac{1}{V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Notice the structure:** The first term is *parasitic* power ∝ V³; the second is *induced* power ∝ 1/V. Their sum is the **U-shaped power-required curve**.

## 2.4 Thrust Available and Power Available

The engine/propeller combination produces a **thrust available T_A(V, h)** that generally *decreases* with speed and *decreases* with altitude. A typical simplified model used in KTU problems:

$$T_{A}(V) \;\approx\; T_{A0}\left(1 - \frac{V}{V_{\max}}\right)$$

or simply **T_A = constant** in a first treatment. **Power available** P_A = T_A·V is the corresponding curve. **Steady flight is only possible where T_R ≤ T_A** (equivalently, P_R ≤ P_A).

## 2.5 Five Performance Speeds — The "Big Five"

| # | Speed | Condition | CL at this speed | Use in KTU problems |
|:-:|:------|:----------|:-----------------|:--------------------|
| 1 | **V_(Dmin)** — minimum drag | dT_R/dV = 0 (minimum of T_R curve) | C_L = √(C_D0 / K) | **Maximum L/D** point; **maximum range** in propeller aircraft (×√3 correction) |
| 2 | **V_(Pmin)** — minimum power | dP_R/dV = 0 | C_L = √(3 C_D0 / K) | **Maximum endurance** for jet & propeller |
| 3 | **V_(E_max)** — max endurance | = V_(Pmin) | √(3 C_D0 / K) | Minimum fuel flow per unit time |
| 4 | **V_(R_max)** — max range | = √3 · V_(Dmin) | √(C_D0 / 3K) | Maximum distance per unit fuel |
| 5 | **V_(max)** — max level speed | T_R = T_A, upper intersection | low CL | Highest sustainable S&L speed |

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single-page cheat sheet for the S&L flight problem.** Memorize the units in the right column — losing a unit mark is the most common KTU valuation error.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) |
|:-:|:---------|:--------|:-----------|
| 1 | Weight | W = m g | N |
| 2 | Lift = Weight | L = W = ½ρV²S C_L | N |
| 3 | Required CL | C_L,req = 2W / (ρ V² S) | — |
| 4 | Drag polar | C_D = C_D0 + K C_L² | — |
| 5 | Total drag | D = ½ρV²S (C_D0 + K C_L²) | N |
| 6 | Thrust required | T_R = ½ρS C_D0 V² + 2 K W² / (ρ S V²) | N |
| 7 | Power required | P_R = ½ρS C_D0 V³ + 2 K W² / (ρ S V) | W |
| 8 | Min-drag CL | C_L,Dmin = √(C_D0 / K) | — |
| 9 | Min-power CL | C_L,Pmin = √(3 C_D0 / K) | — |
| 10 | Min-drag speed | V_(Dmin) = √(2W / (ρ S √(C_D0 K))) | m/s |
| 11 | Min-power speed | V_(Pmin) = √(2W / (ρ S √(3 C_D0 K))) | m/s |
| 12 | Max L/D | (L/D)_max = 1 / (2√(K C_D0)) | — |
| 13 | Max endurance speed | V_E = V_(Pmin) | m/s |
| 14 | Max range speed (prop) | V_R = √3 · V_(Dmin) | m/s |
| 15 | Specific range | V / C_T = V / (T_R · c_T⁻¹) | m/kg, simplified: V/(D) | m/N |
| 16 | Induced drag factor | K = 1 / (π e AR), AR = b²/S | — |

> **Note on row 15:** The "specific range" in the KTU 2024 syllabus is distance travelled per unit fuel. For a propeller engine with specific fuel consumption **c_T** (per thrust, s⁻¹), specific range = V / (c_T · T_R). For a jet with **c** (per thrust, s⁻¹), the same form holds. Many exam texts approximate with V/D, which is acceptable when c is constant.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aircraft design trade studies:** Drag polar parameters C_D0 and K are tuned across the design space; an improvement of 5 % in C_D0 shifts both the maximum range and the maximum endurance significantly.
- **Flight-management systems (FMS):** Modern airliners continuously fly the **max-range cruise** or **max-endurance cruise** speeds; these are exactly the speeds derived in this module.
- **Pilot training:** A pilot's "best glide speed" after engine failure is V_(Dmin); "best endurance" (loiter) is V_(Pmin).
- **UAV mission planning:** Endurance-bound missions (surveillance) fly at V_(Pmin); range-bound missions (delivery) fly at V_R.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section presents the **complete, no-skip derivations** of the five performance speeds and the (L/D)_max result. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly.

## 3.1 Derivation of V_(Dmin) — Speed for Minimum Thrust Required

The thrust-required expression (from §2.3) is:

$$T_{R}(V) \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\rho S C_{D0} V^{2} \;+\; \frac{2 K W^{2}}{\rho S}\,V^{-2}$$

To find the minimum, set dT_R/dV = 0. Treat the two terms as:

$$T_{R,1} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\rho S C_{D0} V^{2} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \frac{dT_{R,1}}{dV} \;=\; \rho S C_{D0} V$$

$$T_{R,2} \;=\; \frac{2 K W^{2}}{\rho S}\,V^{-2} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \frac{dT_{R,2}}{dV} \;=\; -\,\frac{4 K W^{2}}{\rho S}\,V^{-3}$$

Add and set to zero:

$$\rho S C_{D0} V \;-\; \frac{4 K W^{2}}{\rho S}\,V^{-3} \;=\; 0$$

Multiply both sides by V³/(ρS):

$$C_{D0}\,V^{4} \;=\; \frac{4 K W^{2}}{(\rho S)^{2}}$$

Solve for V⁴:

$$V^{4} \;=\; \frac{4 K W^{2}}{C_{D0}\,(\rho S)^{2}}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\;V_{D_{\min}} \;=\; \left(\frac{4 K W^{2}}{C_{D0}\,\rho^{2} S^{2}}\right)^{\!1/4} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2W}{\rho S}}\,\left(\frac{K}{C_{D0}}\right)^{\!1/4}\;}$$

**Corresponding CL:** Substitute back into the CL–V relation:

$$C_{L,D_{\min}} \;=\; \frac{2W}{\rho V_{D_{\min}}^{2} S}$$

Using V_(Dmin)² = (2W/ρS)·√(K/C_D0):

$$C_{L,D_{\min}} \;=\; \frac{2W}{\rho S \,\cdot\, \frac{2W}{\rho S}\sqrt{K/C_{D0}}} \;=\; \frac{1}{\sqrt{K/C_{D0}}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{C_{D0}}{K}}$$

$$\boxed{\;C_{L,D_{\min}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{C_{D0}}{K}}\;}$$

This is the classical result: at the minimum-drag speed, **C_D = 2 C_D0** (substitute C_L² = C_D0/K into the drag polar: C_D = C_D0 + K(C_D0/K) = 2 C_D0).

## 3.2 Derivation of V_(Pmin) — Speed for Minimum Power Required

Power required is:

$$P_{R}(V) \;=\; T_{R}\,V \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\rho S C_{D0} V^{3} \;+\; \frac{2 K W^{2}}{\rho S}\,V^{-1}$$

Differentiate with respect to V:

$$\frac{dP_{R}}{dV} \;=\; \tfrac{3}{2}\rho S C_{D0} V^{2} \;-\; \frac{2 K W^{2}}{\rho S}\,V^{-2}$$

Set to zero:

$$\tfrac{3}{2}\rho S C_{D0} V^{2} \;=\; \frac{2 K W^{2}}{\rho S}\,V^{-2}$$

Multiply by V²:

$$\tfrac{3}{2}\rho S C_{D0} V^{4} \;=\; \frac{2 K W^{2}}{\rho S}$$

$$V^{4} \;=\; \frac{4 K W^{2}}{3 C_{D0}\,(\rho S)^{2}}$$

$$\boxed{\;V_{P_{\min}} \;=\; \left(\frac{4 K W^{2}}{3 C_{D0}\,\rho^{2} S^{2}}\right)^{\!1/4} \;=\; \frac{V_{D_{\min}}}{\sqrt[4]{3}}\;}$$

**Corresponding CL:** Using the same substitution technique:

$$C_{L,P_{\min}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{3 C_{D0}}{K}}$$

**Crucial numerical check:**

$$\frac{V_{P_{\min}}}{V_{D_{\min}}} \;=\; \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt[4]{3}}\right) \;\approx\; \frac{1}{1.3161} \;\approx\; 0.7598$$

So the **minimum-power speed is about 76 % of the minimum-drag speed** — a classic exam result.

## 3.3 Derivation of Maximum Lift-to-Drag Ratio

The lift-to-drag ratio is:

$$\frac{L}{D} \;=\; \frac{C_{L}}{C_{D0} + K C_{L}^{2}}$$

Let **x = C_L**. Then:

$$f(x) \;=\; \frac{x}{C_{D0} + K x^{2}}$$

Maximum occurs where df/dx = 0:

$$\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{x}{C_{D0}+Kx^{2}}\right) \;=\; \frac{(C_{D0}+Kx^{2}) - x(2Kx)}{(C_{D0}+Kx^{2})^{2}} \;=\; \frac{C_{D0} - Kx^{2}}{(C_{D0}+Kx^{2})^{2}} \;=\; 0$$

Numerator = 0:

$$C_{D0} - K x^{2} \;=\; 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad x^{2} \;=\; \frac{C_{D0}}{K} \quad\Rightarrow\quad x \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{C_{D0}}{K}}$$

Thus **maximum L/D occurs at the minimum-drag CL** — a deep result: the most aerodynamically *efficient* speed is the one that *minimises drag* (and hence required thrust). Substitute x² = C_D0/K:

$$C_{D,\text{at max L/D}} \;=\; C_{D0} + K \cdot \frac{C_{D0}}{K} \;=\; 2 C_{D0}$$

$$\boxed{\;\left(\frac{L}{D}\right)_{\max} \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{C_{D0}/K}}{2 C_{D0}} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\sqrt{K C_{D0}}}\;}$$

## 3.4 Derivation of Maximum-Range Speed (Propeller Aircraft)

The Breguet range equation for a propeller aircraft with thrust-specific fuel consumption c_T (per second, inverse seconds) is:

$$R \;=\; \frac{V}{c_{T}}\,\frac{C_{L}}{C_{D}}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{W_{i}}{W_{f}}\right)$$

For fixed fuel fraction, maximizing R is equivalent to maximizing **V·(C_L/C_D) = V·(L/D)**. Using the substitution L = ½ρV²S C_L = W, we have C_L = 2W/(ρV²S), hence:

$$V \cdot \frac{L}{D} \;=\; V \cdot \frac{C_{L}}{C_{D}} \;=\; \frac{2W}{\rho V S\,C_{D}}$$

Minimise C_D at fixed V: this is again the C_L = √(C_D0/K) condition, which fixes the **C_L** but does *not* fix the V. So the analysis must be more careful — we are maximising V·C_L/C_D over V (treating C_L as the design variable, with V adjusting to keep L = W).

Equivalently, we minimise **C_D·C_L⁻¹** = C_D0/C_L + K C_L. Differentiating with respect to C_L and setting to zero:

$$-\frac{C_{D0}}{C_{L}^{2}} + K \;=\; 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad C_{L} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{C_{D0}}{K}}$$

This is *the same* as the minimum-drag CL! Therefore for a propeller, the maximum range condition is **C_L = √(C_D0/K)** — exactly the minimum-drag condition. But wait — the speed corresponding to that C_L depends on altitude and weight. The V that achieves max range is **V = V_(Dmin)** only when the fuel-fraction factor is exactly accounted for. The classical Anderson result is:

$$V_{R_{\max}} \;=\; \sqrt{3}\;V_{D_{\min}} \quad\text{(jet)}$$

$$V_{R_{\max}} \;=\; V_{D_{\min}} \quad\text{(propeller, with c_T constant)}$$

For the **jet case**, the Breguet range equation is **R = (2/c)·√(2/(ρS))·(C_L^(1/2)/C_D)·(W_i^(1/2) − W_f^(1/2))**. Maximising C_L^(1/2)/C_D over C_L:

$$\frac{d}{dC_{L}}\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{C_{L}}}{C_{D0}+KC_{L}^{2}}\right) \;=\; 0$$

After algebra, this yields **C_L = √(3 C_D0/K)** — the *minimum-power* CL! For a jet, the max-range speed is at C_L = √(3 C_D0/K) → V_R = √3 · V_(Dmin). For a propeller, the max-range speed is at C_L = √(C_D0/K) → V_R = V_(Dmin).

## 3.5 Derivation of Specific Endurance and Specific Range

**Specific endurance** (for jets, with c constant): time per unit fuel:

$$E_{s} \;=\; \frac{1}{c}\,\frac{C_{L}}{C_{D}} \cdot \frac{1}{V} \;\propto\; \frac{1}{C_{D}\,V}$$

Minimise C_D·V. At constant CL, V is fixed; so minimise C_D → C_L = √(C_D0/K). But the *E_s* optimum is **max endurance, not max range**: it minimises **C_D·V**, which gives C_L = √(3 C_D0/K) — the minimum-power CL. Therefore:

$$\boxed{\;V_{E_{\max}} \;=\; V_{P_{\min}} \quad\text{(maximum endurance)}\;}$$

**Specific range** (jet, distance per unit fuel): R_s = V/(c·T_R) ∝ V/C_D ∝ V/(C_D0+KC_L²) with C_L ∝ 1/V². Substituting:

$$R_{s}(V) \;\propto\; \frac{V}{C_{D0} + K(2W/(\rho SV^{2}))^{2}}$$

Differentiate w.r.t. V and set to zero → optimum is at **C_L = √(3 C_D0/K)** → **V = √3·V_(Dmin)** for the jet. This confirms the result in §3.4.

## 3.6 Effect of Altitude on Straight-and-Level Performance

The standard atmosphere tells us ρ(h) decreases with altitude. From the CL–V relation:

$$V(h) \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2W}{\rho(h)\,S\,C_{L}}}$$

→ At the **same CL**, V must *increase* as ρ decreases. So altitude speeds shift rightward on the T_R–V diagram.

For the minimum-drag speed:

$$V_{D_{\min}}(h) \;\propto\; \frac{1}{\sqrt{\rho(h)}} \quad\Rightarrow\quad V_{D_{\min}} \text{ increases with } h$$

**True airspeed increases with altitude, but indicated airspeed (IAS) — proportional to √(ρ)·V — is approximately constant at the same CL.** KTU exam tip: always check whether the question gives **TAS, EAS, or IAS**.

At the **service ceiling**, T_R(V_(Dmin)) = T_A at maximum throttle. Above this altitude, straight and level flight is impossible.

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style)

**Given:**
- W = 50 000 N, S = 30 m², C_D0 = 0.02, K = 0.05, h = 5 000 m (ρ = 0.7364 kg/m³).

**Find:** V_(Dmin), V_(Pmin), (L/D)_max.

**Solution:**

**(a) V_(Dmin):**

$$V_{D_{\min}} = \sqrt{\frac{2W}{\rho S}}\left(\frac{K}{C_{D0}}\right)^{1/4} = \sqrt{\frac{2(50000)}{0.7364(30)}}\left(\frac{0.05}{0.02}\right)^{1/4}$$

$$\sqrt{\frac{100000}{22.092}} = \sqrt{4526.2} = 67.28 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\left(\frac{0.05}{0.02}\right)^{1/4} = (2.5)^{0.25} = 1.2574$$

$$V_{D_{\min}} = 67.28 \times 1.2574 = 84.59 \text{ m/s}$$

**(b) V_(Pmin):**

$$V_{P_{\min}} = \frac{V_{D_{\min}}}{\sqrt[4]{3}} = \frac{84.59}{1.3161} = 64.28 \text{ m/s}$$

**(c) (L/D)_max:**

$$\left(\frac{L}{D}\right)_{\max} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{K C_{D0}}} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{0.05 \times 0.02}} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{0.001}} = \frac{1}{0.0632} = 15.81$$

These are the three classical S&L performance numbers that almost every KTU Part B question demands.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Free-Body & Force-Equilibrium Block Diagram

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the **functional architecture** of straight-and-level flight performance: the inputs (atmospheric + aircraft + flight-condition), the equilibrium constraints, the derived quantities, and the five performance speeds.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INP["INPUTS W, S, h, CD0, K, TA model"] --> EQ1["EQUILIBRIUM L = W, T = D"]
    INP --> ATM["STANDARD ATMOSPHERE rho at h"]
    ATM --> CLREQ["Required CL CL = 2W / rho V2 S"]
    EQ1 --> CLREQ
    CLREQ --> DRAG["Drag Polar CD = CD0 + K CL2"]
    DRAG --> TR["Thrust Required TR = 0.5 rho S V2 CD"]
    DRAG --> PR["Power Required PR = TR times V"]
    TR --> SPEEDS["FIVE PERFORMANCE SPEEDS"]
    PR --> SPEEDS
    SPEEDS --> S1["V_Dmin = min of TR curve, CL = sqrt CD0 over K"]
    SPEEDS --> S2["V_Pmin = min of PR curve, CL = sqrt 3 CD0 over K"]
    SPEEDS --> S3["V_Emax = V_Pmin, max endurance"]
    SPEEDS --> S4["V_Rmax = sqrt 3 times V_Dmin jet, = V_Dmin prop"]
    SPEEDS --> S5["V_max = upper intersection of TR and TA"]
    INP --> TA["THRUST AVAILABLE TA h V"]
    TA --> MAXV["SOLVE TR V = TA V for V_max"]
    S1 --> OUT["OUTPUTS Vmin, Vmin P, LDmax, Vmax, R, E"]
    S2 --> OUT
    S3 --> OUT
    S4 --> OUT
    S5 --> OUT
    MAXV --> OUT
    classDef inp fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    classDef eq fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    classDef spd fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#ad1457,color:#000
    class INP inp
    class EQ1,ATM,CLREQ,DRAG,TR,PR,TA,MAXV eq
    class SPEEDS,S1,S2,S3,S4,S5 spd
    class OUT out
```

## 4.2 Concept Map — From Drag Polar to Performance Speeds

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Drag Polar CD = CD0 + K CL2"] --> B["Set dTR/dV = 0"]
    A --> C["Set dPR/dV = 0"]
    B --> D["CL_Dmin = sqrt CD0 over K"]
    C --> E["CL_Pmin = sqrt 3 CD0 over K"]
    D --> F["V_Dmin = V at CL_Dmin"]
    E --> G["V_Pmin = V at CL_Pmin"]
    D --> H["L over D max = 1 over 2 sqrt K CD0"]
    E --> I["Max Endurance"]
    F --> J["Max Range jet = sqrt 3 V_Dmin"]
    F --> K["Max Range prop = V_Dmin"]
    classDef polar fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000
    classDef deriv fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    class A polar
    class B,C,D,E deriv
    class F,G,H,I,J,K result
```

## 4.3 T_R–V and P_R–V Curve Topology (Text Schematic)

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below is a **functional/architectural map** of how the T_R curve and P_R curve are constructed from their two contributing terms. The "shape" produced is the classical **U-curve** that every KTU examiner draws on the answer sheet.

```
Thrust Required (or Power Required) vs V
|
|     \                              /
|      \   Parasitic term (∝V^2)    /  Induced term (∝1/V^2)
|       \                          /
|  P_R:  \                        /    <-- Sum is the U-curve
|         \____                ____/
|              \____        ____/
|                   \______/
|                          *  V_Pmin (P_R minimum)
|                  *  V_Dmin (T_R minimum)
+-------------------------------------------------> V
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **T_R curve** (parasitic ∝ V² + induced ∝ 1/V²) is U-shaped; its minimum is at **V_(Dmin)**.
- The **P_R curve** (parasitic ∝ V³ + induced ∝ 1/V) is also U-shaped but its minimum is **to the left** of V_(Dmin) at **V_(Pmin) ≈ 0.76 V_(Dmin)**.
- The **T_A curve** is generally *decreasing* with V. The right-hand intersection of T_R and T_A gives **V_max**.

## 4.4 Algorithm for Solving a Typical KTU 14-Mark Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["Start: Read W, S, h, CD0, K, TA model"] --> S1["Step 1: Compute rho at h from ISA"]
    S1 --> S2["Step 2: Compute CL at given V: CL = 2W / rho V2 S"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: Compute CD = CD0 + K CL2"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4: Compute D = 0.5 rho V2 S CD = TR"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5: Compute PR = TR times V"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 6: Compute L over D = 1 over CD, multiply by CL"]
    S6 --> S7["Step 7: Compare TR and TA to determine if flight is feasible"]
    S7 --> S8["Step 8: If asked for Vmax, solve TR V = TA V numerically"]
    S8 --> S9["End: Tabulate TR, PR, L over D, Vmax, V_Dmin, V_Pmin"]
    classDef step fill:#fffde7,stroke:#f9a825,color:#000
    class S0,S1,S2,S3,S4,S5,S6,S7,S8,S9 step
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Most Common Pitfalls in S&L Flight Questions**
>
> 1. **Confusing TAS with IAS.** At altitude h > 0, ρ < 1.225 kg/m³, and the *true* airspeed differs from indicated. Always use ρ(h) in the formulas.
> 2. **Mixing up V_(Dmin) and V_(Pmin).** V_(Dmin) is for *minimum drag (thrust)*; V_(Pmin) is for *minimum power*. They differ by a factor of ∜3 ≈ 1.316. Students routinely swap the two — a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> 3. **Forgetting that (L/D)_max and V_(Dmin) correspond to the same C_L = √(C_D0/K).** This is a single most-tested fact.
> 4. **Unit errors on K and C_D0** — these are *dimensionless*, but the induced-drag factor sometimes appears as K = 1/(πeAR) or as an empirical number. Read the question carefully.
> 5. **Failing to state the equilibrium conditions L = W and T = D explicitly** in the first line of a derivation. Examiners allocate 1–2 marks just for the correct force-balance statement.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Conceptual — Definition) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Q:** Define straight and level flight. State the two force-equilibrium conditions that must be satisfied in this regime.

**Model Answer:**

Straight and level flight is a steady, unaccelerated flight regime in which the aircraft's velocity vector is **constant in both magnitude and direction**. The flight path is horizontal (flight-path angle γ = 0) and the aircraft does not turn (turn rate ψ̇ = 0).

The two force-equilibrium conditions are:

1. **Vertical equilibrium:** Lift equals weight,
   $$L = W$$
2. **Horizontal equilibrium:** Thrust equals drag,
   $$T = D$$

These two equations, together with the standard lift and drag equations, form the foundation of all straight-and-level performance analysis. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2 (Conceptual — Performance Speeds) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Distinguish between V_(Dmin) and V_(Pmin). What is the approximate numerical ratio V_(Pmin) / V_(Dmin)?

**Model Answer:**

| Quantity | V_(Dmin) | V_(Pmin) |
|:---------|:---------|:---------|
| Definition | Speed at which **thrust required T_R is minimum** | Speed at which **power required P_R is minimum** |
| Condition | dT_R / dV = 0 | dP_R / dV = 0 |
| Corresponding C_L | √(C_D0 / K) | √(3 C_D0 / K) |
| Application | Max L/D, max range (propeller) | Max endurance, max range (jet) |

The ratio is:

$$\frac{V_{P_{\min}}}{V_{D_{\min}}} \;=\; \frac{1}{\sqrt[4]{3}} \;\approx\; 0.76$$

(3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]`

> An aircraft has the following data: W = 60 000 N, wing area S = 30 m², C_D0 = 0.025, K = 0.045. The aircraft is flying straight and level at sea level (ρ = 1.225 kg/m³).
>
> **(a)** Derive the expression for thrust required as a function of velocity. Compute the thrust required at V = 80 m/s. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Find the speed for minimum thrust required, the corresponding C_L, and the maximum lift-to-drag ratio. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to A(a):

From L = W, the required lift coefficient is:

$$C_{L} \;=\; \frac{2W}{\rho V^{2} S} \;=\; \frac{2(60000)}{1.225\,V^{2}\,(30)} \;=\; \frac{3265.3}{V^{2}}$$

The drag polar is:

$$C_{D} \;=\; C_{D0} + K C_{L}^{2} \;=\; 0.025 + 0.045\,C_{L}^{2}$$

Therefore:

$$T_{R} \;=\; D \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S\,C_{D} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(30)V^{2}\!\left(0.025 + 0.045\,C_{L}^{2}\right)$$

$$T_{R} \;=\; 18.375\,V^{2}\!\left(0.025 + 0.045\,C_{L}^{2}\right) \;\text{N}$$

Substituting C_L = 3265.3/V²:

$$T_{R} \;=\; 18.375\,V^{2}\!\left(0.025 + 0.045 \cdot \frac{3265.3^{2}}{V^{4}}\right) \;=\; 0.4594\,V^{2} + \frac{8.822\times 10^{9}}{V^{2}}$$

Or, in the canonical form:

$$\boxed{\;T_{R} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\rho S C_{D0}\,V^{2} \;+\; \frac{2 K W^{2}}{\rho S\,V^{2}}\;}$$

Numerically at V = 80 m/s:

$$T_{R} \;=\; 0.4594(80)^{2} + \frac{8.822\times 10^{9}}{(80)^{2}} \;=\; 2940.2 + 1\,378\,506 \;=\; 1\,381\,446 \;\text{N}$$

**Sanity check:** This is unphysically large because at V = 80 m/s the C_L is very small (~0.51), placing the aircraft far from the min-drag point. The induced-drag term dominates — this is *slow flight* relative to the design point.

Wait — recompute more carefully. At V = 80 m/s:

$$C_{L} = 3265.3 / 6400 = 0.5102$$

$$C_{D} = 0.025 + 0.045(0.5102)^{2} = 0.025 + 0.01171 = 0.0367$$

$$D = 0.5(1.225)(80)^{2}(30)(0.0367) = 4317 \;\text{N}$$

So T_R ≈ **4317 N** at V = 80 m/s. The induced-drag-term discrepancy above came from algebraic slip; the correct numerical value is **T_R(80 m/s) ≈ 4317 N**.

(7 marks awarded for: 2 marks for CL expression, 2 marks for drag polar, 2 marks for T_R expression, 1 mark for correct numerical answer.)

#### Model Solution to A(b):

The minimum-thrust condition is dT_R/dV = 0, which gives:

$$C_{L,D_{\min}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{C_{D0}}{K}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{0.025}{0.045}} \;=\; \sqrt{0.5556} \;=\; 0.7454$$

Substituting into the CL–V relation:

$$V_{D_{\min}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2W}{\rho S C_{L}}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2(60000)}{1.225 \times 30 \times 0.7454}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{120000}{27.39}} \;=\; \sqrt{4381.5} \;=\; 66.19 \;\text{m/s}$$

Maximum L/D ratio:

$$\left(\frac{L}{D}\right)_{\max} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\sqrt{K C_{D0}}} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\sqrt{0.045 \times 0.025}} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\sqrt{0.001125}} \;=\; \frac{1}{0.06708} \;=\; 14.91$$

(7 marks awarded for: 2 marks for CL, 3 marks for V_(Dmin), 2 marks for (L/D)_max.)

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]`

> A propeller-driven aircraft weighing W = 80 000 N cruises at altitude h = 4000 m (ρ = 0.8194 kg/m³) with a drag polar C_D = 0.018 + 0.04 C_L² and wing area S = 40 m².
>
> **(a)** Find the speed for maximum endurance and the speed for maximum range, stating the relevant CL values. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Compute the power required at the maximum-endurance speed and verify the relation P_R,min = (2/3)·T_R,min·V_(Dmin). (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to B(a):

**Maximum-endurance speed** corresponds to minimum power required, with:

$$C_{L,P_{\min}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{3 C_{D0}}{K}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{3 \times 0.018}{0.04}} \;=\; \sqrt{1.35} \;=\; 1.1619$$

$$V_{P_{\min}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2W}{\rho S C_{L,P_{\min}}}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2(80000)}{0.8194 \times 40 \times 1.1619}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{160000}{38.08}} \;=\; \sqrt{4201.7} \;=\; 64.82 \;\text{m/s}$$

**Maximum-range speed** for a propeller-driven aircraft (with c_T constant) corresponds to the minimum-drag condition:

$$C_{L,D_{\min}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{C_{D0}}{K}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{0.018}{0.04}} \;=\; \sqrt{0.45} \;=\; 0.6708$$

$$V_{R_{\max}} \;=\; V_{D_{\min}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2W}{\rho S C_{L,D_{\min}}}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{160000}{0.8194 \times 40 \times 0.6708}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{160000}{21.99}} \;=\; \sqrt{7276.0} \;=\; 85.30 \;\text{m/s}$$

(7 marks: 2 marks each for the two CL values, 1.5 marks each for the two speeds.)

#### Model Solution to B(b):

**Power required at V_(Pmin):**

$$P_{R,\min} \;=\; T_{R}\,V \quad \text{at} \quad V = V_{P_{\min}}$$

First compute T_R at V_(Pmin):

$$C_{D} \;=\; C_{D0} + K C_{L,P_{\min}}^{2} \;=\; 0.018 + 0.04(1.1619)^{2} \;=\; 0.018 + 0.054 \;=\; 0.0720$$

$$T_{R} \;=\; 0.5 \rho V^{2} S C_{D} \;=\; 0.5(0.8194)(64.82)^{2}(40)(0.0720) \;=\; 4957 \;\text{N}$$

$$P_{R,\min} \;=\; T_{R} \cdot V_{P_{\min}} \;=\; 4957 \times 64.82 \;=\; 321\,313 \;\text{W} \;\approx\; 321.3 \;\text{kW}$$

**Verification of the relation:**

First, T_R,min = T_R at V_(Dmin) (i.e., at V = 85.30 m/s, C_L = 0.6708):

$$C_{D,\text{at }V_{D_{\min}}} \;=\; 0.018 + 0.04(0.6708)^{2} \;=\; 0.018 + 0.018 \;=\; 0.036 \;=\; 2 C_{D0}$$

$$T_{R,\min} \;=\; 0.5(0.8194)(85.30)^{2}(40)(0.036) \;=\; 4294 \;\text{N}$$

Then check:

$$\frac{2}{3}\,T_{R,\min}\,V_{D_{\min}} \;=\; \frac{2}{3}(4294)(85.30) \;=\; 244\,196 \;\text{W}$$

This does **not** equal P_R,min = 321 313 W, so the relation stated in the question is **incorrect** in general. The correct relationship is:

$$P_{R,\min} \;=\; T_{R}(V_{P_{\min}})\,V_{P_{\min}}$$

and the ratio of (P_R,min) to (T_R,min · V_(Dmin)) depends on the specific aircraft parameters. **Students should be careful with formula mnemonics — always re-derive for the given problem.**

(7 marks: 3 marks for P_R computation, 2 marks for the relation check, 2 marks for the critical-evaluation comment.)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-revision checklist for the KTU 2024 S&L Flight topic.**

- [ ] **Equilibrium conditions** L = W and T = D are the *first* line of any solution.
- [ ] **Drag polar** C_D = C_D0 + K C_L² is *the* central equation.
- [ ] **Induced drag factor** K = 1 / (π·e·AR), with AR = b² / S.
- [ ] **C_L at min drag** = √(C_D0 / K); **C_L at min power** = √(3 C_D0 / K).
- [ ] **(L/D)_max** = 1 / (2√(K C_D0)), and this is achieved *at the same C_L as min drag*.
- [ ] **V_(Dmin) : V_(Pmin) ratio = ∜3 : 1 ≈ 1.316 : 1**, hence V_(Pmin) ≈ 0.76 V_(Dmin).
- [ ] **T_R curve is U-shaped** in V (parasitic ∝ V² + induced ∝ 1/V²).
- [ ] **P_R curve is U-shaped** in V (parasitic ∝ V³ + induced ∝ 1/V).
- [ ] **V_max is found by intersection** of T_R(V) and T_A(V); solve numerically if T_A is not constant.
- [ ] **Altitude effect:** V_(Dmin) and V_(Pmin) both *increase* as ρ *decreases* (i.e., as h increases).
- [ ] **Max endurance** = V_(Pmin) for *both* jets and propellers.
- [ ] **Max range**: jet → V = √3 · V_(Dmin); propeller → V = V_(Dmin) (with c_T constant).
- [ ] **Specific range** (jet) ∝ V / C_D; **specific endurance** (jet) ∝ 1 / (C_D·V).
- [ ] **Units:** W in N, ρ in kg/m³, V in m/s, S in m², T_R in N, P_R in W (or kW).
- [ ] **Always state the value of g = 9.81 m/s²** when W is converted to/from mass.
- [ ] **KTU pitfall:** at altitude, *indicated* airspeed (IAS) ≠ true airspeed (TAS). Use ρ(h) in the equations.

> **Final KTU tip:** When you see a 14-mark Part B question on this topic, the hidden sub-mark scheme is almost always **(2 marks force balance) + (4 marks drag polar substitution) + (4 marks differentiation and V_(Dmin)) + (2 marks (L/D)_max) + (2 marks numerical verification)**. If your answer hits those milestones, full marks follow.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Required and Power Available Graphs for Propeller and Jet Aircraft
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET753 - AEROSPACE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Power Required and Power Available Graphs for Propeller and Jet Aircraft

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of steady, level, unaccelerated flight, every aircraft must satisfy Newton's Second Law in the flight direction. Since net acceleration is zero, **Thrust (T)** produced by the propulsion system must exactly equal the **Aerodynamic Drag (D)** opposing the motion. The **Power Required (PR)** is defined as the mechanical power the engine–propulsion combination must deliver just to keep the aircraft flying straight and level at a given velocity and altitude.

$$P_R = D \cdot V$$

The **Power Available (PA)** is defined as the useful propulsive power actually delivered by the propulsion system to the airframe at a given flight condition. It is intrinsically tied to the engine type — a piston-prop, turboprop, turbojet, turbofan, or ramjet — and to altitude.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEMET753 – Module 4):**
> The classical KTU treatment of flight performance requires plotting both $P_R(V)$ and $P_A(V)$ on the same velocity axis. The intersections of the two curves define the **minimum controllable airspeed**, **maximum level-flight speed**, and the **absolute and service ceilings** of the aircraft. The shape of $P_A(V)$ differs dramatically between propeller-driven and jet-driven aircraft — this is the central theme of this module.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Cycling Uphill vs. Downhill:**
> Imagine riding a bicycle on a flat road. To keep a constant speed, your legs must push the pedals at exactly the rate needed to overcome **air resistance** (which grows as the cube of speed). That effort is the *Power Required*. The *Power Available* is the maximum mechanical effort your legs (the "engine") can deliver at each speed.
> - When $P_A > P_R$, you accelerate.
> - When $P_A = P_R$, you cruise.
> - When $P_A < P_R$, you slow down.
> A **propeller bicycle** would have roughly constant pedal power at all speeds (like a geared hub motor), while a **jet bicycle** would deliver thrust that *decreases* with speed (like a rocket nozzle losing efficiency as the exit velocity mismatches ambient).

**Key physical constants and standard metrics** used throughout this module:
- Sea-level air density: $\rho_0 = 1.225 \, \mathbf{kg/m^3}$
- Standard temperature at sea level: $T_0 = 288.15 \, \mathbf{K}$
- Speed of sound at sea level: $a_0 = 340.3 \, \mathbf{m/s}$
- Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \, \mathbf{m/s^2}$

> [!TIP]
> **Why is this topic board-exam critical?**
> A typical KTU question on this topic is a 14-mark problem asking the student to: (i) derive $P_R$ from drag polar, (ii) find $V_{min}$ and $V_{max}$ at a given altitude from the intersection of $P_R$ and $P_A$ curves, and (iii) comment on the shape of the curves for propeller vs jet aircraft.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

### 2.1.1 The Power Required Curve — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1: Lift–Weight equilibrium.** In level flight, the lift equals the weight:

$$L = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_L = W \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_L = \frac{2W}{\rho V^2 S}$$

**Step 2: Express drag via the drag polar.** For a clean aircraft at moderate angles of attack, the parabolic drag polar holds:

$$C_D = C_{D0} + K C_L^2$$

where $C_{D0}$ is the zero-lift drag coefficient (parasite) and $K = \dfrac{1}{\pi \, A \, e}$ is the induced drag factor.

**Step 3: Substitute $C_L$ from Step 1 into Step 2:**

$$C_D = C_{D0} + K \left( \frac{2W}{\rho V^2 S} \right)^2 = C_{D0} + \frac{4 K W^2}{\rho^2 V^4 S^2}$$

**Step 4: Drag magnitude:**

$$D = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S \left[ C_{D0} + \frac{4 K W^2}{\rho^2 V^4 S^2} \right] = \underbrace{\frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_{D0}}_{\text{Parasite Drag (}D_0\text{)}} + \underbrace{\frac{2 K W^2}{\rho V^2 S}}_{\text{Induced Drag (}D_i\text{)}}$$

**Step 5: Power Required** = Drag × Velocity:

$$\boxed{\,P_R(V) = \underbrace{\frac{1}{2}\rho V^3 S C_{D0}}_{\text{Parasite Power}} + \underbrace{\frac{2 K W^2}{\rho V S}}_{\text{Induced Power}}\,}$$

This is the famous **"cubic-plus-inverse"** power curve — *cubic in V at high speed* (parasite-dominated) and *inversely proportional to V at low speed* (induced-dominated). It has a unique minimum.

**Step 6: Velocity for Minimum Power Required ($V_{mp}$).** Setting $\dfrac{dP_R}{dV} = 0$:

$$V_{mp} = \left( \frac{2 K W^2}{\rho^2 S^2 C_{D0}} \right)^{1/4} = V_{\infty} \cdot \sqrt[4]{\frac{1}{3}}$$

Equivalently, $V_{mp} = V_{L/D} / \sqrt[4]{3} \approx 0.759 \cdot V_{L/D}$.

### 2.1.2 The Power Available Curve — Engine Dependent

#### (A) Propeller-Driven Aircraft (Piston or Turboprop)

The engine delivers a **roughly constant shaft power** to the propeller. The propeller converts this into thrust with a velocity-dependent efficiency $\eta_p(V)$. The *useful* power available to the airframe is therefore:

$$P_{A,\text{prop}} = \eta_p(V) \cdot P_{\text{shaft}}(h)$$

For a first-order KTU-level analysis, propeller efficiency is assumed approximately constant (or weakly varying) across the operating envelope:

$$P_{A,\text{prop}} \approx \text{const}(h)$$

This gives an **almost horizontal line** on a $P$–$V$ diagram.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why horizontal?** A piston engine at constant throttle delivers constant brake power (independent of airspeed). A turboprop also maintains nearly constant shaft power with speed (small reduction due to altitude and intake losses). Hence $P_A \approx$ const.

#### (B) Jet Aircraft (Turbojet / Turbofan / Ramjet)

A jet engine produces **thrust**, not power directly. The *power available* is the rate at which the engine does work on the aircraft:
