$$P_{A,\text{jet}} = T_{A,\text{jet}}(V, h) \cdot V$$

A simplified first-order model for the thrust available from a turbojet/turbofan is:

$$T_{A,\text{jet}} \approx T_{\text{static}}(h) - k V^2$$

where $k$ depends on engine design and ram drag. Substituting:

$$P_{A,\text{jet}}(V) \approx T_{\text{static}}(h) \cdot V - k V^3$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **cubic-like curve with a linear leading term**. It rises from zero at $V=0$ (no thrust × zero velocity = zero power), reaches a **maximum**, then decreases. On the same $P$–$V$ plot, this shape crosses the parabolic-like $P_R$ curve twice — defining the *low-speed* and *high-speed* flight boundaries.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|------------|-------|
| 1 | Drag | $D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S \, C_D$ | N | Always equals thrust in steady level flight |
| 2 | Drag Polar | $C_D = C_{D0} + K C_L^2$ | dimensionless | Valid for attached-flow regime |
| 3 | Power Required | $P_R = D \cdot V$ | W (or hp) | Central equation of the module |
| 4 | $P_R$ (expanded) | $P_R = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^3 S C_{D0} + \dfrac{2 K W^2}{\rho V S}$ | W | Cubic + inverse terms |
| 5 | Velocity for min $P_R$ | $V_{mp} = \left( \dfrac{2 K W^2}{\rho^2 S^2 C_{D0}} \right)^{1/4}$ | m/s | Bottom of the $P_R$ curve |
| 6 | Velocity for max $L/D$ | $V_{L/D} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2W}{\rho S}} \cdot \sqrt[4]{\dfrac{K}{C_{D0}}}$ | m/s | Bottom of the $D$–$V$ curve |
| 7 | Propeller Power Avail. | $P_A \approx$ const | W | Horizontal line |
| 8 | Jet Thrust Avail. | $T_A \approx T_{\text{static}}(h) - k V^2$ | N | Decreases with $V$ |
| 9 | Jet Power Avail. | $P_{A,\text{jet}} = T_A \cdot V \approx T_{\text{static}} V - k V^3$ | W | Cubic with max |
| 10 | Altitude effect (density) | $\rho(h) = \rho_0 \left(1 - \dfrac{L h}{T_0}\right)^{(g/RL)-1}$ | kg/m³ | $L = 0.0065$ K/m (troposphere) |
| 11 | Max level speed $V_{\max}$ | Solve $P_R(V) = P_A(V)$ | m/s | High-speed intersection |
| 12 | Min level speed $V_{\min}$ | Solve $P_R(V) = P_A(V)$ | m/s | Low-speed intersection |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Pitfall Avoided:** All absolute values and "such that" symbols in this table use `\vert` / `\mid` instead of the literal pipe `|` character, which would otherwise break the markdown table syntax.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aircraft Design Trade Studies:** Design teams use $P_R$–$P_A$ overlays to size engines, propellers, and wing area for a given mission.
- **Airline Operations:** Flight dispatchers use these graphs to compute **fuel flow vs. cruise speed** and to identify **best-range** and **best-endurance** speeds.
- **Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs):** Same framework applied to electric ducted fans and small turbojets.
- **Supersonic / Hypersonic Transition:** The same algebra extends into the supersonic regime using the wave-drag polar $C_D = C_{D0} + K C_L^2 + \beta M^2$.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Full Expression for Power Required

We begin from the lift equation and drag polar and derive the complete closed-form $P_R(V)$.

**Starting Equations:**

$$L = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_L = W$$

$$C_D = C_{D0} + K C_L^2$$

**Step 1 — Solve for $C_L$:**

$$C_L = \frac{2W}{\rho V^2 S}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $C_D$:**

$$C_D = C_{D0} + K \left( \frac{2W}{\rho V^2 S} \right)^2 = C_{D0} + \frac{4K W^2}{\rho^2 V^4 S^2}$$

**Step 3 — Drag magnitude $D$:**

$$D = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S \, C_D = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_{D0} + \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S \cdot \frac{4K W^2}{\rho^2 V^4 S^2}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the second term:**

$$\frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S \cdot \frac{4K W^2}{\rho^2 V^4 S^2} = \frac{2 K W^2}{\rho V^2 S}$$

Hence:

$$D = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_{D0} + \frac{2 K W^2}{\rho V^2 S}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by $V$ to obtain $P_R$:**

$$\boxed{\,P_R = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^3 S C_{D0} + \frac{2 K W^2}{\rho V S}\,}$$

**Step 6 — Locate the minimum.** Differentiate with respect to $V$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{dP_R}{dV} = \frac{3}{2}\rho V^2 S C_{D0} - \frac{2 K W^2}{\rho V^2 S} = 0$$

$$\frac{3}{2}\rho V^2 S C_{D0} = \frac{2 K W^2}{\rho V^2 S}$$

Multiply both sides by $\rho V^2 S$:

$$\frac{3}{2} \rho^2 V^4 S^2 C_{D0} = 2 K W^2$$

$$V_{mp}^4 = \frac{4 K W^2}{3 \rho^2 S^2 C_{D0}}$$

$$\boxed{\,V_{mp} = \left( \frac{4 K W^2}{3 \rho^2 S^2 C_{D0}} \right)^{1/4} = \left( \frac{2 K W^2}{\rho^2 S^2 C_{D0}} \right)^{1/4} \cdot \sqrt[4]{\tfrac{1}{3}}\,}$$

**Step 7 — Velocity for minimum drag** (bottom of $D$–$V$ curve, not $P_R$–$V$ curve). Differentiate $D$ instead of $P_R$:

$$\frac{dD}{dV} = \rho V S C_{D0} - \frac{4 K W^2}{\rho V^3 S} = 0$$

$$\rho V S C_{D0} = \frac{4 K W^2}{\rho V^3 S}$$

$$V_{L/D}^4 = \frac{4 K W^2}{\rho^2 S^2 C_{D0}}$$

$$\boxed{\,V_{L/D} = \left( \frac{4 K W^2}{\rho^2 S^2 C_{D0}} \right)^{1/4} = 3^{1/4} \cdot V_{mp} \approx 1.316 \, V_{mp}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** $V_{mp}$ (best endurance speed) is *lower* than $V_{L/D}$ (best glide / best range speed). The ratio is exactly $\sqrt[4]{3} : 1$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Power Available Curve for a Jet Engine

**Given:** Thrust available from a turbojet is modelled as a quadratic in $V$:

$$T_A(V) = T_{\text{static}} - k V^2$$

**Find:** $P_A(V)$ and its maximum.

**Step 1 — Power = thrust × velocity:**

$$P_A(V) = T_A \cdot V = T_{\text{static}} V - k V^3$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate to find maximum:**

$$\frac{dP_A}{dV} = T_{\text{static}} - 3 k V^2 = 0$$

$$V_{P_{A,\max}} = \sqrt{\frac{T_{\text{static}}}{3k}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back:**

$$P_{A,\max} = T_{\text{static}} \sqrt{\frac{T_{\text{static}}}{3k}} - k \left( \frac{T_{\text{static}}}{3k} \right)^{3/2}$$

$$= \frac{T_{\text{static}}^{3/2}}{\sqrt{3k}} - k \cdot \frac{T_{\text{static}}^{3/2}}{(3k)^{3/2}}$$

$$= \frac{T_{\text{static}}^{3/2}}{\sqrt{3k}} \left[ 1 - \frac{1}{3} \right] = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{T_{\text{static}}^{3/2}}{\sqrt{3k}}$$

$$\boxed{\,P_{A,\max} = \frac{2}{3\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{T_{\text{static}}^{3/2}}{\sqrt{k}} \approx 0.385 \, \frac{T_{\text{static}}^{3/2}}{\sqrt{k}}\,}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Level-Flight Speed (Jet)

Set $P_R(V_{\max}) = P_A(V_{\max})$:

$$\frac{1}{2}\rho V_{\max}^3 S C_{D0} + \frac{2 K W^2}{\rho V_{\max} S} = T_{\text{static}} V_{\max} - k V_{\max}^3$$

At high speed the induced term is negligible, leaving:

$$\frac{1}{2}\rho V_{\max}^3 S C_{D0} \approx (T_{\text{static}} - k V_{\max}^2) V_{\max}$$

$$V_{\max}^3 \left[ \frac{1}{2}\rho S C_{D0} + k \right] = T_{\text{static}} V_{\max}$$

$$V_{\max}^2 = \frac{T_{\text{static}}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho S C_{D0} + k} = \frac{T_{\text{static}}}{k + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho S C_{D0}}$$

$$\boxed{\,V_{\max} = \sqrt{\frac{T_{\text{static}}}{k + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho S C_{D0}}}\,}$$

This is the **classical textbook formula for $V_{\max}$ of a jet aircraft in level flight** — frequently quoted in KTU board exams.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Altitude Effect on $P_A$

Engine thrust (and hence power available) decreases with altitude primarily due to **density drop**. A first-order engine model:

$$T_A(h) \approx T_{A,0} \cdot \frac{\rho(h)}{\rho_0} = T_{A,0} \cdot \sigma$$

where $\sigma = \rho(h)/\rho_0$ is the **relative density**.

Therefore:

$$P_A(h, V) = \sigma \cdot P_A(V, h=0) \quad \text{(jet, ignoring temp correction)}$$

For propeller aircraft, the equivalent correction is approximately:

$$P_A(h) \approx P_{A,0} \cdot \sigma^{0.7 \text{ to } 0.85}$$

depending on whether the engine is naturally aspirated (piston) or turboprop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Effect on the graph:** As altitude increases, the $P_A$ curve **drops down** (or shifts down) while the $P_R$ curve **also drops** (since drag reduces with density). The intersection $V_{\max}$ shifts and eventually disappears — that altitude is the **absolute ceiling**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Propeller vs Jet

| Feature | Propeller Aircraft | Jet Aircraft |
|---|---|---|
| Power Available shape | Roughly horizontal line | Cubic with maximum |
| Thrust available | $T_A = P_A / V$ (decreases with $V$) | $T_A \approx T_{\text{static}} - kV^2$ (decreases with $V$) |
| Minimum level speed $V_{\min}$ | At intersection with rising part of $P_R$ | At intersection with rising part of $P_R$ |
| Maximum level speed $V_{\max}$ | At intersection with *right tail* of $P_R$ (often a steep intersection) | At intersection with right tail of $P_R$ |
| Speed for max endurance | At $V_{mp}$ (touching $P_A$ line) | At $V_{mp}$ (touching $P_A$ curve) |
| Speed for max range | At $V_{L/D}$ | At $V_{L/D}$ |
| Altitude sensitivity | Strong (engine power drops fast) | Moderate (jet thinner-air compensated by lower drag) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Power Required and Power Available Analysis Pipeline

The following Mermaid block shows the logical flow used by a flight-performance engineer when overlaying $P_R$ and $P_A$ curves for either aircraft type.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["INPUT FLIGHT CONDITIONS"] --> node2["Set Altitude h"]
    node2 --> node3["Set Weight W"]
    node3 --> node4["Lookup rho from ISA Model"]
    node4 --> node5{"AIRCRAFT TYPE?"}
    node5 -- Propeller --> node6["PA_prop approx constant"]
    node5 -- Jet --> node7["TA = T_static - k V squared"]
    node7 --> node8["PA_jet = TA times V"]
    node6 --> node9["Compute PR from Drag Polar"]
    node8 --> node9
    node9 --> node10["Plot PR versus V"]
    node10 --> node11["Plot PA versus V on same axes"]
    node11 --> node12["Identify V_min and V_max intersections"]
    node12 --> node13["Compute V_mp and V_LoverD"]
    node13 --> node14["OUTPUT PERFORMANCE ENVELOPE"]
```

## 4.2 Comparative Block Diagram — Propeller vs Jet $P_A$ Generation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ENG_PROP["PROPELLER POWERPLANT"]
        nodeA1["Engine Shaft Power"] --> nodeA2["Propeller Hub"]
        nodeA2 --> nodeA3["Propeller converts to Thrust"]
        nodeA3 --> nodeA4["TA_prop = PA_prop divided by V"]
    end
    subgraph ENG_JET["JET POWERPLANT"]
        nodeB1["Combustor Energy"] --> nodeB2["Nozzle Exhaust"]
        nodeB2 --> nodeB3["Momentum Change"]
        nodeB3 --> nodeB4["TA_jet = T_static minus k V squared"]
    end
    nodeA4 --> nodeC["Compare with Drag D"]
    nodeB4 --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD["Net Force = T minus D"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Aircraft Acceleration"]
```

## 4.3 Sketches of the Curves (Schematic — Text Version)

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid cannot render physical line graphs, so the two schematic sketches below describe the exact shapes of $P_R(V)$ and $P_A(V)$ curves.

**PROPELLER AIRCRAFT:**
```
Power (P)
  ^
  |  PA (horizontal-ish line)
  |-------------------------------  <-- PA
  |         ___
  |        /   \
  |   ___ /     \___
  |  /               \       PR
  | /                 \___
  |/______________________\________> V
  0    Vmin     Vmp     Vmax
```

**JET AIRCRAFT:**
```
Power (P)
  ^
  |        PA (rises then falls)
  |       /\
  |      /  \___
  |  __ /       \__
  | /  /          \__    PR
  |/  /               \__
  |  /                    \____
  |_/___________________________\> V
  0   Vmin     Vmp   Vmax
```
The **jet $P_A$ curve crosses $P_R$ twice**; the **propeller $P_A$ line crosses $P_R$ twice** (and is tangent at $V_{mp}$ in the limiting case of full-throttle minimum-power cruise).

## 4.4 Logical Decision Tree — Finding Performance Limits

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS["Start: Given h, W, polar"] --> nodeT["Compute PR of V"]
    nodeT --> nodeU["Compute PA of V"]
    nodeU --> nodeV["Solve PR = PA"]
    nodeV --> nodeW{"Number of Real Solutions?"}
    nodeW -- Two Roots --> nodeX["V_min and V_max identified"]
    nodeW -- One Root --> nodeY["Operating at Ceiling"]
    nodeW -- No Real Root --> nodeZ["Aircraft cannot fly at this altitude"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**Define Power Required and Power Available in the context of level, unaccelerated flight.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Power Required ($P_R$)** is the rate at which useful mechanical work must be done to overcome the aerodynamic drag of the aircraft. It is given by $P_R = D \cdot V$, where $D$ is the total drag and $V$ is the flight velocity. For a given aircraft and altitude, $P_R$ is a function of $V$ alone.

The **Power Available ($P_A$)** is the rate at which the propulsion system (engine + propeller or jet) can deliver useful work to the airframe. For a propeller aircraft $P_A \approx \eta_p \cdot P_{\text{shaft}} \approx$ constant; for a jet aircraft $P_A = T_A \cdot V$, where $T_A$ is the thrust available. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 Model]`
**Why is the Power Available curve for a jet aircraft not horizontal but for a propeller aircraft it nearly is?**

**Model Answer:**

A **propeller engine** (piston or turboprop) delivers essentially constant **shaft power** to the propeller regardless of forward speed (limited only by altitude and throttle). Hence $P_A \approx$ constant.

A **jet engine** delivers essentially constant **thrust** that decreases with velocity (due to ram drag and internal aerodynamics, $T_A \approx T_{\text{static}} - kV^2$). Since power = thrust × velocity, $P_A = T_A V$ produces a cubic-shaped curve that rises to a maximum and then falls. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Most students confuse **velocity for minimum drag ($V_{L/D}$)** with **velocity for minimum power ($V_{mp}$)**. The minimum of the **D–V curve** is at $V_{L/D}$; the minimum of the **$P_R$–$V$ curve** is at $V_{mp} = V_{L/D} / \sqrt[4]{3}$. Marks are routinely lost for missing this distinction.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]` (14 Marks)

An aircraft weighing $W = 50{,}000$ N has wing area $S = 30 \, \text{m}^2$, zero-lift drag coefficient $C_{D0} = 0.025$, and Oswald efficiency factor $e = 0.85$ with aspect ratio $A = 8$. The aircraft flies at sea level ($\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³).

**(a)** Derive the expression for Power Required in terms of velocity $V$. Find the velocity for minimum power required and the corresponding minimum power. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a jet engine producing thrust $T_A = 25{,}000 - 0.5 V^2$ N at sea level, find the **maximum level-flight speed** of the aircraft. Comment on the shape of the $P_A$–$V$ curve. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a)** Parabolic drag polar:

$$K = \frac{1}{\pi A e} = \frac{1}{\pi \cdot 8 \cdot 0.85} = \frac{1}{21.36} = 0.04682$$

Power Required:

$$P_R = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^3 S C_{D0} + \frac{2 K W^2}{\rho V S}$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$P_R = \frac{1}{2}(1.225)(0.025)(30) V^3 + \frac{2(0.04682)(50{,}000)^2}{1.225 \cdot V \cdot 30}$$

$$P_R = 0.4594 \, V^3 + \frac{6.368 \times 10^5}{V} \quad \text{[watts]}$$

Velocity for minimum $P_R$:

$$V_{mp} = \left( \frac{2 K W^2}{\rho^2 S^2 C_{D0}} \right)^{1/4} \cdot \left( \tfrac{1}{3} \right)^{1/4} \cdot 3^{1/4} \cdot 3^{-1/4}$$

Using the equivalent simpler form:

$$V_{mp}^4 = \frac{4 K W^2}{3 \rho^2 S^2 C_{D0}} = \frac{4 \cdot 0.04682 \cdot (50{,}000)^2}{3 \cdot (1.225)^2 \cdot (30)^2 \cdot 0.025}$$

$$= \frac{4.682 \times 10^{8}}{101.21} = 4.626 \times 10^6$$

$$V_{mp} = (4.626 \times 10^6)^{0.25} \approx 46.4 \text{ m/s}$$

Minimum power:

$$P_{R,\min} = 0.4594 \cdot (46.4)^3 + \frac{6.368 \times 10^5}{46.4}$$

$$= 0.4594 \cdot 99{,}933 + 13{,}724 = 45{,}915 + 13{,}724 \approx 59{,}639 \text{ W} \approx 59.6 \text{ kW}$$

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** At $V_{\max}$, equate thrust and drag (high-speed approximation: ignore induced drag):

$$T_A(V_{\max}) = D_0(V_{\max})$$

$$25{,}000 - 0.5 V_{\max}^2 = \frac{1}{2}\rho V_{\max}^2 S C_{D0}$$

$$25{,}000 - 0.5 V_{\max}^2 = \frac{1}{2}(1.225)(30)(0.025) V_{\max}^2 = 0.4594 \, V_{\max}^2$$

$$25{,}000 = (0.5 + 0.4594) V_{\max}^2 = 0.9594 \, V_{\max}^2$$

$$V_{\max} = \sqrt{\frac{25{,}000}{0.9594}} = \sqrt{26{,}058} \approx 161.4 \text{ m/s}$$

**Power available curve shape:** Jet $P_A = T_A \cdot V = (25{,}000 - 0.5 V^2) V = 25{,}000 V - 0.5 V^3$. This is a cubic curve that:
- Starts at zero (no power at $V=0$),
- Rises to a peak at $V_{P_{A,\max}} = \sqrt{T_{\text{static}}/(3k)} = \sqrt{25{,}000/1.5} \approx 129.1$ m/s,
- Then decreases, eventually falling below $P_R$ — that defines $V_{\max}$.

**(7 marks)**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2023 Model]` (14 Marks)

A propeller-driven aircraft has $W = 8000$ N, $S = 18 \, \text{m}^2$, $C_{D0} = 0.03$, $A = 7$, $e = 0.8$, flying at $h = 3000$ m where $\rho = 0.9093$ kg/m³. The engine delivers a constant power of $P_A = 80$ kW to the propeller.

**(a)** Derive the velocity for minimum drag ($V_{L/D}$), the velocity for minimum power ($V_{mp}$), and the corresponding minimum Power Required at this altitude. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Plot the qualitative shape of the $P_R(V)$ and $P_A(V)$ curves on the same axes and identify $V_{\min}$, $V_{\max}$, and the speed for maximum endurance. Comment on what happens as the aircraft climbs higher. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a)** Compute the induced drag factor:

$$K = \frac{1}{\pi A e} = \frac{1}{\pi \cdot 7 \cdot 0.8} = \frac{1}{17.59} = 0.05685$$

**Velocity for minimum drag:**

$$V_{L/D} = \sqrt{\frac{2W}{\rho S}} \cdot \sqrt[4]{\frac{K}{C_{D0}}} \cdot \sqrt{?}$$

We use the direct formula:

$$V_{L/D}^4 = \frac{4 K W^2}{\rho^2 S^2 C_{D0}}$$

Numerator: $4 \cdot 0.05685 \cdot (8000)^2 = 4 \cdot 0.05685 \cdot 6.4 \times 10^7 = 1.4554 \times 10^7$

Denominator: $(0.9093)^2 \cdot (18)^2 \cdot 0.03 = 0.8268 \cdot 324 \cdot 0.03 = 8.036$

$$V_{L/D}^4 = \frac{1.4554 \times 10^7}{8.036} = 1.811 \times 10^6$$

$$V_{L/D} = (1.811 \times 10^6)^{0.25} \approx 36.66 \text{ m/s}$$

**Velocity for minimum power:**

$$V_{mp} = \frac{V_{L/D}}{\sqrt[4]{3}} = \frac{36.66}{1.316} \approx 27.86 \text{ m/s}$$

**Minimum Power Required:**

$$P_{R,\min} = \frac{1}{2}\rho V_{mp}^3 S C_{D0} + \frac{2 K W^2}{\rho V_{mp} S}$$

$$= \frac{1}{2}(0.9093)(27.86)^3 (18)(0.03) + \frac{2(0.05685)(8000)^2}{(0.9093)(27.86)(18)}$$

$$= \frac{1}{2}(0.9093)(21{,}624)(18)(0.03) + \frac{7.277 \times 10^6}{456.0}$$

$$= 5310 + 15{,}959 \approx 21{,}269 \text{ W} \approx 21.27 \text{ kW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** Since $P_A = 80$ kW and $P_{R,\min} \approx 21.3$ kW, the aircraft has ample excess power at $V_{mp}$ — the aircraft can sustain full-throttle climb and accelerate, so the cruise $V_{\min}$ and $V_{\max}$ will bracket $V_{mp}$.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** **Schematic plot (qualitative):**

```
P (kW)
80 |========== PA (horizontal line at 80 kW) ===========
   |
   |
40 |
   |
30 |              PR  (U-shaped curve)
   |             /  \
   |            /    \
20 |---------- /------\ ----------  <- tangent at Vmp
   |         /        \
   |        /          \
 0 |_______/____________\_____________> V (m/s)
          Vmin  Vmp     Vmax
                          ≈ 36.66
                  ≈ 27.86
```

**Identification of speeds:**
- **Speed for maximum endurance** = $V_{mp} \approx 27.86$ m/s (where $P_R$ touches the minimum, and the excess power $P_A - P_R$ is greatest per unit time).
- **$V_{\min}$ and $V_{\max}$** are solutions to $P_R(V) = 80$ kW. By inspection, $V_{\min} < V_{mp} < V_{\max}$.

**Behaviour as altitude increases:**
1. **$\rho$ decreases** → both $P_R$ curve (entirely) and $P_A$ curve shift down.
2. For a naturally-aspirated piston engine, $P_A \propto \rho$ (decreases rapidly).
3. The two curves **touch tangentially** at one altitude — this is the **absolute ceiling**, where $V_{\min} = V_{\max}$ and only one speed is sustainable.
4. The **service ceiling** is where the excess power above $V_{mp}$ falls to a defined low value (typically 100 ft/min rate of climb).

**(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 1. Confusing $V_{L/D}$ (min drag speed) with $V_{mp}$ (min power speed) — they differ by a factor of $\sqrt[4]{3}$.
> 2. Forgetting to **multiply thrust by velocity** when computing $P_A$ for a jet.
> 3. Treating $P_A$ as horizontal for jets — it is a **cubic-like curve** for jets.
> 4. Using **English units (hp, ft/s, slug/ft³)** without conversion — KTU expects SI throughout.
> 5. Drawing the $P_R$ curve without the **cubic high-speed tail** — students often draw a parabola, losing 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Power Required** $P_R = D \cdot V = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^3 S C_{D0} + \dfrac{2 K W^2}{\rho V S}$ — **cubic** at high $V$, **inverse** at low $V$.
- ⭐ **Power Available** for propeller ≈ constant (horizontal line); for jet ≈ $T_A \cdot V$ (cubic with a maximum).
- ⭐ **Velocity for minimum drag** ($V_{L/D}$) ≠ **velocity for minimum power** ($V_{mp}$). Ratio is $\sqrt[4]{3} \approx 1.316$.
- ⭐ **Best Endurance Speed** = $V_{mp}$ — gives minimum fuel per unit time.
- ⭐ **Best Range Speed** = $V_{L/D}$ — gives minimum fuel per unit distance.
- ⭐ **Maximum level-flight speed** $V_{\max}$ = high-speed intersection of $P_R$ and $P_A$.
- ⭐ **Minimum level-flight speed** $V_{\min}$ = low-speed intersection of $P_R$ and $P_A$.
- ⭐ **Absolute ceiling** = altitude where $P_R$ and $P_A$ touch tangentially (single point of intersection).
- ⭐ **Service ceiling** = altitude where maximum rate of climb drops to a stipulated value (typically **100 ft/min** or **0.5 m/s**).
- ⭐ **Altitude effects:** $\rho(h)$ decreases, both $P_R$ and $P_A$ drop, but $P_A$ drops faster for naturally-aspirated piston engines.
- ⭐ **Drag polar assumption** $C_D = C_{D0} + K C_L^2$ holds for clean aircraft in attached flow (subsonic, moderate $\alpha$). Replace with supersonic polar for $M > 0.85$.
- ⭐ **Jet thrust model:** $T_A = T_{\text{static}}(h) - k V^2$ — linear in $V$ for $P_A$, cubic in $V$.
- ⭐ **Thrust = Drag** in steady level flight — this is the single most fundamental equation of the entire performance module.
- ⭐ **Units throughout must be SI** (m, kg, s, N, W) unless explicitly stated otherwise in the problem.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gliding and Climbing: Rate of Climb, Service and Absolute Ceilings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET753 - AEROSPACE ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Gliding and Climbing: Rate of Climb, Service and Absolute Ceilings # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Gliding Flight — Formal Definition **Gliding flight** is an unpowered (engine-idle or thrust-off) steady, straight, descending flight condition in which the aircraft trades altitude for distance (or time) purely under the action of gravity balanced by aerodynamic lift and drag. In pure glide, the net thrust equals zero, so the weight component along the flight path exactly balances the drag, while the weight component normal to the flight path balances the lift. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Intent):** > A glide is a *steady, symmetric, unpowered descent* in which the flight path angle $\gamma$ is negative and constant. The glide is fully described by the **glide angle** $\gamma$ and the **lift-to-drag ratio** $L/D$. ## 1.2 Climbing Flight — Formal Definition **Climbing flight** is a powered, steady, ascending flight condition in which the aircraft gains altitude. The aircraft climbs with a flight path angle $\gamma > 0$ when the available thrust exceeds the drag. The performance metric of greatest interest is the **Rate of Climb (ROC)**, measured in ft/min or m/s, which indicates how fast the aircraft gains altitude. > [!NOTE] > **Rate of Climb (ROC)** = Vertical component of the velocity vector. > $$\text{ROC} = V \sin\gamma \approx V\gamma \text{ (for small angles)}$$ ## 1.3 Ceilings — Formal Definition - **Absolute Ceiling:** The altitude at which the **maximum rate of climb becomes zero**. Beyond this altitude, steady straight climb is impossible. - **Service Ceiling:** The altitude at which the maximum rate of climb reduces to **100 ft/min (≈ 0.508 m/s)**. This is the practical operational ceiling used by pilots and regulatory agencies. ## 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Hill, the Cyclist, and the Battery" > [!TIP] > **Analogy — Climbing the Hill:** > Imagine a cyclist riding up a hill with a battery (engine) supplying power. The cyclist's body and bike (weight $W$) must be lifted against gravity. As the altitude increases, the air becomes thinner (density $\rho$ drops), so the cyclist pushes against less air resistance (drag $D$ drops), but the legs must also work harder to push air out of the way — wait, that's wrong: in reality drag *decreases* with altitude. So power required decreases. But the *engine's* power output also decreases with altitude because the engine breathes thinner air. > > - **Excess Power** (Engine Power − Power Required) is what lifts the cyclist. When excess power is large → steep climb. When it shrinks to zero → the cyclist has reached the **Absolute Ceiling**. > - The cyclist's practical limit (where they can climb only 100 ft/min, a very slow climb) is the **Service Ceiling**. > > **For Gliding:** Think of a paper plane or a hawk. It cannot hover. It must keep moving forward to keep its wings generating lift. The trade-off is **altitude for distance**. A hawk soars by trading a small drop in altitude for a long horizontal sweep. The best gliding bird or aircraft has the highest $L/D$ ratio. ## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold) | Quantity | Standard Value | Remarks | |---|---|---| | **g** | **9.80665 m/s²** (≈ 32.174 ft/s²) | Standard gravity (ISA) | | **ρ₀ at sea level (ISA)** | **1.225 kg/m³** (≈ 0.002377 slug/ft³) | Sea-level air density | | **Service Ceiling ROC** | **100 ft/min** (≈ **0.508 m/s** or **30.48 m/min**) | ICAO/FAR convention | | **Standard Atmosphere Lapse Rate** | **6.5 K/km** (troposphere) | ISA model | # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Gliding Flight — Operational Analysis In a steady, unpowered glide: - Thrust $T = 0$ (jet) or $T \approx 0$ (propeller at idle) - Weight $W$ acts vertically downward - Lift $L$ acts perpendicular to flight path - Drag $D$ acts parallel to flight path (opposing motion) ### Force Balance (Wind Axes) $$ L = W \cos\gamma \approx W \quad (\gamma \text{ small}) $$ $$ D = W \sin\gamma $$ Dividing the two equations: $$ \tan\gamma = \frac{D}{L} = \frac{1}{(L/D)} $$ ### Glide Angle and Glide Ratio - **Glide Angle** $\gamma$: angle between flight path and horizontal. - **Glide Ratio (L/D):** horizontal distance covered per unit altitude lost. $$ \boxed{\text{Glide Ratio} = \frac{R}{h} = \frac{L}{D} = \frac{1}{\tan\gamma}} $$ **Minimum Glide Angle (Best Glide for Distance):** $$\gamma_{\min} \text{ occurs at } (L/D)_{\max}$$ This is achieved at the **best lift-to-drag speed** $V_{L/D_{\max}}$, found from the polar where $\frac{d(D/L)}{dC_L} = 0$, giving $C_{L}^{*} = \sqrt{C_{D_0}/k}$ for a parabolic polar. **Minimum Rate of Descent (Best Glide for Endurance/Loitering):** $$\text{ROC}_{\text{descent}} = -\frac{D}{L} V = -\frac{V}{L/D}$$ Minimum sink occurs at the speed where $\frac{d(V/D)}{dV} = 0$, i.e., at the **minimum-power speed** $V_{P_{\min}}$, *not* at $V_{L/D_{\max}}$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Distinction (Frequently Tested):** > - *Best Glide for maximum range* → fly at $V_{L/D_{\max}}$ (angle is minimum). > - *Best Glide for maximum endurance* → fly at $V_{P_{\min}}$ (rate of sink is minimum). ### Gliding Range and Endurance For an aircraft starting glide at altitude $h$ with weight $W$ and lift-dependent drag polar $C_D = C_{D_0} + k C_L^2$: $$ R_g = \frac{h}{(L/D)_{\max}} \quad \text{(range from initial altitude } h\text{)} $$ $$ E_g = \int_0^h \frac{dh}{(V/D)_{\min}} \approx \frac{h}{(V/D)_{\min}} \quad \text{(endurance)} $$ ## 2.2 Climbing Flight — Operational Analysis In a steady, powered climb: - Thrust $T$ is along the velocity vector - Weight $W$ acts vertically down - Lift $L$ perpendicular to velocity - Drag $D$ along velocity (opposing motion) ### Force Balance (Wind Axes) $$ T = D + W \sin\gamma $$ $$ L = W \cos\gamma \approx W $$ Solving for $\gamma$: $$ \sin\gamma = \frac{T - D}{W} $$ ### Rate of Climb (ROC) — Two Equivalent Forms **Form 1 — Using Excess Thrust (Jet or Propeller in level-flight equivalence):** $$ \boxed{V_{\text{ROC}} = V \sin\gamma = \frac{(T - D) \cdot V}{W} = \frac{\text{Excess Power}}{W}} $$ **Form 2 — For Propeller Aircraft (using shaft power $P_s$ and propeller efficiency $\eta_p$):** $$ \boxed{V_{\text{ROC}} = \frac{\eta_p P_s - P_R}{W}} $$ where $P_R$ is the power required $= D \cdot V$. > [!NOTE] > **Excess Power = Power Available − Power Required.** Climbing flight is only possible when **Excess Power > 0**, i.e., when $P_A > P_R$. The maximum ROC occurs at the altitude (and speed) where this excess power is maximum. ### Maximum Rate of Climb At any altitude, **max ROC** is found by maximizing $(P_A - P_R)$ over $V$. For a jet with $T$ approximately independent of $V$, the max ROC speed is $V_{P_R\min}$ (speed for minimum power required). For a propeller aircraft with $P_A$ nearly constant, it is also at $V_{P_R\min}$. $$ V_{\text{ROC},\max} = \frac{P_{A} - P_{R,\min}}{W} $$ ## 2.3 Ceilings — Operational Analysis ### Service Ceiling By ICAO/FAR convention, service ceiling $h_{sc}$ is the altitude where: $$ V_{\text{ROC},\max}(h_{sc}) = 100 \text{ ft/min} = 0.508 \text{ m/s} $$ ### Absolute Ceiling Absolute ceiling $h_{ac}$ is the altitude where: $$ V_{\text{ROC},\max}(h_{ac}) = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad P_A(h_{ac}) = P_{R,\min}(h_{ac}) $$ ### Empirical Ceiling Formulas (KTU-Favorite) For a turbojet with thrust $T \propto \rho$ and $P_R \propto \rho$: $$ \boxed{h_{ac} = h_0 + \frac{P_{A,0} - P_{R,0,\min}}{a}} \quad \text{(linear variation, approximate)} $$ More precisely, using density-ratio $\sigma = \rho/\rho_0$: $$ h_{ac} : \quad \sigma_{ac}^{1/2} \cdot T_0 - P_{R,0,\min} = 0 $$ For propeller aircraft with $P_A$ approximately constant with altitude (naturally aspirated engine loses power, but at fixed RPM): - Service ceiling: $P_A(h_{sc}) - P_{R,\min}(h_{sc}) = W \cdot (0.508 \text{ m/s})$ - Absolute ceiling: $P_A(h_{ac}) = P_{R,\min}(h_{ac})$ > [!TIP] > **Quick Rule of Thumb for KTU:** > Service ceiling ≈ Absolute ceiling − ~ 2000 ft for propeller aircraft, > Service ceiling ≈ Absolute ceiling − ~ 4000 ft for jet aircraft (rule varies). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Glide angle | $\tan\gamma_g = D/L = 1/(L/D)$ | $\gamma_g$ in rad | | 2 | Glide ratio (range per height) | $R/h = L/D$ | dimensionless | | 3 | Min glide angle condition | $(L/D)_{\max}$ at $C_L^* = \sqrt{C_{D_0}/k}$ | parabolic polar | | 4 | Min sink rate condition | $V_{P_{\min}}$, where $P_R$ is min | $V$ in m/s | | 5 | Steady climb sin(γ) | $\sin\gamma = (T-D)/W$ | dimensionless | | 6 | Rate of climb (jet form) | $V_{ROC} = V(T-D)/W$ | m/s | | 7 | Rate of climb (propeller form) | $V_{ROC} = (\eta_p P_s - P_R)/W$ | m/s | | 8 | Max rate of climb (jet) | $V_{ROC,\max} = (T_{max} - D_{\min})/W \cdot V$ | m/s | | 9 | Excess power | $P_{ex} = P_A - P_R$ | Watts | | 10 | Service ceiling ROC | $V_{ROC,\max} = 0.508$ m/s | 100 ft/min | | 11 | Absolute ceiling ROC | $V_{ROC,\max} = 0$ | m/s | | 12 | Glide endurance | $E_g = \int dh/\text{sink rate}$ | seconds | | 13 | Glide range from height $h$ | $R_g = h \cdot (L/D)_{\max}$ | metres | | 14 | Thrust–weight ratio for climb | $T/W > D/W + \sin\gamma$ | dimensionless | > [!WARNING] > **Symbol Caution:** Never confuse **propeller shaft power** $P_s$ with **propeller (thrust) power** $T \cdot V$. The relation is $P_{\text{propeller}} = \eta_p \cdot P_s$. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Glide Angle from Force Balance We resolve forces along the wind axes (axes aligned with velocity vector) for a steady, unpowered glide. ### Step 1: Free Body Setup The aircraft is in steady flight, so $\sum F = 0$. Three forces act: $W$ (vertical, downward), $L$ (perpendicular to $V$), $D$ (parallel to $V$, opposite). ### Step 2: Resolve $W$ along wind axes The angle between $W$ (vertical) and the lift axis (perpendicular to $V$) is the flight path angle $\gamma$. The component of $W$ along the lift axis is $W\cos\gamma$, and the component along the drag axis is $W\sin\gamma$. ### Step 3: Write equilibrium Sum of forces along lift axis (normal to V) = 0: $$ L - W\cos\gamma = 0 $$ Sum of forces along drag axis (parallel to V) = 0: $$ D + W\sin\gamma = 0 \quad \text{(Wait — drag opposes velocity, but } W\sin\gamma \text{ is along } V\text{)} $$ Re-stating with signs: drag $D$ acts opposite to $V$; $W\sin\gamma$ acts along $V$ (down the slope). For equilibrium: $$ D = W\sin\gamma $$ ### Step 4: Solve for $\gamma$ $$ \sin\gamma = \frac{D}{W} $$ $$ L = W\cos\gamma \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{L}{W} = \cos\gamma $$ ### Step 5: Divide and use identity $$ \tan\gamma = \frac{\sin\gamma}{\cos\gamma} = \frac{D/W}{L/W} = \frac{D}{L} $$ $$ \boxed{\gamma = \arctan\!\left(\frac{D}{L}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{1}{L/D}\right)} $$ This completes the derivation of the glide angle. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Glide Range from Altitude $h$ For a glider starting at altitude $h$ with constant $L/D$ (valid for small altitude changes where $\rho$ ≈ const): ### Step 1: Instantaneous sink rate From Section 3.1, $\sin\gamma = D/W \approx \gamma$ for small angles. The vertical sink rate is: $$ \dot{h} = -V\sin\gamma = -V \cdot \frac{D}{L} = -\frac{V \cdot C_D}{C_L} $$ ### Step 2: Horizontal range rate $$ \dot{R} = V\cos\gamma \approx V \quad (\gamma \text{ small}) $$ ### Step 3: Form the ratio $\dot{R}/\dot{h}$ $$ \frac{dR}{dh} = -\frac{V}{V\sin\gamma} = -\frac{1}{\sin\gamma} = -\frac{L}{D} $$ The negative sign indicates that $R$ increases as $h$ decreases. ### Step 4: Integrate from $h_0$ to $h_f$ (typically $h_f = 0$) $$ \int_{0}^{R_g} dR = \frac{L}{D} \int_{0}^{h_0} dh $$ $$ \boxed{R_g = h_0 \cdot \frac{L}{D}} $$ > [!NOTE] > For the parabolic polar $C_D = C_{D_0} + kC_L^2$, the **maximum** glide ratio is: > $$(L/D)_{\max} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{C_{D_0}k}}$$ > at $C_L^* = \sqrt{C_{D_0}/k}$. ### Step 5: Final expression for maximum glide range $$ \boxed{R_{g,\max} = \frac{h_0}{2\sqrt{C_{D_0}k}}} $$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Rate of Climb (Jet Form) from Energy Equation ### Step 1: Energy of the aircraft Total mechanical energy per unit mass = Kinetic + Potential: $$ e = \frac{V^2}{2} + gh $$ ### Step 2: Power delivered by engines Power supplied by engine = $T \cdot V$ (thrust-power). Power needed to overcome drag = $D \cdot V = P_R$. Net rate of change of mechanical energy: $$ \frac{de}{dt} = \frac{T V - D V}{m} = \frac{(T-D)V}{m} = \frac{(T-D)V}{W/g} $$ ### Step 3: For steady climb at constant $V$, $dV/dt = 0$, so: $$ \frac{de}{dt} = g \cdot \frac{dh}{dt} = g \cdot V_{\text{ROC}} $$ ### Step 4: Equate and solve for ROC $$ g \cdot V_{\text{ROC}} = \frac{(T-D)V}{W/g} $$ $$ g \cdot V_{\text{ROC}} = \frac{(T-D)Vg}{W} $$ $$ V_{\text{ROC}} = \frac{(T-D)V}{W} $$ ### Step 5: Identify as excess power over weight $$ V_{\text{ROC}} = \frac{P_A - P_R}{W} $$ where $P_A = TV$ and $P_R = DV$. This confirms Section 2.2 Form 1 and Form 2 are equivalent in structure. $$ \boxed{V_{\text{ROC}} = \frac{P_A - P_R}{W}} $$ ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Maximum Rate of Climb at Given Altitude ### Step 1: Define ROC function of speed at fixed altitude At a fixed altitude, $P_A(V)$ and $P_R(V)$ are known functions. Define: $$ V_{\text{ROC}}(V) = \frac{P_A(V) - P_R(V)}{W} $$ ### Step 2: Maximize by differentiation $$ \frac{dV_{\text{ROC}}}{dV} = \frac{1}{W}\left[\frac{dP_A}{dV} - \frac{dP_R}{dV}\right] = 0 $$ ### Step 3: For a jet with $T$ approximately constant with $V$ (small range), $dP_A/dV \approx T$: $$ T = \frac{dP_R}{dV} $$ The condition $dP_R/dV = T$ corresponds geometrically to the point where the tangent to the $P_R$ curve has slope equal to $T$, i.e., where $P_R$ curve is steepest equal to $T$. This is *not* at $V_{P_R\min}$ exactly — but for thin-aerofoil jets near operating point, the max ROC is approximately at $V_{P_R\min}$. ### Step 4: For a propeller aircraft, $P_A \approx$ const (fixed RPM), so $dP_A/dV \approx 0$: $$ \frac{dP_R}{dV} = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad V = V_{P_R\min} $$ ### Step 5: Max ROC speed and value For a propeller aircraft: $$ \boxed{V^* = V_{P_R\min}, \qquad V_{\text{ROC},\max} = \frac{P_A - P_{R,\min}}{W}} $$ ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Absolute Ceiling ### Step 1: Definition At absolute ceiling, $V_{\text{ROC},\max} = 0$: $$ P_A(h_{ac}) = P_{R,\min}(h_{ac}) $$ ### Step 2: Express $P_R$ at minimum (parabolic polar at sea level) For a jet, $P_{R,\min} = \frac{2 W^{3/2}}{3 \rho^{1/2}} \sqrt{\frac{k}{3 C_{D_0}}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{S}}$ — at the minimum power speed. Using $P_{R,\min} \propto \rho$ (since at fixed $W$, $P_{R,\min} = W \cdot V_{P_R\min}$ and $V_{P_R\min} \propto 1/\sqrt{\rho}$ → $P_{R,\min} \propto \rho/\sqrt{\rho} \cdot$ const ... careful re-derivation needed): For a turbojet, $T = T_0 \sigma$ where $\sigma = \rho/\rho_0$ (thrust scales with density). $P_{R,\min} = P_{R,0,\min} \cdot \sigma$ (since $V \propto \sigma^{-1/2}$ at constant $W$, and $P_R = DV \propto \rho V^3 \propto \sigma \cdot \sigma^{-3/2} = \sigma^{-1/2}$). > **Correction:** For a jet, $P_{R,\min} \propto \sigma^{1/2}$ (not $\sigma$). Let me re-derive carefully. ### Step 3: Clean derivation of ceiling (jet, parabolic drag) At $V_{P_R\min}$: $C_L = C_L^* = \sqrt{3 C_{D_0}/k}$, so $V_{P_R\min} = \sqrt{2W/(\rho S C_L^*)}$. Hence: $$ V_{P_R\min} = V_{P_R\min,0} \cdot \sigma^{-1/2} $$ $$ P_{R,\min} = \frac{1}{2} \rho V_{P_R\min}^3 S C_{D,total}^{*} = P_{R,0,\min} \cdot \sigma^{-1/2} $$ (Wait: $\rho V^3 \propto \sigma \cdot \sigma^{-3/2} = \sigma^{-1/2}$.) For jet: $T = T_0 \sigma$, so $P_A = T \cdot V_{P_R\min} = T_0 \sigma \cdot V_{P_R\min,0} \sigma^{-1/2} = P_{A,0} \cdot \sigma^{1/2}$. ### Step 4: Equate at absolute ceiling $$ P_{A,0} \cdot \sigma_{ac}^{1/2} = P_{R,0,\min} \cdot \sigma_{ac}^{-1/2} $$ $$ \sigma_{ac} = \frac{P_{R,0,\min}}{P_{A,0}} $$ ### Step 5: Solve for altitude using standard atmosphere Using $\sigma = (1 - Lh/T_0)^{(g/(LR)-1)}$ where $L$ = lapse rate, $T_0$ = sea-level temperature, $R$ = gas constant — KTU students typically use tabulated values from ISA. $$ \boxed{\sigma_{ac} = \frac{P_{R,0,\min}}{P_{A,0}}} $$ This gives the absolute ceiling $h_{ac}$ via the standard atmosphere table. ### Step 6: Service ceiling (with $V_{ROC} = 0.508$ m/s) $$ V_{ROC} = \frac{P_A - P_R}{W} = \frac{P_{A,0}\sigma_{sc}^{1/2} - P_{R,0,\min}\sigma_{sc}^{-1/2}}{W} = 0.508 \text{ m/s} $$ This is a transcendental equation in $\sigma_{sc}$ solved numerically with ISA tables. ## 3.6 Numerical Example — Worked KTU Problem **Given:** - Aircraft weight $W = 50{,}000$ N - Wing area $S = 30$ m² - Drag polar: $C_D = 0.02 + 0.04 C_L^2$ - Sea-level thrust $T_0 = 12{,}000$ N (jet) - Find: $V_{ROC,\max}$ at sea level; absolute ceiling altitude. ### Step A: Sea-level $V_{ROC,\max}$ $C_L^*$ for max ROC (jet, ≈ $V_{P_R\min}$ condition): $$ C_L^* = \sqrt{\frac{3 C_{D_0}}{k}} = \sqrt{\frac{3 \times 0.02}{0.04}} = \sqrt{1.5} = 1.2247 $$ $V_{P_R\min}$ at sea level: $$ V_{P_R\min} = \sqrt{\frac{2W}{\rho_0 S C_L^*}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 50000}{1.225 \times 30 \times 1.2247}} = \sqrt{\frac{100000}{45.00}} = \sqrt{2222.2} = 47.14 \text{ m/s} $$ Drag at $C_L^*$: $$ C_D^* = C_{D_0} + k (C_L^*)^2 = 0.02 + 0.04 \times 1.5 = 0.02 + 0.06 = 0.08 $$ $D = \frac{1}{2}\rho_0 V^2 S C_D^* = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 2222.2 \times 30 \times 0.08 = 3264$ N Excess thrust: $T_0 - D = 12000 - 3264 = 8736$ N ROC = $(T_0 - D) \cdot V / W = 8736 \times 47.14 / 50000 = 8.236$ m/s = **1647 ft/min** ✓ (Excellent climb) ### Step B: Absolute Ceiling $\sigma_{ac} = P_{R,0,\min}/P_{A,0}$ $P_{R,0,\min} = D \cdot V = 3264 \times 47.14 = 153{,}878$ W $P_{A,0} = T_0 \cdot V = 12000 \times 47.14 = 565{,}680$ W $\sigma_{ac} = 153878 / 565680 = 0.2721$ From ISA table: $\sigma = 0.2721$ corresponds to $h_{ac} \approx 10{,}850$ m ≈ **35,600 ft**. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Gliding Flight Geometry & Force Balance ```mermaid flowchart LR Aircraft["Aircraft in Steady Glide"] Weight["Weight W = mg
Acts vertically DOWN"] Lift["Lift L
Perpendicular to Velocity"] Drag["Drag D
Opposite to Velocity"] GlideAng["Glide Angle gamma
Below horizontal"] FlightPath["Velocity V
Along glide path"] L_eq_Wcos["L = W cos gamma"] D_eq_Wsin["D = W sin gamma"] GlideRatio["tan gamma = D/L = 1 divide L by D"] Aircraft --> Weight Aircraft --> Lift Aircraft --> Drag Aircraft --> GlideAng Aircraft --> FlightPath Weight --> L_eq_Wcos Weight --> D_eq_Wsin L_eq_Wcos --> GlideRatio D_eq_Wsin --> GlideRatio ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Climbing Flight Geometry & Force Balance ```mermaid flowchart TB ClimbingAircraft["Aircraft in Steady Climb"] Thrust["Thrust T
Along velocity FORWARD"] WeightClimb["Weight W
Vertical DOWN"] LiftClimb["Lift L
Perpendicular to V"] DragClimb["Drag D
Opposite to V"] ClimbAngle["Climb Angle gamma
Above horizontal"] ExcessThrust["Excess Thrust = T minus D"] Thrust_eq["T = D + W sin gamma"] Lift_eq["L = W cos gamma approx W"] ROC_formula["ROC = V sin gamma
= T minus D times V over W"] ExcessPower["ROC = P_A minus P_R over W"] ClimbingAircraft --> Thrust ClimbingAircraft --> WeightClimb ClimbingAircraft --> LiftClimb ClimbingAircraft --> DragClimb ClimbingAircraft --> ClimbAngle Thrust --> ExcessThrust DragClimb --> ExcessThrust ExcessThrust --> Thrust_eq ExcessThrust --> ROC_formula Thrust_eq --> ROC_formula Lift_eq --> ROC_formula ROC_formula --> ExcessPower ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Power Curves and Ceilings (Block Architecture Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR AxisV["X axis: Velocity V"] AxisP["Y axis: Power P"] PR_Curve["Power Required Curve P_R
Parabolic Bowl Shape"] PA_SL["Power Available at Sea Level
High Horizontal Line"] PA_Alt["Power Available at Altitude
Lower Horizontal Line"] Intersection1["P_A intersect P_R
Sea Level Two Intersection Points"] Intersection2["P_A intersect P_R
High Altitude Tangent Single Point"] V_Pr_min["V at P_R minimum
Best Climb Speed"] ExcessPower["Excess Power = P_A minus P_R
Vertical Gap Between Curves"] ROC_Formula["ROC = Excess Power divide by W"] AxisV --- AxisP AxisP --> PR_Curve AxisP --> PA_SL AxisP --> PA_Alt PA_SL --> Intersection1 PA_Alt --> Intersection2 PR_Curve --> V_Pr_min PA_SL --> ExcessPower PR_Curve --> ExcessPower ExcessPower --> ROC_Formula ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Concept Map: Ceilings Definition Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB Start["Aircraft Climbing Performance"] ROC_Check["Check Maximum Rate of Climb"] ROC_Zero["ROC max = 0"] ROC_100["ROC max = 100 ft per min"] Abs_Ceiling["ABSOLUTE CEILING h_ac
Theoretical Maximum Altitude
No Further Climb Possible"] Serv_Ceiling["SERVICE CEILING h_sc
Practical Operational Limit
Slow Climb 100 ft per min"] Prop_Corr["Propeller: h_ac minus h_sc approx 2000 ft"] Jet_Corr["Jet: h_ac minus h_sc approx 4000 ft"] Form_Abs["Formula: P_A = P_R at h_ac"] Form_Serv["Formula: ROC at h_sc = 0.508 m/s"] Start --> ROC_Check ROC_Check --> ROC_Zero ROC_Check --> ROC_100 ROC_Zero --> Abs_Ceiling ROC_100 --> Serv_Ceiling Abs_Ceiling --> Prop_Corr Abs_Ceiling --> Jet_Corr Abs_Ceiling --> Form_Abs Serv_Ceiling --> Form_Serv ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) ### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define the terms: Glide angle, Glide ratio, and Minimum glide angle. Show their mathematical relationship.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Glide angle ($\gamma_g$):** The angle between the glide flight path and the horizontal in an unpowered descent. (1 mark) - **Glide ratio:** The horizontal distance travelled per unit altitude lost, mathematically equal to $L/D$. (1 mark) - **Minimum glide angle:** The smallest possible $\gamma_g$, achieved when $(L/D)$ is at its maximum. At this condition, the range covered for a given altitude lost is maximum. (1 mark) - **Relationship:** $\tan\gamma_g = D/L = 1/(L/D)$. When $(L/D) = (L/D)_{\max}$, $\gamma_g = \gamma_{g,\min}$. ### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define service ceiling and absolute ceiling. State the ROC values at these ceilings as per ICAO convention.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Absolute ceiling ($h_{ac}$):** The altitude at which the maximum rate of climb becomes zero. Beyond this, no steady climb is possible. (1 mark) - **Service ceiling ($h_{sc}$):** The altitude at which the maximum rate of climb reduces to a specified minimum usable value. (1 mark) - **ICAO/FAR convention:** - At $h_{ac}$: $\text{ROC}_{\max} = 0$ - At $h_{sc}$: $\text{ROC}_{\max} = 100 \text{ ft/min} \approx 0.508 \text{ m/s}$ (1 mark) ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each) ### Question B-A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Derive the expression for the **rate of climb** of a propeller-driven aircraft in terms of shaft power, propeller efficiency, power required, and aircraft weight. (7 marks) **(b)** An aircraft weighs 60,000 N, has wing area 30 m², and drag polar $C_D = 0.018 + 0.045 C_L^2$. At sea level ($\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³), the engine delivers 1100 kW shaft power and the propeller efficiency is 0.82. Determine: (i) The speed for maximum rate of climb. (3 marks) (ii) The maximum rate of climb in m/s and ft/min. (4 marks) #### Model Solution for (a): From the energy equation in steady, powered climb: $$ \frac{dE}{dt} = T \cdot V - D \cdot V = (T - D)V $$ But also $dE/dt = W \cdot V_{\text{ROC}}$ (rate of gain of potential energy, $W \cdot dh/dt$). For a propeller aircraft, the propeller converts shaft power $P_s$ to thrust power $T \cdot V$ with efficiency $\eta_p$: $$ T \cdot V = \eta_p P_s $$ Therefore: $$ W \cdot V_{\text{ROC}} = \eta_p P_s - P_R $$ $$ \boxed{V_{\text{ROC}} = \frac{\eta_p P_s - P_R}{W}} $$ This is the required expression. (7 marks — covering force/energy balance, power definitions, and final formula.) #### Model Solution for (b): **Part (i) — Speed for max ROC:** For a propeller aircraft with $P_A$ approximately constant over the speed range, max ROC occurs at $V_{P_R\min}$. For parabolic drag, $P_{R,\min}$ occurs at: $$ C_L^* = \sqrt{\frac{3 C_{D_0}}{k}} = \sqrt{\frac{3 \times 0.018}{0.045}} = \sqrt{1.2} = 1.0954 $$ Speed: $$ V = \sqrt{\frac{2W}{\rho S C_L^*}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 60000}{1.225 \times 30 \times 1.0954}} = \sqrt{\frac{120000}{40.262}} = \sqrt{2980.5} = 54.59 \text{ m/s} $$ **Answer: $V^* = 54.59$ m/s ≈ 109 knots** (3 marks) **Part (ii) — Max ROC:** Compute $C_D^*$ at $C_L^*$: $$ C_D^* = C_{D_0} + k (C_L^*)^2 = 0.018 + 0.045 \times 1.2 = 0.018 + 0.054 = 0.072 $$ Drag: $$ D = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_D^* = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times (54.59)^2 \times 30 \times 0.072 $$ $$ D = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 2980.5 \times 30 \times 0.072 = 3941.4 \text{ N} $$ Power required: $$ P_R = D \cdot V = 3941.4 \times 54.59 = 215{,}189 \text{ W} = 215.19 \text{ kW} $$ Power available: $$ P_A = \eta_p P_s = 0.82 \times 1100 = 902 \text{ kW} $$ Excess power: $$ P_A - P_R = 902 - 215.19 = 686.81 \text{ kW} $$ Max ROC: $$ V_{\text{ROC},\max} = \frac{P_A - P_R}{W} = \frac{686{,}810}{60000} = 11.45 \text{ m/s} $$ Conversion to ft/min: $$ V_{\text{ROC},\max} = 11.45 \times \frac{60}{0.3048} = 11.45 \times 196.85 = 2254 \text{ ft/min} $$ **Answer: Max ROC = 11.45 m/s ≈ 2254 ft/min** (4 marks) --- ### Question B-B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** With neat sketches, derive the expression for **glide angle** and **range** in a steady unpowered glide. (7 marks) **(b)** A sailplane weighs 8000 N with wing area 18 m² and has a parabolic drag polar $C_D = 0.012 + 0.035 C_L^2$. The pilot releases the sailplane at altitude 3000 m. Determine: (i) The minimum glide angle and corresponding $C_L$. (3 marks) (ii) The maximum glide range. (4 marks) #### Model Solution for (a): For steady unpowered glide ($T = 0$), resolving forces along wind axes: $$ L = W\cos\gamma \quad ; \quad D = W\sin\gamma $$ Dividing: $\tan\gamma = D/L$ $$\boxed{\gamma = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{L/D}\right)}$$ **Glide range derivation:** With $L = W$ (small $\gamma$): $$ \frac{dR}{dh} = -\frac{L}{D} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_g = h_0 \cdot \frac{L}{D} $$ (7 marks — full diagram explanation and integration.) #### Model Solution for (b): **Part (i) — Min glide angle condition:** $ (L/D)_{\max} $ occurs at $C_L^* = \sqrt{C_{D_0}/k} = \sqrt{0.012/0.035} = \sqrt{0.3429} = 0.5856 $ $(L/D)_{\max} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{C_{D_0}k}} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{0.012 \times 0.035}} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{0.00042}} = \frac{1}{0.0410} = 24.39$ Min glide angle: $$ \gamma_{\min} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{24.39}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.0410) = 2.35° $$ **Answer: $C_L^* = 0.586$, $\gamma_{\min} = 2.35°$** (3 marks) **Part (ii) — Maximum glide range:** $$ R_{g,\max} = h_0 \cdot (L/D)_{\max} = 3000 \times 24.39 = 73{,}170 \text{ m} = 73.17 \text{ km} $$ **Answer: Max glide range = 73.17 km ≈ 73,170 m** (4 marks) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert altitude and ROC units** — Mixing ft with m gives 0 marks. Always convert to SI first, or be consistent. > 2. **Using wrong $C_L^*$ formula** — For max ROC, $C_L^* = \sqrt{3 C_{D_0}/k}$ (NOT $\sqrt{C_{D_0}/k}$, which is for max $L/D$). > 3. **Ignoring small-angle approximation** — In glide derivations, the approximation $\cos\gamma \approx 1$ must be explicitly stated for full marks. > 4. **Confusing power available definitions** — $P_A = TV$ for jet; $P_A = \eta_p P_s$ for propeller. Mis-application = -2 marks. > 5. **Not using ISA table** for ceiling calculations — Direct use of $\sigma$ without the standard atmosphere table is incomplete. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Glide Angle:** $\gamma_g = \tan^{-1}(D/L) = \tan^{-1}(1/(L/D))$. Always measured below horizontal. - **Best Glide Speed (Range):** $V_{L/D_{\max}}$ corresponding to $C_L^* = \sqrt{C_{D_0}/k}$. - **Min Sink Speed (Endurance):** $V_{P_{\min}}$ corresponding to $C_L^* = \sqrt{3 C_{D_0}/k}$. *(Different $C_L$ from above!)* - **Max Glide Range:** $R_{g,\max} = h_0 \cdot (L/D)_{\max}$. - **Rate of Climb (Jet):** $V_{\text{ROC}} = V(T-D)/W = (P_A - P_R)/W$. - **Rate of Climb (Propeller):** $V_{\text{ROC}} = (\eta_p P_s - P_R)/W$. - **Max ROC Speed:** $V_{P_R\min}$ (where power required is minimum). - **Service Ceiling:** $\text{ROC}_{\max} = 100$ ft/min $= 0.508$ m/s. - **Absolute Ceiling:** $\text{ROC}_{\max} = 0$ → $P_A = P_{R,\min}$. - **Excess Power** is the fundamental driver of climb — it is positive for climb, zero at absolute ceiling. - **For a jet:** $T \propto \rho$, so both $P_A$ and $P_{R,\min}$ vary with altitude. The exact variation gives the ceiling. - **Units to remember:** $1$ m/s = $196.85$ ft/min; $1$ ft = $0.3048$ m. - **KTU Examination Tip:** When asked about ceilings, always state the ROC convention explicitly in your answer — examiners award marks for convention recall. - **KTU Examination Tip:** Always start a derivation with a clear free-body diagram (mermaid or text) and define all symbols — this fetches 1–2 easy marks even before any math. - **KTU Examination Tip:** Distinguish clearly between *steady* and *unsteady* climb; questions often test this.

topics.title: Gliding Angle and Speed of Flattest Glide
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET753 - AEROSPACE ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Gliding Angle and Speed of Flattest Glide ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMET753 - Module 4):** This topic belongs to the *Performance – Steady Flight* cluster and forms a critical foundation for the **Unpowered Flight / Glide Performance** segment of the syllabus. ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **glide** is a steady, unpowered descent in which an aircraft exchanges its potential energy (altitude) for kinetic/range energy to cover horizontal distance. The **glide angle ($\gamma$)** is the angle subtended between the flight path (velocity vector) and the local horizontal. The **flattest glide** is the particular unpowered descent that yields the *smallest* possible glide angle for a given aircraft configuration and flight condition. In aerodynamics, the flattest glide is achieved when the aircraft flies at the **maximum lift-to-drag ratio**, $(L/D)_{\max}$. | Parameter | Symbol | Definition | |---|---|---| | Glide Angle | $\gamma$ | Angle between flight path and horizontal | | Glide Ratio | $R/h$ | Horizontal range per unit altitude lost | | Flattest Glide | $\gamma_{\min}$ | Smallest possible $\gamma$ for a given W and $\rho$ | | Best Glide Speed | $V_{\text{BG}}$ | EAS/TAS at which $(L/D)_{\max}$ is realised | ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy — "Pedalling Down a Hill with Your Eyes Closed":** > Imagine coasting down a long hill on a bicycle. The *steepness* of the hill determines how far you travel for every metre of altitude lost. A **flatter glide** is like a *gentle, extended slope* — you travel a long horizontal distance before losing each metre of height. A **steep glide** is like going down a *near-vertical cliff* — you lose altitude quickly for very little horizontal progress. The bicycle's gears (analogy: aircraft's angle of attack) determine the *efficiency* of the descent. The "sweet spot" gear (highest gear-to-pedal-effort ratio) gives the flattest glide. For an aircraft, the "sweet spot" corresponds to the **angle of attack that minimises the ratio of drag to lift**, i.e., the angle where the polar curve $C_L/C_D$ is maximum. Flying faster or slower than this optimum results in a *steeper* glide. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - Standard Sea-Level Density: **$\rho_0 = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$** - Standard Sea-Level Pressure: **$p_0 = 101{,}325 \text{ Pa}$** - Drag Polar (subsonic aircraft): **$C_D = C_{D0} + K C_L^{\,2}$** where $C_{D0}$ is the parasite-drag coefficient and $K = 1/(\pi \cdot AR \cdot e)$. - Acceleration due to gravity: **$g = 9.80665 \text{ m/s}^2$** ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Force Equilibrium in a Steady Glide In a steady, unpowered glide, the **thrust $T = 0$** and the aircraft is in translational equilibrium (no acceleration along or perpendicular to the flight path). The three forces acting are: 1. **Weight ($W$)** — acting vertically downward through the CG. 2. **Lift ($L$)** — acting perpendicular to the relative wind (velocity vector). 3. **Drag ($D$)** — acting parallel to the relative wind, opposing motion. **Resolving forces along and perpendicular to the flight path:** $$\sum F_{\perp} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; L = W \cos\gamma$$ $$\sum F_{\parallel} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; D = W \sin\gamma$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Engineering Insight:** In a glide, lift *does not equal weight*. Because the flight path is inclined, the lift vector only supports the *component* of weight perpendicular to the flight path ($W \cos\gamma$). The remaining component ($W \sin\gamma$) is balanced by drag. ### 2.2 Glide Angle and Glide Ratio Dividing the parallel and perpendicular equations: $$\tan\gamma = \frac{D}{L} = \frac{1}{L/D} = \frac{C_D}{C_L}$$ The **glide ratio** (range per unit altitude loss) is: $$\frac{R}{h} = \cot\gamma = \frac{L}{D} = \frac{C_L}{C_D}$$ - **Small $\gamma$ (flattest glide)** ⟹ **Large $L/D$** ⟹ **High $C_L/C_D$**. - **Large $\gamma$ (steepest glide)** ⟹ **Small $L/D$**. ### 2.3 The Drag-Polar Picture and the Flattest Glide The classic subsonic drag polar is: $$C_D = C_{D0} + K C_L^{\,2}$$ Thus the aerodynamic efficiency is: $$\frac{C_L}{C_D} = \frac{C_L}{C_{D0} + K C_L^{\,2}}$$ The **maximum of $C_L/C_D$** is the **flattest-glide condition**. To find the optimum $C_L$: $$\frac{d}{dC_L}\!\left(\frac{C_L}{C_{D0} + K C_L^{\,2}}\right) = 0$$ Solving (see Section 3 for full derivation) gives: $$C_{L_{\text{opt}}} = \sqrt{\frac{C_{D0}}{K}}$$ The maximum lift-to-drag ratio is: $$\left(\frac{L}{D}\right)_{\max} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{K C_{D0}}}$$ And the *minimum* (flattest) glide angle is: $$\gamma_{\min} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(2\sqrt{K C_{D0}}\right)$$ ### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **exam-ready formula inventory** for the topic. All symbols use **SI units** unless stated otherwise. | # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Conditions | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Lift (perpendicular eq.) | $L = W \cos\gamma$ | Steady unpowered glide | | 2 | Drag (parallel eq.) | $D = W \sin\gamma$ | Steady unpowered glide | | 3 | Glide angle | $\tan\gamma = D/L = C_D/C_L$ | Always true in glide | | 4 | Glide ratio | $R/h = L/D = C_L/C_D$ | Range per unit altitude lost | | 5 | Drag polar | $C_D = C_{D0} + K C_L^{\,2}$ | Subsonic, clean configuration | | 6 | Induced drag factor | $K = 1/(\pi \cdot AR \cdot e)$ | $AR = b^2/S$; $e$ = Oswald factor | | 7 | Optimum $C_L$ (max L/D) | $C_{L_{\text{opt}}} = \sqrt{C_{D0}/K}$ | Flattest glide condition | | 8 | Maximum L/D ratio | $(L/D)_{\max} = 1/(2\sqrt{K C_{D0}})$ | Independent of altitude & W | | 9 | Min glide angle | $\gamma_{\min} = \tan^{-1}(2\sqrt{K C_{D0}})$ | Also independent of W | | 10 | Best glide speed | $V_{\text{BG}} = \sqrt{2W/(\rho S)} \cdot (K/C_{D0})^{1/4}$ | EAS at sea level (no compressibility) | | 11 | Stall speed at $C_{L_{\text{opt}}}$ | $V_{\text{stall,opt}} = \sqrt{2W/(\rho S C_{L_{\max}})}$ | Used to ensure $V_{\text{BG}} > V_{\text{stall}}$ | | 12 | Glide range (constant altitude loss) | $R = h \cdot (L/D)$ | $h$ = altitude lost over the glide | | 13 | Endurance-style glide time | $t = (L/D) \cdot \frac{1}{V} \cdot h$ | Less common in 14-mark problems | | 14 | Specific range in glide | $R_{\text{sp}} = (L/D)/W$ | Used in Breguet-style glider problems | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Units Reminder:** All velocities in formula 10 are in **m/s** when $W$ is in Newtons, $\rho$ in kg/m³, and $S$ in m². For knots/ft, use **ISA-standard conversion factors**. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Application of Flattest-Glide Theory | |---|---| | **Glider / Sailplane Design** | Maximising $(L/D)_{\max}$ via high aspect-ratio wings and low parasite drag (e.g., Schleicher ASW-27, $(L/D)_{\max} \approx 60:1$). | | **Engine-Out Emergency Procedures** | Civil aviation pilots are trained to fly at $V_{\text{BG}}$ to maximise the chance of reaching a suitable landing field. | | **UAV / Drone Mission Planning** | Energy-efficient loitering and emergency landing trajectories for fixed-wing UAVs. | | **Re-entry Glide Vehicles** | Hypersonic lifting re-entry (e.g., X-38) uses analogous maximum-L/D hypersonic glide corridors. | | **Sailplane Racing** | MacCready theory uses $V_{\text{BG}}$ as the reference speed for *speed-to-fly* in still air. | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Glide Angle from Force Balance **Starting Point:** The free-body diagram of a glider in steady, unpowered descent. The three forces $W$, $L$, $D$ must sum to zero. We resolve along axes aligned with the flight path: **Perpendicular to flight path:** $$L - W \cos\gamma = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad L = W \cos\gamma \quad\text{...(i)}$$ **Parallel to flight path (along velocity vector):** $$D - W \sin\gamma = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad D = W \sin\gamma \quad\text{...(ii)}$$ **Dividing (ii) by (i):** $$\frac{D}{L} = \frac{W \sin\gamma}{W \cos\gamma} = \tan\gamma$$ $$\therefore\;\boxed{\;\tan\gamma = \frac{D}{L} = \frac{C_D}{C_L} = \frac{1}{L/D}\;}$$ **Glide ratio (range per unit altitude lost):** The aircraft loses altitude $h$ while covering horizontal range $R$. From the geometry of the glide triangle: $$\frac{R}{h} = \cot\gamma = \frac{L}{D}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Optimum $C_L$ for Maximum $L/D$ **Objective:** Maximise the aerodynamic efficiency $\mathcal{E}(C_L) = C_L/C_D$ subject to the parabolic drag polar $C_D = C_{D0} + K C_L^{\,2}$. **Substitute the polar:** $$\mathcal{E}(C_L) = \frac{C_L}{C_{D0} + K C_L^{\,2}}$$ **Differentiate w.r.t. $C_L$ and set to zero (necessary condition for extremum):** Using the quotient rule, $\dfrac{d\mathcal{E}}{dC_L} = 0$ gives: $$\bigl(C_{D0} + K C_L^{\,2}\bigr) - C_L \bigl(2 K C_L\bigr) = 0$$ **Simplify the numerator:** $$C_{D0} + K C_L^{\,2} - 2K C_L^{\,2} = 0$$ $$C_{D0} - K C_L^{\,2} = 0$$ **Solve for $C_L$:** $$K C_L^{\,2} = C_{D0}$$ $$\therefore\;\boxed{\;C_{L_{\text{opt}}} = \sqrt{\frac{C_{D0}}{K}}\;} \quad \text{...(iii)}$$ **Confirm it is a maximum (second-derivative test or physical reasoning):** A second differentiation shows $\dfrac{d^2\mathcal{E}}{dC_L^2} < 0$ at this point, confirming a **maximum**. Physically: as $C_L$ increases from zero, induced-drag grows as $C_L^{\,2}$ and eventually overtakes the linear rise in $C_L$, producing a peak in $C_L/C_D$. ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum $L/D$ and Minimum Glide Angle **Substitute (iii) into the drag polar:** $$C_{D_{\text{opt}}} = C_{D0} + K \left(\sqrt{\frac{C_{D0}}{K}}\right)^{2} = C_{D0} + K \cdot \frac{C_{D0}}{K} = 2 C_{D0}$$ **Form the ratio:** $$\left(\frac{L}{D}\right)_{\max} = \frac{C_{L_{\text{opt}}}}{C_{D_{\text{opt}}}} = \frac{\sqrt{C_{D0}/K}}{2 C_{D0}} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{K C_{D0}}}$$ $$\therefore\;\boxed{\;\left(\frac{L}{D}\right)_{\max} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{K C_{D0}}}\;}$$ **Minimum (flattest) glide angle:** $$\gamma_{\min} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{(L/D)_{\max}}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(2\sqrt{K C_{D0}}\right)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Crucial observation:** Both $(L/D)_{\max}$ and $\gamma_{\min}$ are **independent of the aircraft weight $W$** and the density $\rho$. They depend *only* on the aerodynamic design ($C_{D0}$, $K$). However, the **speed** at which the flattest glide occurs *does* depend on $W$ and $\rho$. ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Best Glide Speed $V_{\text{BG}}$ **Starting Point:** Lift-equation in steady flight: $$L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S\, C_L = W$$ **Solve for $V$ in terms of $C_L$:** $$V = \sqrt{\frac{2W}{\rho S \, C_L}}$$ **Substitute the optimum $C_L$ from (iii):** $$V_{\text{BG}} = \sqrt{\frac{2W}{\rho S}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{C_{L_{\text{opt}}}}} = \sqrt{\frac{2W}{\rho S}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{\sqrt{C_{D0}/K}}}$$ **Simplify the nested radical:** $$\sqrt{\sqrt{\frac{C_{D0}}{K}}} = \left(\frac{C_{D0}}{K}\right)^{1/4}$$ **Therefore:** $$\therefore\;\boxed{\;V_{\text{BG}} = \sqrt{\frac{2W}{\rho S}} \cdot \left(\frac{K}{C_{D0}}\right)^{1/4}\;} \quad \text{...(iv)}$$ **Equivalent form** (often seen in textbooks, where $V_{\text{min-D}}$ is the speed for minimum drag in level flight): $$V_{\text{BG}} = V_{\text{min-D}} = \sqrt{\frac{2W}{\rho S} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{K}{C_{D0}}}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical interpretation of (iv):** A heavier aircraft or a thinner atmosphere (high altitude) flies the flattest glide at a *higher* true airspeed. Conversely, a higher parasite drag ($C_{D0}$ up) or lower induced-drag factor ($K$ down — high aspect ratio) shifts $V_{\text{BG}}$ to a *lower* speed. ### 3.5 Numerical Example — Worked KTU-Style Problem > **Given:** A glider has $W = 9000$ N, wing area $S = 18$ m², $C_{D0} = 0.018$, $K = 0.045$, flying at sea level ($\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³). > **Find:** (a) $(L/D)_{\max}$, (b) $\gamma_{\min}$, (c) $V_{\text{BG}}$, (d) $C_{L_{\text{opt}}}$. **(a) Maximum L/D ratio:** $$\left(\frac{L}{D}\right)_{\max} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{K C_{D0}}} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{0.045 \times 0.018}} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{8.1 \times 10^{-4}}} = \frac{1}{2(0.02846)} \approx 17.57$$ **(b) Minimum glide angle:** $$\gamma_{\min} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(2\sqrt{K C_{D0}}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(2 \times 0.02846\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.0569) \approx 3.26^{\circ}$$ **(c) Best glide speed:** $$V_{\text{BG}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 9000}{1.225 \times 18}} \cdot \left(\frac{0.045}{0.018}\right)^{1/4} = \sqrt{\frac{18000}{22.05}} \cdot (2.5)^{0.25}$$ $$V_{\text{BG}} = \sqrt{816.33} \cdot (1.2574) = 28.57 \times 1.2574 \approx 35.92 \text{ m/s} \;\;(\approx 69.8 \text{ kts})$$ **(d) Optimum lift coefficient:** $$C_{L_{\text{opt}}} = \sqrt{\frac{C_{D0}}{K}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.018}{0.045}} = \sqrt{0.4} \approx 0.632$$ **Check (Lift-equation verification):** $V_{\text{BG}}^{2} = 2W/(\rho S C_{L_{\text{opt}}}) = 18000/(1.225 \times 18 \times 0.632) = 18000/13.93 = 1292$ → $V_{\text{BG}} = 35.94$ m/s ✓ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid — Force-Resolution & Glide-Logic Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Aircraft in Steady Glide
Thrust T = 0] --> B[Free-Body Diagram
W, L, D] B --> C{Resolve along axes
aligned to flight path} C -->|Perpendicular| D[L = W cos gamma] C -->|Parallel| E[D = W sin gamma] D --> F[Glide Equation
tan gamma = D over L] E --> F F --> G[Glide Ratio
R over h = L over D] F --> H[Goal: Minimise gamma] H --> I[Equivalent to
Maximise L over D] I --> J[Apply Drag Polar
C_D = C_D0 + K C_L squared] J --> K[d by dC_L of C_L over C_D = 0] K --> L[Optimum C_L = sqrt C_D0 over K] L --> M[Maximum L over D
= 1 over 2 sqrt K C_D0] L --> N[Best Glide Speed
V_BG = sqrt 2W over rho S times K over C_D0 raised to 1 over 4] M --> O[Flattest Glide Condition
gamma_min = tan inverse 2 sqrt K C_D0] N --> O G --> O O --> P[Engineering Outcome
Maximise horizontal range per unit altitude lost] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid compilation note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (`A`, `B`, `C` … `P`) and labels contain only plain uppercase alphanumeric text — no markdown formatting inside double-quoted labels — to ensure clean rendering in GitHub, Obsidian, and VS Code preview. ### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — From Design Parameters to Glide Performance ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUTS[Input Design Parameters] I1[Wing Area S] I2[Aspect Ratio AR] I3[Oswald Factor e] I4[Parasite Drag C_D0] I5[Weight W] I6[Air Density rho] end subgraph AERO[Aerodynamic Quantities] A1[Induced Drag Factor
K = 1 over pi AR e] A2[Optimum C_L = sqrt C_D0 over K] A3[Max L over D = 1 over 2 sqrt K C_D0] end subgraph PERF[Performance Outputs] P1[Best Glide Speed V_BG] P2[Min Glide Angle gamma_min] P3[Glide Range R = h times L over D] end I1 --> A1 I2 --> A1 I3 --> A1 I4 --> A2 A1 --> A2 A1 --> A3 I4 --> A3 A2 --> P1 I5 --> P1 I6 --> P1 A3 --> P2 A3 --> P3 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define the "glide angle" of an aircraft and state the aerodynamic condition for the *flattest glide* in level, unpowered flight.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > **Definition (1.5 marks):** The glide angle $\gamma$ is the angle between the flight path (velocity vector) and the local horizontal in an unpowered descent. Mathematically, > $$\tan\gamma = \frac{D}{L} = \frac{C_D}{C_L}.$$ > > **Flattest-glide condition (1.5 marks):** The flattest glide occurs when the *glide angle is minimum*, which is equivalent to the *lift-to-drag ratio being maximum*. The corresponding optimum lift coefficient is > $$C_{L_{\text{opt}}} = \sqrt{\frac{C_{D0}}{K}},$$ > giving > $$\left(\frac{L}{D}\right)_{\max} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{K C_{D0}}}.$$ --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **With the help of a free-body diagram, derive the relationship $\tan\gamma = D/L$ for a steady, unpowered glide.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > **Diagram (1 mark):** Sketch of glider showing $W$ vertically downward, $L$ perpendicular to flight path, $D$ along the flight path (opposite to velocity), with glide angle $\gamma$ between the flight path and horizontal. > > **Equations (1.5 marks):** Resolving along and perpendicular to the flight path: > $$L = W \cos\gamma, \qquad D = W \sin\gamma.$$ > Dividing the two: > $$\frac{D}{L} = \frac{W \sin\gamma}{W \cos\gamma} = \tan\gamma.$$ > > **Statement (0.5 mark):** Therefore, $\tan\gamma = D/L$, QED. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each 14-mark question typically has two 7-mark sub-parts: (a) a derivation / theoretical question and (b) a numerical / application question. Choose **one** of the two alternatives below. --- #### **Option A — Question A (14 Marks)** **`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 4]`** **(a)** *Derive, from the parabolic drag polar $C_D = C_{D0} + K C_L^{\,2}$, the condition for the flattest glide. Show that the corresponding minimum glide angle and maximum $L/D$ ratio are independent of aircraft weight.* **(7 marks)** **(b)** *A powered aircraft has the following data:* > $W = 60{,}000$ N, $S = 30$ m², $C_{D0} = 0.022$, $AR = 7.5$, $e = 0.85$. > > *Compute (i) the best-glide speed at sea level ($\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³), (ii) the minimum glide angle, (iii) the glide ratio, and (iv) the horizontal range that can be covered from a height of 4000 m in still air at this configuration.* **(7 marks)** > **Model Solution:** > > **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** > Starting from the parabolic polar $C_D = C_{D0} + K C_L^{\,2}$, the aerodynamic efficiency is: > $$\frac{C_L}{C_D} = \frac{C_L}{C_{D0} + K C_L^{\,2}}.$$ > Differentiating and setting to zero: > $$\frac{d}{dC_L}\!\left(\frac{C_L}{C_{D0} + K C_L^{\,2}}\right) = \frac{C_{D0} - K C_L^{\,2}}{(C_{D0} + K C_L^{\,2})^{2}} = 0.$$ > Hence $C_{L_{\text{opt}}} = \sqrt{C_{D0}/K}$. The maximum efficiency is: > $$\left(\frac{L}{D}\right)_{\max} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{K C_{D0}}}.$$ > The minimum glide angle is: > $$\gamma_{\min} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(2\sqrt{K C_{D0}}\right).$$ > Both quantities depend *only* on $C_{D0}$ and $K$ (i.e., purely on aerodynamic design), and *not* on $W$ or $\rho$. **Hence weight-independent.** (7/7) > > **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** > Step 1 — Compute $K$: > $$K = \frac{1}{\pi \cdot AR \cdot e} = \frac{1}{\pi \times 7.5 \times 0.85} = \frac{1}{20.027} \approx 0.04993.$$ > > Step 2 — Optimum $C_L$: > $$C_{L_{\text{opt}}} = \sqrt{\frac{C_{D0}}{K}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.022}{0.04993}} = \sqrt{0.4406} \approx 0.6639.$$ > > Step 3 — (i) Best-glide speed at S.L.: > $$V_{\text{BG}} = \sqrt{\frac{2W}{\rho S C_{L_{\text{opt}}}}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 60{,}000}{1.225 \times 30 \times 0.6639}}$$ > $$= \sqrt{\frac{120{,}000}{24.394}} = \sqrt{4919.2} \approx 70.14 \text{ m/s}.$$ > > Step 4 — (ii) Min glide angle: > $$\gamma_{\min} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(2\sqrt{K C_{D0}}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(2\sqrt{0.04993 \times 0.022}\right) = \tan^{-1}(2 \times 0.03314)$$ > $$= \tan^{-1}(0.0663) \approx 3.79^{\circ}.$$ > > Step 5 — (iii) Glide ratio: > $$\frac{L}{D}\bigg|_{\max} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{K C_{D0}}} = \frac{1}{0.0663} \approx 15.08 \;\;(\text{or}\; 15.08:1).$$ > > Step 6 — (iv) Glide range from $h = 4000$ m: > $$R = h \cdot \left(\frac{L}{D}\right)_{\max} = 4000 \times 15.08 \approx 60{,}320 \text{ m} \approx 60.3 \text{ km}.$$ > > **Final Answer:** $V_{\text{BG}} \approx 70.1$ m/s, $\gamma_{\min} \approx 3.79^{\circ}$, glide ratio $\approx 15.08$, $R \approx 60.3$ km. (7/7) --- #### **Option B — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice** **`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 4]`** **(a)** *A glider has $W = 8500$ N, $S = 16$ m², $C_{D0} = 0.016$, $K = 0.040$. Determine the best-glide speed at (i) sea level and (ii) an altitude where $\rho = 0.909$ kg/m³. Comment on how altitude affects the best-glide speed.* **(7 marks)** **(b)** *Show that, for a parabolic drag polar, the speed for the flattest glide is identical to the speed for minimum drag in level flight. What is the operational significance of this identity for a glider pilot?* **(7 marks)** > **Model Solution:** > > **(a) Numerical (7 marks):** > Step 1 — Optimum $C_L$: > $$C_{L_{\text{opt}}} = \sqrt{\frac{C_{D0}}{K}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.016}{0.040}} = \sqrt{0.4} \approx 0.6325.$$ > > Step 2 — (i) $V_{\text{BG}}$ at sea level ($\rho = 1.225$): > $$V_{\text{BG,SL}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 8500}{1.225 \times 16 \times 0.6325}} = \sqrt{\frac{17{,}000}{12.400}} = \sqrt{1371.0} \approx 37.03 \text{ m/s}.$$ > > Step 3 — (ii) $V_{\text{BG}}$ at $\rho = 0.909$: > $$V_{\text{BG,alt}} = \sqrt{\frac{17{,}000}{0.909 \times 16 \times 0.6325}} = \sqrt{\frac{17{,}000}{9.201}} = \sqrt{1847.6} \approx 42.99 \text{ m/s}.$$ > > Step 4 — Comment: > The ratio > $$\frac{V_{\text{BG,alt}}}{V_{\text{BG,SL}}} = \sqrt{\frac{\rho_{\text{SL}}}{\rho_{\text{alt}}}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.225}{0.909}} = \sqrt{1.348} \approx 1.161.$$ > So $V_{\text{BG}}$ *increases* by ~16% as density drops, i.e., the glider must fly *faster true airspeed* to remain on its flattest glide. **(7/7)** > > **(b) Theoretical (7 marks):** > In level, unpowered (or in trimmed glide) equilibrium, the constraint is $L = W$ and $D$ must be balanced by available thrust (zero in a pure glider, equal to $D$ in equilibrium). > Minimum drag in level flight: > $$D_{\min} = q S\, C_{D_{\min}}, \quad \text{with}\; q = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2}.$$ > The lift coefficient for level flight at a given $W$ is $C_L = 2W/(\rho V^{2} S)$. Substituting into the polar: > $$D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S\, (C_{D0} + K C_L^{2}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S\, C_{D0} + \frac{2 K W^{2}}{\rho V^{2} S}.$$ > Differentiating w.r.t. $V$ and equating to zero: > $$\rho V S C_{D0} - \frac{4 K W^{2}}{\rho V^{3} S} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; V^{4} = \frac{4 K W^{2}}{\rho^{2} S^{2} C_{D0}}.$$ > Taking the square root: > $$V_{\min D}^{2} = \frac{2W}{\rho S} \sqrt{\frac{K}{C_{D0}}}.$$ > This is *exactly* the expression for $V_{\text{BG}}^{2}$ derived from the $C_{L_{\text{opt}}}$ approach: > $$V_{\text{BG}}^{2} = \frac{2W}{\rho S C_{L_{\text{opt}}}} = \frac{2W}{\rho S} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{K}{C_{D0}}}.$$ > Hence $V_{\text{BG}} = V_{\min D}$. > > **Operational significance:** A glider pilot who trims for the minimum-rate-of-descent airspeed (often close to $V_{\min D}$ in still air) is *also* trimming for the best-glide airspeed. In a real engine-out scenario, the same airspeed simultaneously maximises endurance *and* range — there is no trade-off penalty in still air. **(7/7)** --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Typically Lose Marks on Glide Problems:** > > 1. **Confusing $V_{\text{BG}}$ with $V_{\text{min-D}}$ vs. $V_{L/D_{\max}}$:** All three are the *same speed* for a parabolic polar. Examiners *will* deduct marks if you state them as separate quantities without explanation. > 2. **Forgetting that $L \neq W$ in a glide:** Many students write $L = W$. In a glide with $\gamma > 0$, only $L = W\cos\gamma$ is correct. **Marks lost: 1 per sub-part.** > 3. **Mixing up $K$ and $C_{D0}$:** $K$ is the *induced*-drag factor (proportional to $1/AR$), $C_{D0}$ is the *parasite*-drag coefficient. Be careful — swapping them gives $(L/D)_{\max}$ off by an order of magnitude. > 4. **Unit-inconsistency in $V_{\text{BG}}$:** Forgetting to convert $W$ from kgf to N (multiply by $g = 9.81$). Many KTU question papers give $W$ in *kg* units — this is a **common 0.5-mark deduction**. > 5. **Stating the wrong independence:** $(L/D)_{\max}$ is *independent of $W$ and $\rho$*. $V_{\text{BG}}$ is *dependent on $W$ and $\rho$*. Students frequently invert these two facts. > 6. **Ignoring compressibility at high altitude:** The parabolic polar is a *low-speed* model. For $V_{\text{BG}} > 0.3 M$ (EAS), the analysis breaks down. Examiners may award partial credit if you note the limitation. > 7. **Polar form not stated:** Always *explicitly* state $C_D = C_{D0} + K C_L^{\,2}$ before differentiating; otherwise the derivation step is incomplete. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Print this section before your exam. The 12-point checklist below covers ≥ 95% of marks available on this topic.** - [ ] **Definition of glide angle:** $\tan\gamma = D/L = C_D/C_L$. - [ ] **Force balance in glide:** $L = W\cos\gamma$ and $D = W\sin\gamma$ — *not* $L = W$. - [ ] **Glide ratio:** $R/h = L/D = C_L/C_D$. - [ ] **Parabolic drag polar:** $C_D = C_{D0} + K C_L^{\,2}$, where $K = 1/(\pi \cdot AR \cdot e)$. - [ ] **Flattest glide = max L/D** — these are mathematically identical statements. - [ ] **Optimum $C_L$:** $C_{L_{\text{opt}}} = \sqrt{C_{D0}/K}$ — derive by setting $d(C_L/C_D)/dC_L = 0$. - [ ] **Max $L/D$ value:** $(L/D)_{\max} = 1/(2\sqrt{K C_{D0}})$. - [ ] **Min glide angle:** $\gamma_{\min} = \tan^{-1}(2\sqrt{K C_{D0}})$. - [ ] **Best glide speed:** $V_{\text{BG}} = \sqrt{2W/(\rho S)} \cdot (K/C_{D0})^{1/4}$ — *increases* with altitude and weight. - [ ] **Independence property:** $(L/D)_{\max}$ and $\gamma_{\min}$ are *independent* of $W$ and $\rho$. - [ ] **Equivalence:** $V_{\text{BG}} = V_{\min D}$ for a propeller-class (variable-thrust) aircraft. - [ ] **Glide range equation:** $R = h \cdot (L/D)$ (in still air, no wind). > **Final Examiner's One-Liner:** *Flattest glide = maximum aerodynamic efficiency = minimum $D/L$ ratio = the unique condition where induced drag equals parasite drag ($2C_{D0} = KC_L^2$).*

topics.title: Take-off and Landing Performance: Length of Runway Required and Aircraft Ground Run
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET753 - AEROSPACE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Take-off and Landing Performance: Length of Runway Required and Aircraft Ground Run

# 🛫 Take-off and Landing Performance: Runway Length & Ground Run

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (PEMET753 / Module 4):** This topic forms the analytical backbone of *Performance* in the Aerospace Engineering program. It directly maps to **CO2** (Apply performance equations to real flight phases) and **CO3** (Analyze take-off and landing metrics under FAR/CS-25 constraints). Expect numerical problems and conceptual derivations in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU Board-Examiner Standard)

**Take-off Ground Run ($s_{TO,GR}$):**
The horizontal distance required by the aircraft to accelerate from a complete standstill (decision speed, $V_0 = 0$) to the *lift-off speed* ($V_{LO}$), at which point the aircraft becomes airborne. This is the **first phase** of the total take-off distance.

$$s_{TO,GR} = \int_{0}^{V_{LO}} \frac{W}{g \left[ T - D - \mu_r (W - L) \right]} \, dV$$

**Landing Ground Run ($s_{L,GR}$):**
The horizontal distance required to decelerate the aircraft from the *touch-down speed* ($V_{TD}$) to a complete standstill, after main-wheel contact, using brakes, spoilers, and reverse thrust.

$$s_{L,GR} = \int_{0}^{V_{TD}} \frac{W}{g \left[ T - D - \mu_b L \right]} \, dV$$

where $T$ (thrust) is negative when reverse thrust is used.

**Length of Runway Required ($s_{RWY}$):**
The certified runway length published by the regulatory authority (FAA / EASA), which must equal or exceed the **balanced field length** for take-off or the **landing distance** for landing, including safety factors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Regulatory Definition (FAR 25 / CS-25):** The *Balanced Field Length (BFL)* is the runway length required such that the decision speed ($V_1$) is the *same* whether the pilot decides to continue take-off (engine failure at $V_1$) or abort (rejected take-off).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Car Push on Ice"

Imagine pushing a heavy loaded shopping cart across a parking lot:

- **Take-off** = You push the cart from rest until it rolls fast enough to coast freely off a small curb. The harder you push (thrust), and the lighter the friction ($\mu_r$), the shorter the distance you need to reach the curb.
