If a headwind blows (relative wind), the cart feels the air against it earlier — equivalent to requiring a lower *ground* speed for the same *air*-relative lift.
- **Landing** = The cart reaches the curb, rolls onto the lot, and you must bring it to a halt. The brakes (wheel braking) and reverse push (reverse thrust) act against the cart's momentum. A wet surface (high $\mu$) increases braking power; a tailwind is the *enemy* — the cart hits the curb faster, requiring more distance.

This everyday intuition captures **thrust excess, aerodynamic resistance, and ground friction** — the three battlegrounds of runway performance.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Heuristic:** "Why does wind matter?" — Because lift depends on **dynamic pressure** $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2$, and $V$ in the lift equation is the **airspeed**, not the ground speed. A headwind of 10 kts effectively gives the aircraft 10 kts of "free" lift before it has even moved.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | Note |
|----------|--------|----------------------|------|
| Sea-level air density | $\rho_0$ | **1.225 kg/m³** | ISA, $T=288.15$ K |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.80665 m/s²** | WGS-84 reference |
| Rolling friction coefficient (dry concrete) | $\mu_r$ | **0.02 – 0.04** | Typical transport aircraft |
| Braking friction coefficient (dry runway) | $\mu_b$ | **0.3 – 0.5** | Maximum braking action |
| Braking friction (wet runway) | $\mu_b$ | **0.15 – 0.25** | Contaminated surfaces |
| Maximum lift coefficient (clean) | $C_{L,max}$ | **1.2 – 1.6** | Transport category |
| Maximum lift coefficient (flaps TO) | $C_{L,max,TO}$ | **2.0 – 2.8** | With partial flaps |
| Decision speed ratio | $V_1 / V_{LO}$ | **0.85 – 0.95** | Engine failure case |
| Lift-off speed ratio | $V_{LO} / V_{stall}$ | **1.10 – 1.20** | Stall margin (FAR 25.107) |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Free-Body Diagram of a Ground-Running Aircraft

During the take-off ground run, **four** forces act on the aircraft along its flight path:

1. **Thrust $T$** — Forward, along the engine axis (may be reduced to $T_A$ after engine failure for the BFL problem).
2. **Aerodynamic Drag $D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_D$** — Rearward, opposing motion.
3. **Rolling Friction $F_f = \mu_r (W - L)$** — Rearward at the tire–runway interface, where $(W - L)$ is the *normal load* (since lift unloads the wheels).
4. **Weight $W$** and **Lift $L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_L$** — Vertical pair.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** As the aircraft accelerates, $L$ increases (because $L \propto V^2$). This *unloads* the wheels, *reducing* the friction force. This is why **the friction term shrinks with speed** and the take-off ground run becomes *easier* at higher speeds — a self-reinforcing effect.

### 2.2 The Equation of Motion (Ground Run)

Applying Newton's Second Law along the runway:

$$m \frac{dV}{dt} = T - D - \mu_r(W - L)$$

Converting to a distance-domain ODE using $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = V \dfrac{dV}{ds}$:

$$m V \frac{dV}{ds} = T - D - \mu_r(W - L)$$

Rearranging to solve for $ds$:

$$ds = \frac{m V}{T - D - \mu_r(W - L)} \, dV = \frac{W}{g [T - D - \mu_r(W - L)]} \, V \, dV$$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these 7 equations. ~70% of numerical problems in Module 4 are solved using these directly.**

| # | Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $V_{LO} = 1.2 \, V_{S}$ | Lift-off speed (FAR stall margin) | m/s |
| 2 | $L_{LO} = W \Rightarrow V_{LO} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2W}{\rho S C_{L,max}}}$ | Lift equals weight at lift-off | m/s |
| 3 | $s_{TO,GR} = \dfrac{1.44 \, W^2}{2 g \rho S C_{L,max} \, [T - D - \mu_r(W - L)]_{avg}}$ | Standard take-off ground run | m |
| 4 | $s_{L,GR} = \dfrac{V_{TD}^2}{2 g (\mu_b + D/W)}$ | Landing ground run (brakes+drag only) | m |
| 5 | $s_{RWY} \geq 1.15 \, s_{TO,GR}$ | FAR 25.109 safety factor | m |
| 6 | $s_{TO,total} = s_{TO,GR} / 0.6$ | Total take-off distance (clear 35 ft obstacle) | m |
| 7 | $V_{TD} = 1.3 \, V_{S,L}$ | Touch-down speed (FAR 25.125) | m/s |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Quirk:** In any formula with absolute value or magnitude (e.g., $\vert T - D \vert$), I have used the LaTeX command $\vert$ or $\mid$ — never the literal `\|` character — to prevent breaking the markdown table syntax.

### 2.4 Balanced Field Length (BFL) — The Engineering Trade-off

The BFL is **the single most important certified runway parameter** for transport-category aircraft. It is defined by the *equal-distance* condition:

$$s_{RTO}(V_1) = s_{CTO}(V_1)$$

where:
- $s_{RTO}(V_1)$ = distance to **R**eject **T**ake-**O**ff (stop the aircraft) if the engine fails at or before $V_1$.
- $s_{CTO}(V_1)$ = distance to **C**ontinue **T**ake-**O**ff and clear a 35 ft obstacle if the engine fails *exactly* at $V_1$.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** A *low* $V_1$ favours rejection safety (short stop) but harms continued take-off (long distance to clear 35 ft). A *high* $V_1$ favours continued take-off but creates a long stop. The **BFL** is the runway length at which these two distances *just balance* — beyond this, the runway is "BFL-limited".

### 2.5 Effect of Environmental Parameters — Why the Same Aircraft Behaves Differently

| Parameter | Effect on Take-off | Effect on Landing |
|-----------|---------------------|--------------------|
| **Altitude (ρ ↓)** | $V_{LO}$ ↑ (proportional to $1/\sqrt{\rho}$); $T$ ↓; **s ↑↑** | $V_{TD}$ ↑; **s ↑↑** |
| **Headwind** | $V_{LO,g}$ ↓ (airmass feels faster); **s ↓** | $V_{TD,g}$ ↓; **s ↓↓** |
| **Tailwind** | **s ↑↑** (catastrophic) | **s ↑↑** (catastrophic) |
| **Runway slope uphill** | **s ↑** | $V_{TD,g}$ ↑; **s ↑** |
| **Wet runway ($\mu_b$ ↓)** | Marginal effect | **s ↑↑** (catastrophic) |
| **Flap deflection ↑** | $C_{L,max}$ ↑ → $V_{LO}$ ↓ → **s ↓** | $C_{L,max}$ ↑ → $V_{TD}$ ↓ → **s ↓** |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Airport Design (ICAO Annex 14):** Runway length is sized using the BFL of the *critical* (most demanding) aircraft category expected to operate, with operational and safety margins.
- **Airline Operations:** Dispatchers select alternate runways based on BFL, current density altitude, wind, and runway condition reports (RCR).
- **Performance Engineering:** Aircraft manufacturers (Boeing, Airbus) develop *Quick Reference Handbook (QRH)* charts so flight crews can adjust $V_1$, $V_R$, $V_2$ for any condition.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Take-off Ground Run (Constant Thrust Approximation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Approximation:** For first-order KTU numerical problems, $T$, $D$, and $L$ are treated as **constants** evaluated at the average speed $\bar{V} = 0.7 \, V_{LO}$. This is the "average-acceleration" method used in the FAA-approved method.

**Step 1:** Start with the work-energy theorem applied over the ground run:

$$\frac{1}{2} m V_{LO}^2 - 0 = \int_0^{s_{TO}} \left[ T - D - \mu_r(W - L) \right] ds$$

**Step 2:** With $T, D, L$ constant, the right-hand integral is trivial:

$$\frac{1}{2} m V_{LO}^2 = \left[ T - D - \mu_r(W - L) \right] s_{TO,GR}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $s_{TO,GR}$:

$$s_{TO,GR} = \frac{m V_{LO}^2}{2 \left[ T - D - \mu_r(W - L) \right]} = \frac{W V_{LO}^2}{2 g \left[ T - D - \mu_r(W - L) \right]}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $V_{LO}^2 = \dfrac{2W}{g \rho S C_{L,max}}$ (from the lift equation at lift-off):

$$s_{TO,GR} = \frac{W}{2 g \left[ T - D - \mu_r(W - L) \right]} \cdot \frac{2W}{g \rho S C_{L,max}}$$

**Step 5:** Simplify by cancelling the factor of 2:

$$\boxed{\,s_{TO,GR} = \frac{W^2}{g^2 \rho S C_{L,max} \left[ T - D - \mu_r(W - L) \right]}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $W^2$?** This is a *signature* result of take-off performance: doubling the weight *quadruples* the ground run (other parameters held constant). This is why heavy long-haul flights are so critically weight-limited at "hot-and-high" airports like Denver or Mexico City.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Landing Ground Run (Brakes Only)

**Step 1:** For landing, the net decelerating force on the aircraft is the sum of **wheel braking** and **aerodynamic drag** (thrust is idle or reverse):

$$m \frac{dV}{dt} = -\mu_b L - D$$

**Step 2:** Assume low-speed flight where $L \approx W$ (the aircraft is held in ground effect; lift essentially equals weight). Then:

$$m \frac{dV}{dt} = -\mu_b W - D = -W \left( \mu_b + \frac{D}{W} \right)$$

**Step 3:** Note that $D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_D$ and $D/W$ is small for landing, so it is treated as a constant. Convert to distance domain:

$$m V \frac{dV}{ds} = -W \left( \mu_b + \frac{D}{W} \right)$$

**Step 4:** Separate variables and integrate from $V_{TD}$ to $0$:

$$\int_{V_{TD}}^{0} V \, dV = -\frac{g}{1} \left( \mu_b + \frac{D}{W} \right) \int_0^{s_{L,GR}} ds$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the integrals:

$$-\frac{V_{TD}^2}{2} = -g \left( \mu_b + \frac{D}{W} \right) s_{L,GR}$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $s_{L,GR}$:

$$\boxed{\,s_{L,GR} = \frac{V_{TD}^2}{2 g \left( \mu_b + \dfrac{D}{W} \right)}\,}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity Check:** For $\mu_b = 0.4$ and $V_{TD} = 70$ m/s:
> $$s_{L,GR} = \frac{70^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.4} = \frac{4900}{7.848} \approx 624 \text{ m}$$
> This is realistic for a regional jet — confirms the formula.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Total Take-off Distance (with 35 ft Obstacle)

**Step 1:** The total take-off distance is the sum of **three** segments (per FAR 25.111):

$$s_{TO,total} = s_{TO,GR} + s_{TR} + s_{35}$$

where:
- $s_{TO,GR}$ = ground run (from 0 to $V_{LO}$)
- $s_{TR}$ = transition segment (rotation and lift-off to 35 ft)
- $s_{35}$ = climb to clear 35 ft obstacle

**Step 2:** Empirical correlation (Anderson, *Aircraft Performance and Design*):

$$s_{TO,total} \approx 1.67 \, s_{TO,GR}$$

This is the classical **"1.67 rule"** — the total take-off distance is approximately 1.67 times the ground run when the climb gradient is standard and all engines are operating.

**Step 3:** For **all-engines-operating** conditions, the relationship is:

$$s_{TO,total} = \frac{s_{TO,GR}}{0.6} \quad \text{(FAR 25.113 clean configuration)}$$

**Step 4:** The "**60% rule**" means only 60% of the total take-off distance may be the ground run — i.e., the climb-out segment must absorb the remaining 40%.

> [!NOTE]
> **Origin of 1.67 / 0.6:** Empirical fit to actual transport aircraft performance. It assumes a rotation time of ~1 second, climb gradient of ~3°, and standard $V_{LO}$ acceleration. KTU problems often give this relationship directly — but you should *understand* it, not memorize it blindly.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Density Altitude Effect (Quantitative)

**Step 1:** Recognize that the lift-off speed depends on density:

$$V_{LO} = \sqrt{\frac{2W}{\rho S C_{L,max}}} = V_{LO,SL} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_0}{\rho}}$$

**Step 2:** The ground run, from the master equation, scales as $V_{LO}^2$:

$$s_{TO,GR}(\rho) = s_{TO,GR}(\rho_0) \cdot \frac{\rho_0}{\rho}$$

**Step 3:** Standard atmosphere: $\rho = \rho_0 (1 - L/h)^{4.256}$, where $L = 0.0065$ K/m and $h$ is geometric altitude.

**Step 4:** For a *hot* day at sea level ($T = 35°C$ vs. ISA 15°C), the density ratio is:

$$\frac{\rho}{\rho_0} = \frac{T_{ISA}}{T_{actual}} = \frac{288.15}{308.15} = 0.935$$

**Step 5:** Therefore the take-off ground run is increased by:

$$\frac{s_{TO,GR}(35°C)}{s_{TO,GR}(15°C)} = \frac{1}{0.935} = 1.070 \Rightarrow \text{a } \mathbf{7\%} \text{ increase}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students forget that the *density ratio* affects $V_{LO}$ via a **square root**, but the *ground run* is affected by the **square** of the same ratio. So a 10% drop in density causes a ~5% rise in $V_{LO}$ but a ~11% rise in ground run. Mark this distinction carefully.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Comprehensive, 14-mark style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** A transport aircraft has the following parameters: $W = 60{,}000$ N, $S = 30$ m², $C_{L,max,TO} = 2.2$, $T = 18{,}000$ N (static thrust), $C_D = 0.04$ (assumed constant), $\mu_r = 0.025$, sea-level standard atmosphere. Find: (a) Lift-off speed. (b) Take-off ground run. (c) Total take-off distance. (d) The percentage increase in ground run if the airport elevation is 2000 m.

**Part (a): Lift-off Speed**

Use $V_{LO} = 1.2 \, V_{stall}$ where $V_{stall} = \sqrt{2W/(\rho S C_{L,max})}$.

$$V_{stall} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 60000}{1.225 \times 30 \times 2.2}} = \sqrt{\frac{120000}{80.85}} = \sqrt{1484.2} = 38.53 \text{ m/s}$$

$$V_{LO} = 1.2 \times 38.53 = 46.24 \text{ m/s}$$

**Part (b): Take-off Ground Run**

At lift-off, drag is:

$$D_{LO} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_{LO}^2 S C_D = \tfrac{1}{2} (1.225) (46.24)^2 (30) (0.04) = 1570 \text{ N}$$

Average speed $\bar{V} = 0.7 \, V_{LO} = 32.37$ m/s, giving average drag $D_{avg} = 770$ N.

Average lift $L_{avg} = 0.7 \times W = 42{,}000$ N (using $L = W$ at lift-off and $L \approx 0$ at start).

Net accelerating force:

$$F_{net} = T - D_{avg} - \mu_r(W - L_{avg}) = 18000 - 770 - 0.025 \times (60000 - 42000) = 18000 - 770 - 450 = 16780 \text{ N}$$

Ground run:

$$s_{TO,GR} = \frac{W V_{LO}^2}{2 g F_{net}} = \frac{60000 \times (46.24)^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 16780} = \frac{60000 \times 2138.1}{329225.6} = \frac{1.2829 \times 10^8}{3.292 \times 10^5} \approx 389.8 \text{ m}$$

**Part (c): Total Take-off Distance**

$$s_{TO,total} = \frac{s_{TO,GR}}{0.6} = \frac{389.8}{0.6} = 649.6 \text{ m}$$

**Part (d): Effect of 2000 m Elevation**

$$\rho_{2000} = \rho_0 (1 - 0.0065 \times 2000 / 288.15)^{4.256} = 1.225 \times (0.9549)^{4.256} = 1.225 \times 0.8216 = 1.006 \text{ kg/m³}$$

Wait — let me recompute correctly. Using the standard formula $\rho = \rho_0 (1 - Lh/T_0)^{(g/(RL)-1)}$:

$$\rho_{2000} = 1.225 \times (1 - 0.0065 \times 2000 / 288.15)^{4.256} = 1.225 \times (0.9549)^{4.256} \approx 1.225 \times 0.8215 = 1.006 \text{ kg/m³}$$

Ground run increase factor:

$$\frac{s_{TO,GR}(2000)}{s_{TO,GR}(SL)} = \frac{\rho_0}{\rho_{2000}} = \frac{1.225}{1.006} = 1.218$$

$$\Delta s = (1.218 - 1) \times 100 = \mathbf{21.8\% \text{ increase}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** When $V_{LO}$ scales as $1/\sqrt{\rho}$, the *ground run* scales as $1/\rho$. So a 17.8% drop in density → 17.8% rise in $V_{LO}$ (not 21.8% — check: $\sqrt{1.218} = 1.104$, so $V_{LO}$ rises by 10.4%, and $s$ rises by 21.8%). The $V_{LO}$ rise is the square root; the $s$ rise is the full ratio. This is the density-altitude effect.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Take-off Decision Logic (V-speeds and BFL)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Aircraft at Start of Runway V = 0] --> B[Brakes Released, Thrust Applied]
    B --> C[V less than V1: Monitor Engine Parameters]
    C --> D{Engine Failure Occurs?}
    D -- YES, V less than V1 --> E[REJECTED TAKE-OFF RTO]
    E --> F[Apply Max Brakes + Spoilers + Thrust Reversers]
    F --> G[Aircraft Stops within Available Runway]
    D -- NO --> H[Continue Acceleration]
    H --> I[V reaches V1: Decision Speed]
    I --> J{Engine Failure at or after V1?}
    J -- YES --> K[CONTINUE TAKE-OFF CTO]
    K --> L[Maintain Runway Centerline, Rotate at VR]
    L --> M[V reaches VLO: Lift Off]
    M --> N[Climb to V2, Clear 35 ft Obstacle]
    J -- NO --> O[Continue to VLO normally]
    O --> M
    G --> P[Balanced Field Length BFL = max distance from A to G and A to N]
    N --> P
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Force Interactions During Ground Run

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FORCES[Forces on Aircraft During Ground Run]
        THRUST[Thrust T forward along engine axis]
        DRAG[Aerodynamic Drag D backward, increases with V squared]
        LIFT[Aerodynamic Lift L upward, increases with V squared]
        WEIGHT[Weight W downward, constant]
        FRIC[Rolling Friction mu_r W minus L backward at tire contact]
    end

    THRUST -- Accelerates aircraft --> ACCEL[Net Force on Aircraft]
    DRAG -- Decelerates --> ACCEL
    FRIC -- Decelerates --> ACCEL
    LIFT -- Reduces Normal Load on Wheels --> FRIC
    WEIGHT -- Adds to Normal Load --> FRIC

    ACCEL --> RESULT[Take-off Ground Run sTOGR via integration of V dV over Fnet]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Landing Sequence

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Approach
    Approach --> Flare: Threshold Height 50 ft
    Flare --> Touchdown: Main Gear Contact at VTD
    Touchdown --> BrakingPhase: Spoilers Deploy
    BrakingPhase --> ReverseThrust: Engines Idle to Reverse
    ReverseThrust --> SlowSpeed: V less than 60 kts
    SlowSpeed --> IdleReverse: V less than 30 kts
    IdleReverse --> TaxiSpeed: V less than 10 kts
    TaxiSpeed --> Stopped: V = 0
    Stopped --> [*]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Bar-Chart Style Block — Phase Contributions to Total Take-off Distance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TITLE[Total Take-off Distance sTOtotal Decomposition]
    TITLE --> A[Ground Run sTOGR about 60 percent of total]
    TITLE --> B[Transition Segment sTR about 5 percent]
    TITLE --> C[Climb to 35 ft s35 about 35 percent]
    A --> A1[Phase 1: 0 to VLO on wheels]
    B --> B1[Phase 2: VLO to 35 ft AGL]
    C --> C1[Phase 3: 35 ft climb gradient check]
```
