## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper]**

**Q1. Define the term "Balanced Field Length" as used in aircraft performance. State the regulatory basis for its use.**

**Model Answer:**
*Balanced Field Length (BFL) is the minimum runway length required for a transport-category aircraft such that, in the event of a critical engine failure at decision speed $V_1$, the distance required to either reject the take-off (RTO) or continue the take-off and clear a 35 ft obstacle (CTO) is equal.* It is the certified takeoff distance per **FAR 25.109 / CS-25**. The BFL forms the basis for runway-length certification and airline dispatch planning. *(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for equal-distance condition, 1 for regulatory citation.)*

---

**Q2. Why is the lift-off speed set at $1.2 \, V_{stall}$ and not exactly equal to $V_{stall}$?**

**Model Answer:**
The lift-off speed is set above stall speed to provide a **stall margin** that accounts for: (i) gust-induced load factor variations, (ii) margin against asymmetric thrust, (iii) engine-out safety, and (iv) handling qualities during rotation. Per **FAR 25.107**, $V_{LO} \geq 1.10 \, V_{S}$ to $1.20 \, V_{S}$ depending on configuration, ensuring a 10–20% speed margin over the stall boundary. *(3 marks — 1 for the 1.2 factor, 1 for reasons, 1 for the regulatory anchor.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / ESE Module 4, Choice-Based]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **(a)** Starting from Newton's second law, derive the expression for the take-off ground run of an aircraft, clearly stating the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A twin-engine transport aircraft has the following data:
> - Take-off mass $W = 55{,}000$ N
> - Wing area $S = 28$ m²
> - $C_{L,max,TO} = 2.0$ at the take-off flap setting
> - Thrust per engine $T = 16{,}000$ N
> - Average drag coefficient $\bar{C}_D = 0.05$
> - Rolling friction coefficient $\mu_r = 0.03$
> - Sea-level standard density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³
>
> Compute: (i) Lift-off speed $V_{LO}$; (ii) Take-off ground run $s_{TO,GR}$; (iii) Total take-off distance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**
*(See Section 3.1 above. Full derivation gives:)*

$$s_{TO,GR} = \frac{W^2}{g^2 \rho S C_{L,max} [T - D - \mu_r(W-L)]}$$

*Assumptions to be stated explicitly:*
1. Constant thrust (no throttle advance delay).
2. Constant drag coefficient (no flap-induced drag rise modeled).
3. Average-altitude method: $L, D$ evaluated at $\bar{V} = 0.7 V_{LO}$.
4. Flat, dry, hard runway (no slope, no wind).
5. Sea-level standard atmosphere.
6. All engines operating.
7. Instantaneous rotation at $V_{LO}$.

*(7 marks: 2 for free-body diagram and Newton's law, 2 for separation of variables and integration, 2 for substitution of $V_{LO}$ and simplification, 1 for listing assumptions.)*

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Lift-off speed:**

$$V_{LO} = 1.2 \sqrt{\frac{2W}{\rho S C_{L,max}}} = 1.2 \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 55000}{1.225 \times 28 \times 2.0}}$$

$$= 1.2 \sqrt{\frac{110000}{68.6}} = 1.2 \sqrt{1603.5} = 1.2 \times 40.04 = 48.05 \text{ m/s}$$

**(ii) Take-off ground run:**

Drag at lift-off:
$$D_{LO} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_{LO}^2 S \bar{C}_D = \tfrac{1}{2} (1.225)(48.05)^2 (28)(0.05) = 1981 \text{ N}$$

Average drag $D_{avg} = 0.7 \times D_{LO} = 1387$ N (using $0.7 V_{LO}$ as average).

Average lift $L_{avg} = 0.7 \times W = 38{,}500$ N.

Net force:
$$F_{net} = 2 \times 16000 - 1387 - 0.03 \times (55000 - 38500) = 32000 - 1387 - 495 = 30118 \text{ N}$$

Ground run:
$$s_{TO,GR} = \frac{W V_{LO}^2}{2 g F_{net}} = \frac{55000 \times (48.05)^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 30118} = \frac{55000 \times 2308.8}{591,000} = \frac{1.2698 \times 10^8}{5.91 \times 10^5} \approx 214.9 \text{ m}$$

**(iii) Total take-off distance:**

$$s_{TO,total} = \frac{s_{TO,GR}}{0.6} = \frac{214.9}{0.6} = \mathbf{358.2 \text{ m}}$$

*(7 marks: 1.5 each for parts i, ii, iii; 1 for the average-altitude method application, 1 for the 0.6 rule; 0.5 for units consistency.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative choice for the same module*

> **(a)** Derive the expression for the landing ground run of an aircraft, assuming brakes are the primary decelerating force. State clearly the assumptions and limitations of the derivation. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** An aircraft lands at a touch-down speed of $V_{TD} = 65$ m/s on a dry concrete runway with $\mu_b = 0.4$. The aircraft weight is $W = 50{,}000$ N and the ratio $D/W = 0.05$. Find: (i) the landing ground run distance; (ii) the additional distance required if the runway is wet ($\mu_b = 0.2$); (iii) the effect of a 10 kts tailwind on the touch-down ground speed if the approach airspeed is unchanged. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**
*(See Section 3.2 above. Final expression:)*

$$s_{L,GR} = \frac{V_{TD}^2}{2g(\mu_b + D/W)}$$

*Key assumptions:*
1. Lift ≈ Weight throughout landing roll ($L \approx W$).
2. No reverse thrust contribution (or absorbed into effective friction).
3. Constant $\mu_b$ (no anti-skid modulation).
4. No wind.
5. Level runway.
6. Constant $D/W$ ratio.

*Limitations:*
- Real anti-skid systems vary $\mu_b$ continuously.
- Reverse thrust is highly effective and must be added.
- Ground effect changes $C_L$ and $C_D$ near the ground.

*(7 marks: 2 derivation, 2 substitution, 2 assumptions, 1 limitations.)*

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Landing ground run (dry):**

$$s_{L,GR,dry} = \frac{65^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times (0.4 + 0.05)} = \frac{4225}{8.829} = \mathbf{478.5 \text{ m}}$$

**(ii) Landing ground run (wet):**

$$s_{L,GR,wet} = \frac{65^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times (0.2 + 0.05)} = \frac{4225}{4.905} = 861.4 \text{ m}$$

Additional distance = $861.4 - 478.5 = \mathbf{382.9 \text{ m}}$ (an 80% increase — catastrophic for runway overruns).

**(iii) Tailwind effect:**

A 10 kts tailwind = 10 × 0.5144 = 5.14 m/s increase in ground speed.

If approach *airspeed* is unchanged, then ground speed at touch-down is $65 + 5.14 = 70.14$ m/s (relative to ground; the airspeed is the same 65 m/s relative to airmass).

Revised ground run (dry):
$$s_{L,GR,tw} = \frac{70.14^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.45} = \frac{4919.6}{8.829} = 557.2 \text{ m}$$

Increase = $557.2 - 478.5 = 78.7$ m (**16.4% increase**).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (3 Common Mistakes):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 1.2 / 1.3 stall margin multiplier.** Many students compute $V_{LO} = V_{stall}$ directly. The 1.2 (take-off) and 1.3 (landing) factors are *regulatory minimums* — missing them costs 1–2 marks per question.
>
> 2. **Using $\bar{V} = 0.5 V_{LO}$ instead of $0.7 V_{LO}$.** The average-speed method uses $0.7 V_{LO}$ as the speed at which $L$ and $D$ are evaluated. Using $0.5$ underestimates the run by ~15–20%.
>
> 3. **Mixing up the 0.6 rule and the 1.67 rule.** Both are correct: $s_{TO,total} = 1.67 \, s_{TO,GR} = s_{TO,GR} / 0.6$. Use the form the question gives. Mixing them up in the same problem loses 2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this section. Re-read it 24 hours before the exam.**

✅ **Master Definitions (3 marks guaranteed):**
- Take-off Ground Run ($s_{TO,GR}$) — distance from rest to $V_{LO}$.
- Total Take-off Distance ($s_{TO,total}$) — distance to clear 35 ft obstacle.
- Landing Ground Run ($s_{L,GR}$) — distance from $V_{TD}$ to stop.
- Balanced Field Length (BFL) — runway where RTO and CTO distances are equal.
- Decision Speed ($V_1$), Rotation Speed ($V_R$), Take-off Safety Speed ($V_2$).

✅ **Master the 7 Equations (14-mark questions):**
- $V_{LO} = 1.2 V_S$ (FAR stall margin)
- $V_{TD} = 1.3 V_{S,L}$ (FAR landing margin)
- $V_{LO} = \sqrt{2W / (\rho S C_{L,max})}$ (lift = weight)
- $s_{TO,GR} = W^2 / [g^2 \rho S C_{L,max} (T - D - \mu_r(W-L))]$ (master formula)
- $s_{L,GR} = V_{TD}^2 / [2g(\mu_b + D/W)]$ (brakes + drag)
- $s_{TO,total} = s_{TO,GR} / 0.6 = 1.67 \, s_{TO,GR}$ (FAR 25.113)
- $s_{RWY} \geq 1.15 \, s_{TO}$ (safety factor for dispatch)

✅ **Master the 6 Standard Assumptions (1 mark per problem):**
1. Sea-level standard atmosphere (or density specified).
2. Flat, dry, hard runway.
3. No wind.
4. Constant thrust, $C_D$, $\mu_r$, $\mu_b$.
5. Average-altitude method ($L, D$ at $0.7 V_{LO}$).
6. All engines operating (unless "engine failure" specified).

✅ **Master the 4 Environmental Effects (always ask "what changed?"):**
- Altitude (ρ ↓) → $V_{LO}$ ↑, $s$ ↑↑.
- Headwind → $V_{LO,g}$ ↓, $s$ ↓.
- Tailwind → $V_{LO,g}$ ↑, $s$ ↑↑.
- Wet runway → $\mu_b$ ↓, $s$ ↑↑ (landing only).

✅ **Master the Units and Conversion (3 marks of "free" credit):**
- 1 knot = 0.5144 m/s.
- 1 ft = 0.3048 m.
- $W$ in N, $\rho$ in kg/m³, $S$ in m² → $V$ in m/s, $s$ in m.
- $W = mg$ → $m = W/g$ for Newton's Law.

✅ **The 3-Second Exam Sanity Check:**
- $V_{LO}$ for a 60 kN transport ≈ 45–50 m/s (≈ 95–100 kts). ✓
- $s_{TO,GR}$ for the same ≈ 400–600 m on a standard day. ✓
- $s_{L,GR}$ for the same ≈ 500–700 m with $\mu_b = 0.4$. ✓
- If your answer is off by an order of magnitude, **re-check the equation** before submitting.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Pro-Tip from KTU Valuation Patterns:** In 14-mark questions, examiners allocate 2–3 marks for a *clear free-body diagram*. Always draw it, label all four forces, and indicate the direction of motion. Students who skip the diagram routinely lose 1–2 marks even when their algebra is correct.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Circling Flight: Radius of Tightest Turn
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET753 - AEROSPACE ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Circling Flight: Radius of Tightest Turn ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) **Circling Flight** (also called **Coordinated Turn** or **Steady Turn**) is a steady, symmetric, horizontal maneuver in which the aircraft's flight path describes a circle of constant radius at a constant altitude, with the wings banked at a fixed angle and the slip/skid ball centered. The **Radius of the Tightest Turn** ($R_{min}$) is the smallest possible radius of a horizontal circular flight path that an aircraft can sustain in level flight without stalling. It corresponds to the **maximum permissible bank angle** the aircraft can hold while maintaining lift sufficient to balance weight. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview Imagine a child swinging a ball tied to a string in a horizontal circle. To make the ball trace a **smaller circle** (a tighter turn), you must either: 1. **Swing the ball faster** (more velocity, more centripetal pull), or 2. **Shorten the string** (smaller radius directly). In an aircraft, we cannot change the "string length" mid-air, so to tighten the turn we must **bank the wings** — this tilts the lift vector, and its horizontal component pulls the aircraft inward (like the string tension). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** Bank angle converts *vertical* lift into a combination of *vertical support* and *horizontal centripetal pull*. The steeper the bank, the tighter the turn — until the wing reaches its stall limit, beyond which it stops flying. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ - **Sea-level air density (ISA):** $\rho_0 = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - **Turn rate unit:** rad/s (often expressed in deg/s) - **Load factor (g's):** dimensionless ratio $n = L/W$ > [!NOTE] > A 60° bank doubles the load on the wings. A 75° bank quadruples it. Pilots feel "heavier" because their apparent weight grows with $n = 1/\cos(\phi)$. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Physics of a Coordinated Level Turn In a **steady, coordinated, level turn**, three forces act on the aircraft (lift $L$, weight $W$, thrust $T$) along with the centripetal acceleration toward the center of the turn. Resolving forces: **Vertical equilibrium** (no vertical acceleration): $$L \cos\phi = W$$ **Horizontal (radial) equilibrium** (centripetal force toward center): $$L \sin\phi = \frac{W}{g} \cdot \frac{V^2}{R}$$ Dividing the two equations eliminates $L$ and $W$: $$\tan\phi = \frac{V^2}{g R}$$ ### 2.2 Why Does Bank Angle Tighten the Turn? - As $\phi \to 90°$, $\tan\phi \to \infty$, so $R \to 0$ in theory. - In practice, lift grows as $L = W/\cos\phi$. When $\phi$ is large, the wing must produce **more lift** at the same speed, demanding a **higher angle of attack** — until it reaches the **stall angle**. - Therefore, the **aerodynamic stall** sets the upper bound on bank angle in level flight. ### 2.3 The Two Limits to Maximum Bank Angle | Limit Type | Source of Limit | Maximum Bank Angle | |------------|----------------|--------------------| | **Aerodynamic (Stall)** | Wing reaches $C_{L_{max}}$ | $\cos\phi_{max} = (V_s/V)^2$ | | **Structural** | Airframe certified limit load | $\phi_{max} = \arccos(1/n_{max})$ | For civil aviation and KTU problems, the **stall-limited bank angle** is the binding constraint. ### 2.4 Step-by-Step Logic 1. **Define load factor** $n = L/W$. From vertical equilibrium: $n = 1/\cos\phi$. 2. **Stall condition:** $W = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_s^2 S C_{L_{max}}$ and the in-turn lift must satisfy $L = nW = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_{L_{max}}$ (with $C_L$ capped at $C_{L_{max}}$). 3. **Divide** the two lift equations → $\dfrac{nW}{W} = \dfrac{V^2}{V_s^2}$, giving $n_{max} = (V/V_s)^2$. 4. **Solve** for $\phi_{max}$: $\cos\phi_{max} = 1/n_{max} = (V_s/V)^2$. 5. **Insert** into $R = V^2/(g\tan\phi)$ to obtain $R_{min}$. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Boundary / Notes | |---|----------|---------|-------|------------------| | 1 | Lift (general) | $L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_L$ | N | Standard lift equation | | 2 | Load factor | $n = L/W = 1/\cos\phi$ | dimensionless | $n \geq 1$ in level turn | | 3 | Turn rate | $\omega = (g \tan\phi)/V$ | rad/s | Often converted to deg/s: multiply by $180/\pi$ | | 4 | Turn radius | $R = V^2/(g \tan\phi)$ | m | Direct from radial equilibrium | | 5 | Stall speed | $V_s = \sqrt{2W/(\rho S C_{L_{max}})}$ | m/s | Wing-level stall | | 6 | Max bank angle (stall) | $\cos\phi_{max} = (V_s/V)^2$ | rad | Decisive for tightest turn | | 7 | Max load factor (stall) | $n_{max} = (V/V_s)^2$ | dimensionless | Equivalently $n_{max} = 1/\cos\phi_{max}$ | | 8 | Min turn radius | $R_{min} = V^2/(g \tan\phi_{max})$ | m | The tightest possible turn | | 9 | $R_{min}$ (alt form) | $R_{min} = V_s^2 \, n_{max} / (g \sqrt{n_{max}^2 - 1})$ | m | Using $\tan\phi_{max} = \sqrt{n_{max}^2 - 1}$ | | 10 | Min turn radius (final) | $R_{min} = V^2 / \sqrt{g^2 (V/V_s)^4 - g^2}$ | m | Pure form in $V, V_s, g$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sanity check:** When $V = V_s$, $\cos\phi_{max} = 1$ so $\phi_{max} = 0°$ and $R_{min} \to \infty$. This makes physical sense: at the stall speed you cannot bank at all without stalling. As $V \gg V_s$, $\phi_{max} \to 90°$ and $R_{min} \to V^2/g$ (the centripetal acceleration becomes $g$). ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Fighter maneuverability:** Minimum-radius turn is a key performance metric in air combat. Modern fighters reach $n_{max} \approx 9$ at $V \approx 3V_s$. - **Air traffic control:** ATC uses standard-rate turns (3 deg/s, i.e., 2 minutes for a full circle) for traffic separation. - **Civil certification:** The aircraft's $V_A$ (maneuvering speed) is set such that at $V_A$, full deflection of controls just reaches $C_{L_{max}}$ at the design limit load factor. - **Aerobatic limits:** Aerobatic category aircraft are certified to higher limit load factors (+6/-3 g). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Turn Radius and Turn Rate from Force Balance **Starting point:** A symmetric aircraft in a steady, coordinated, level turn at true airspeed $V$ and bank angle $\phi$ (measured from the horizontal). **Step 1 — Free-body diagram equations.** The aircraft experiences weight $W$ acting vertically downward and lift $L$ acting perpendicular to the wings (i.e., tilted by $\phi$ from vertical). For steady level flight (no vertical climb): $$L \cos\phi - W = 0 \quad \text{(vertical)}$$ $$L \sin\phi = \frac{W}{g} \cdot \frac{V^2}{R} \quad \text{(radial, toward turn center)}$$ **Step 2 — Solve the vertical equation for $L$.** $$L = \frac{W}{\cos\phi}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute into the radial equation.** $$\frac{W}{\cos\phi} \cdot \sin\phi = \frac{W}{g} \cdot \frac{V^2}{R}$$ The $W$ cancels cleanly: $$\frac{\sin\phi}{\cos\phi} = \tan\phi = \frac{V^2}{g R}$$ **Step 4 — Invert to obtain turn radius.** $$\boxed{R = \frac{V^2}{g \tan\phi}}$$ **Step 5 — Derive turn rate $\omega = V/R$.** $$\omega = \frac{V}{R} = \frac{V \cdot g \tan\phi}{V^2} = \frac{g \tan\phi}{V}$$ $$\boxed{\omega = \frac{g \tan\phi}{V}}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Bank Angle (Stall-Limited) **Step 1 — Stall speed equation.** At stall in wings-level flight, the wing produces $C_{L_{max}}$ at speed $V_s$: $$W = \frac{1}{2}\rho V_s^2 \, S \, C_{L_{max}}$$ **Step 2 — In-turn lift at speed $V$.** In a level turn at bank angle $\phi$, the aircraft needs lift $L = W/\cos\phi$. The available lift at speed $V$ is: $$L_{available} = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 \, S \, C_L$$ **Step 3 — Apply the stall ceiling $C_L \leq C_{L_{max}}$.** For the turn to be sustained without stalling: $$\frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 \, S \, C_{L_{max}} \geq \frac{W}{\cos\phi}$$ $$\frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 \, S \, C_{L_{max}} \geq \frac{1}{\cos\phi} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho V_s^2 \, S \, C_{L_{max}}$$ **Step 4 — Cancel common factors.** After dividing both sides by $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho S C_{L_{max}}$: $$V^2 \geq \frac{V_s^2}{\cos\phi}$$ **Step 5 — Solve for the largest permissible bank angle.** $$\cos\phi_{max} = \left(\frac{V_s}{V}\right)^2$$ $$\boxed{\phi_{max} = \arccos\left[\left(\frac{V_s}{V}\right)^2\right]}$$ ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Radius of the Tightest Turn **Step 1 — Start from the general turn radius.** $$R = \frac{V^2}{g \tan\phi}$$ **Step 2 — Insert $\phi = \phi_{max}$ and use $\cos\phi_{max} = (V_s/V)^2$.** Compute $\sin\phi_{max}$ and $\tan\phi_{max}$ using $\sin^2 + \cos^2 = 1$: $$\sin\phi_{max} = \sqrt{1 - \cos^2\phi_{max}} = \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{V_s}{V}\right)^4}$$ $$\tan\phi_{max} = \frac{\sin\phi_{max}}{\cos\phi_{max}} = \frac{\sqrt{1 - (V_s/V)^4}}{(V_s/V)^2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{V}{V_s}\right)^4 - 1}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute into $R$.** $$R_{min} = \frac{V^2}{g \sqrt{(V/V_s)^4 - 1}}$$ **Step 4 — Equivalent form using load factor.** Define $n_{max} = (V/V_s)^2$. Then $\tan\phi_{max} = \sqrt{n_{max}^2 - 1}$, so: $$\boxed{R_{min} = \frac{V_s^2 \, n_{max}}{g \sqrt{n_{max}^2 - 1}}}$$ **Numerical sanity check.** Take $V = 2 V_s$. Then $n_{max} = 4$, $\tan\phi_{max} = \sqrt{16 - 1} = \sqrt{15} \approx 3.873$, so $\phi_{max} \approx 75.5°$. And $R_{min} = (2V_s)^2/(g \cdot 3.873) = 4V_s^2/(3.873 g) \approx 1.033\,V_s^2/g$. If $V_s = 70$ m/s and $g = 9.81$ m/s², $R_{min} \approx 1.033 \times 4900/9.81 \approx 516$ m — a very tight turn. ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Table — Bank Angle, $n$, and Radius | Bank $\phi$ | $\cos\phi$ | Load Factor $n = 1/\cos\phi$ | $\tan\phi$ | $R$ (relative to $V^2/g$) | |------------:|-----------:|-----------------------------:|-----------:|--------------------------:| | 0° | 1.000 | 1.00 | 0.000 | $\infty$ | | 15° | 0.966 | 1.04 | 0.268 | $3.73\,V^2/g$ | | 30° | 0.866 | 1.15 | 0.577 | $1.73\,V^2/g$ | | 45° | 0.707 | 1.41 | 1.000 | $1.00\,V^2/g$ | | 60° | 0.500 | 2.00 | 1.732 | $0.577\,V^2/g$ | | 75° | 0.259 | 3.86 | 3.732 | $0.268\,V^2/g$ | | 80° | 0.174 | 5.76 | 5.671 | $0.176\,V^2/g$ | This table makes the central trade-off visible: doubling $n$ roughly halves $R$, but the wing load grows rapidly. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Turning-Flight Analysis The figure below maps the analytical pipeline from aircraft parameters to the tightest-turn performance metrics. Each block represents a transformation of physical quantities and is connected by labelled arrows indicating the mathematical operation applied. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[INPUT BLOCK
Aircraft Parameters
Wing Area S, Weight W, Density rho, C_L_max, True Airspeed V, Stall Speed V_s] B[FORCE BALANCE BLOCK
Vertical: L cos phi equals W
Radial: L sin phi equals W V squared divided by g R] C[GEOMETRY BLOCK
Derive R equals V squared over g tan phi
Derive omega equals g tan phi over V] D[LOAD FACTOR BLOCK
n equals L over W equals 1 over cos phi
n max equals V squared divided by V_s squared] E[STALL LIMIT BLOCK
cos phi max equals V_s to the fourth over V to the fourth
phi max equals arccos of V_s squared over V squared] F[TIGHTEST TURN OUTPUT
R min equals V squared divided by g tan phi max
R min equals V_s squared times n max divided by g times sqrt of n max squared minus 1] G[PERFORMANCE MAP
Turn rate omega, Load factor n, Bank phi, Radius R] A --> B B --> C C --> D A --> D D --> E C --> E E --> F D --> F F --> G C --> G ``` ### 4.2 Reading the Diagram - The **Input Block** supplies both the geometric parameters ($S, W, \rho, C_{L_{max}}$) and the flight condition ($V$ and $V_s$). - The **Force Balance Block** uses Newton's second law in two perpendicular directions to yield the universal turn relation $R = V^2/(g\tan\phi)$. - The **Load Factor Block** converts bank angle into pilot-perceived g-loading. - The **Stall Limit Block** is the *gatekeeper* — it caps $\phi$ based on how far above $V_s$ the aircraft is flying. - The **Tightest Turn Output** joins all three branches to deliver $R_{min}$, the headline result of this module. - The **Performance Map** aggregates every derived quantity into a single visualization-ready dataset. > [!NOTE] > **Why a block diagram instead of a free-body sketch?** A Mermaid block diagram maps the *causal and logical structure* of the analysis with absolute precision. The two force-balance equations themselves are universal and well documented in every textbook, so the analytical pipeline (inputs → physics → limits → outputs) carries the highest pedagogical value for KTU board preparation. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set A]** Define **load factor** in a turning flight. How is it related to the bank angle? **Model Answer:** Load factor $n$ is defined as the ratio of the lift produced by the wing to the weight of the aircraft, $n = L/W$. In a coordinated level turn at bank angle $\phi$, the vertical component of lift must balance weight, so $L\cos\phi = W$, giving: $$n = \frac{1}{\cos\phi}$$ Thus, as $\phi$ increases from $0°$ to $90°$, $n$ rises from $1$ to $\infty$. A 60° bank implies $n = 2$, meaning the wings carry twice the aircraft's weight and the pilot feels "2 g". **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]** What is the **aerodynamic limitation** on the radius of the tightest turn in level flight? **Model Answer:** The radius cannot be made arbitrarily small because, as bank angle $\phi$ increases, the wing must produce lift $L = W/\cos\phi$ at the same speed. When the required $C_L$ exceeds $C_{L_{max}}$, the wing stalls. The maximum bank angle is therefore: $$\cos\phi_{max} = \left(\frac{V_s}{V}\right)^2$$ The radius of the tightest turn is $R_{min} = V^2/(g \tan\phi_{max})$. So the **stall speed of the aircraft** (and the chosen flight speed $V$) sets the lower bound on turn radius. ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 14 Marks Each) --- #### **Question A [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]** **(a)** Derive the expression for the **radius of a turn** and the **rate of turn** for an aircraft executing a coordinated, steady, level turn. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A jet trainer weighs $W = 45{,}000$ N, has a wing area $S = 30$ m², and $C_{L_{max}} = 1.6$. It flies at sea level ($\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³) at $V = 200$ m/s. Compute: - (i) Stall speed $V_s$. - (ii) Maximum bank angle $\phi_{max}$ for level flight. - (iii) Radius of the tightest turn $R_{min}$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation:** (Write all five steps from Section 3.1.) 1. Vertical equilibrium: $L\cos\phi = W$. 2. Radial equilibrium: $L\sin\phi = (W/g)(V^2/R)$. 3. Divide: $\tan\phi = V^2/(gR)$. 4. Hence $R = V^2/(g\tan\phi)$. 5. Turn rate $\omega = V/R = g\tan\phi/V$. **Assumptions:** - Steady, symmetric, coordinated, level turn. - Thrust equals drag (no acceleration along path). - Constant altitude, constant airspeed. - Small perturbation, rigid body. **(b) Numerical solution:** **(i) Stall speed:** $$V_s = \sqrt{\frac{2W}{\rho S C_{L_{max}}}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 45{,}000}{1.225 \times 30 \times 1.6}}$$ $$= \sqrt{\frac{90{,}000}{58.8}} = \sqrt{1530.6} \approx 39.12 \text{ m/s}$$ **(ii) Maximum bank angle:** $$\cos\phi_{max} = \left(\frac{V_s}{V}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{39.12}{200}\right)^2 = (0.1956)^2 \approx 0.03826$$ $$\phi_{max} = \arccos(0.03826) \approx 87.81°$$ **(iii) Radius of tightest turn:** $$\tan\phi_{max} = \sqrt{(V/V_s)^4 - 1} = \sqrt{(5.112)^4 - 1} = \sqrt{683.1 - 1} = \sqrt{682.1} \approx 26.12$$ $$R_{min} = \frac{V^2}{g \tan\phi_{max}} = \frac{(200)^2}{9.81 \times 26.12} = \frac{40{,}000}{256.2} \approx 156.1 \text{ m}$$ **Verification via load-factor form:** $n_{max} = (V/V_s)^2 = 26.13$. $R_{min} = V_s^2 n_{max}/(g\sqrt{n_{max}^2 - 1}) = (1530.6 \times 26.13)/(9.81 \times 26.12) \approx 156.1$ m ✓ --- #### **Question B [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Alternate Choice]** **(a)** With the aid of a **force diagram**, derive the equilibrium equations for an aircraft in a **steady coordinated level turn**. Show that the load factor is $n = 1/\cos\phi$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An aircraft has $n_{max} = 5$ at sea level. If the load factor equals $1/\cos\phi$, derive the relationship between turn radius $R$ and true airspeed $V$. Determine the **minimum turn radius** when $V = 180$ m/s. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a)** (Draw the standard free-body diagram mentally: lift $L$ tilted by $\phi$, weight $W$ vertical.) - Vertical: $L\cos\phi = W$ - Radial: $L\sin\phi = (W/g)(V^2/R)$ - Dividing: $\tan\phi = V^2/(gR)$ - Load factor definition: $n = L/W$. - Substituting $L = W/\cos\phi$: $n = (W/\cos\phi)/W = 1/\cos\phi$. ∎ **(b)** From (a): $\tan\phi = V^2/(gR)$ and $n = 1/\cos\phi$. Also, $\tan^2\phi = (1 - \cos^2\phi)/\cos^2\phi = (1 - 1/n^2)/(1/n^2) = n^2 - 1$. So: $$\frac{V^4}{g^2 R^2} = n^2 - 1 \quad\Rightarrow\quad R = \frac{V^2}{g\sqrt{n^2 - 1}}$$ For $n_{max} = 5$: $$R_{min} = \frac{V^2}{g\sqrt{n_{max}^2 - 1}} = \frac{(180)^2}{9.81 \times \sqrt{25 - 1}} = \frac{32{,}400}{9.81 \times 4.899}$$ $$R_{min} = \frac{32{,}400}{48.06} \approx 674.2 \text{ m}$$ **Verification of bank angle:** $\cos\phi_{max} = 1/n_{max} = 0.2 \Rightarrow \phi_{max} \approx 78.46°$. $\tan\phi_{max} \approx 4.899$. $R_{min} = 32{,}400/(9.81 \times 4.899) \approx 674$ m ✓ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Forgetting to subtract 1 inside the radical:** Writing $R_{min} = V^2/(g\,n_{max})$ instead of $V^2/(g\sqrt{n_{max}^2 - 1})$ is a common error and costs 2–3 marks. > 2. **Confusing stall speed with flight speed:** The formula $\cos\phi_{max} = (V_s/V)^2$ requires $V \geq V_s$. Plugging $V < V_s$ yields an impossible $\cos\phi_{max} > 1$. > 3. **Mixing up degrees and radians in $\omega$:** KTU often expects $\omega$ in rad/s. Always state the unit and convert: $1$ rad/s $= 57.296$ deg/s. > 4. **Ignoring thrust/drag balance:** A "steady" turn requires $T = D$. Examiners sometimes deduct marks if this assumption is not stated explicitly. > 5. **Skipping the free-body step:** Even when the derivation looks obvious, the KTU board mandates a labelled force diagram or a clear two-line force resolution in part (a). Missing it forfeits 2 marks. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Coordinated turn assumption:** No slip, no skid; $L$ lies in the plane of symmetry; bank angle $\phi$ is the only tilt. - **Core radial balance:** $L\sin\phi = (W/g)(V^2/R)$ is the single most important equation in this module — memorise it. - **Universal turn-radius formula:** $R = V^2/(g\tan\phi)$ — derived once, used everywhere. - **Turn rate:** $\omega = g\tan\phi/V$ — increases with bank, decreases with speed. - **Load factor:** $n = 1/\cos\phi$ — a 60° bank = 2 g, a 75° bank ≈ 3.86 g. - **Stall-limited bank angle:** $\cos\phi_{max} = (V_s/V)^2$ — the gatekeeper of the tightest turn. - **Tightest turn radius:** $R_{min} = V^2/(g\tan\phi_{max}) = V_s^2 n_{max}/(g\sqrt{n_{max}^2 - 1})$. - **Limiting cases:** - At $V = V_s$: $\phi_{max} = 0$, $R_{min} \to \infty$. - At $V \gg V_s$: $\phi_{max} \to 90°$, $R_{min} \to V^2/g$ (max centripetal accel = $g$). - **Structural limit:** If $n_{max}$ is set by airframe certification, use $\cos\phi_{max} = 1/n_{max}$ instead of the stall formula. - **KTU board trick:** When given $V_s$ and $V$, immediately compute $V/V_s$, square it to get $n_{max}$, and then take $\sqrt{n_{max}^2 - 1}$ — this three-step sequence unlocks almost every numerical. - **Units to memorise:** $g = 9.81$ m/s² (or 32.2 ft/s²); $\omega$ in rad/s; $R$ in metres; bank angle in degrees for interpretation, radians for calculation.

topics.title: Jet and Rocket Assisted Take-off and High Lift Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET753 - AEROSPACE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Jet and Rocket Assisted Take-off and High Lift Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Jet Assisted Take-Off (JATO) and Rocket Assisted Take-Off (RATO)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> **Jet Assisted Take-Off (JATO)** is an auxiliary propulsion system in which one or more auxiliary jet units (typically rocket motors) are attached to an aircraft to provide **supplementary thrust during the take-off run**, thereby reducing the ground roll distance and allowing take-off from short runways or with heavier payloads.
> 
> **Rocket Assisted Take-Off (RATO)** is essentially synonymous with JATO when the auxiliary units are pure rocket motors (carrying both oxidizer and fuel onboard, unlike air-breathing jet engines).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine pushing a heavy shopping cart from a standstill. The initial push (the "breakaway") is the hardest part. Once it's rolling, you need less force to keep it going. Now imagine a small firecracker taped to the back of the cart, firing for a few seconds — that sudden extra push gets the cart moving much faster in a much shorter distance. **JATO/RATO is exactly this principle applied to aircraft**: a short-duration "kick" of thrust from a rocket motor (or auxiliary jet) is delivered only during the critical take-off phase, dramatically shortening the ground roll without the permanent penalty of carrying that heavy booster all flight long.

### 1.2 High Lift Devices (HLDs)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> **High Lift Devices** are aerodynamic surfaces (typically flaps, slats, and slots) that can be deployed or extended from the basic wing planform to **increase the maximum lift coefficient ($C_{L,\max}$)** of the aircraft, enabling safer take-off and landing at lower approach speeds from shorter runways.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Hold your hand out of a moving car, palm flat — you feel some lift/drag. Now slightly curl your fingers down at the trailing edge and angle your palm up a bit. Suddenly the force on your hand grows enormously, and small vortices form at the tips of your curled fingers. **High Lift Devices work the same way** — by reshaping the wing's curvature (camber) and managing the airflow at low speeds, they essentially "curl the air" to extract much more lift than the clean wing could ever produce.

### Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard sea-level air density:** $\rho_0 = \mathbf{1.225 \ kg/m^3}$
- **Standard sea-level speed of sound:** $a_0 = \mathbf{340.29 \ m/s}$
- **Specific impulse range for solid propellants:** $I_{sp} \approx \mathbf{200\text{–}250 \ s}$
- **Typical JATO thrust augmentation duration:** $t_b \approx \mathbf{10\text{–}15 \ s}$
- **Typical $C_{L,\max}$ for clean wing:** $\mathbf{1.2 \text{–} 1.6}$
- **Typical $C_{L,\max}$ with full HLD deployment:** $\mathbf{2.5 \text{–} 3.5+}$

### Classification of Assisted Take-Off Methods

| System | Propellant | Oxidizer Source | Typical $I_{sp}$ (s) | Typical Use Case |
|:------:|:----------:|:---------------:|:--------------------:|:----------------|
| Solid JATO | Solid grain | Internal (carried) | 200–250 | Military, carrier aircraft |
| Liquid JATO | Liquid bipropellant | Internal (carried) | 250–310 | Heavy bombers, space shuttle |
| Water-injection JATO | Water + JP fuel | Air-breathing | — | Turbojet thrust augmentation |
| Afterburner JATO | JP fuel | Air-breathing | 1500+ (effective) | High-performance fighters |

> [!NOTE]
> A **"jet" in JATO** historically refers to the *jet of exhaust gases*, not necessarily an air-breathing turbojet. Hence JATO and RATO are often used interchangeably in older literature.

---

### Classification of High Lift Devices

| Location on Wing | Device | Primary Effect |
|:----------------:|:------:|:--------------:|
| Trailing edge | Plain flap | Increases camber |
| Trailing edge | Split flap | Increases camber + sharp trailing edge |
| Trailing edge | Slotted flap | Delays flow separation |
| Trailing edge | Fowler flap | Increases wing area + camber |
| Trailing edge | Triple-slotted flap | Maximum $C_L$ augmentation |
| Leading edge | Fixed slat | Delays stall via slot effect |
| Leading edge | Movable slat | Variable slot gap |
| Leading edge | Krueger flap | Increases leading edge camber |
| Wing tip / upper | Vortex generators | Boundary layer re-energization |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Assisted Take-Off is Required

Aircraft take-off is governed by Newton's second law applied to the aircraft on the ground:

$$T - D - \mu(W - L) = \frac{W}{g}\frac{dV}{dt}$$

Where:
- $T$ = available thrust (N)
- $D$ = aerodynamic drag (N)
- $W$ = aircraft weight (N)
- $L$ = lift (N)
- $\mu$ = rolling friction coefficient ($\approx 0.02$ for rubber on concrete)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration ($9.81 \ m/s^2$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Take-Off Bottleneck:** The aircraft must accelerate from $V = 0$ to $V_{LOF}$ (lift-off speed) over a finite runway length. The ground run $s_{TO}$ is inversely proportional to the **net accelerating force** $(T - D)$. When $T$ is barely greater than $D$, the aircraft takes forever to accelerate. JATO/RATO increase $T$ by 20–60% for a short burst, cutting ground roll dramatically.

### 2.2 Logic Steps of JATO/RATO Operation

1. **Pre-Take-Off:** Pilot arms the JATO ignition circuit; rocket motors are armed but unlit.
2. **Brake Release:** Pilot releases wheel brakes; aircraft begins to roll on engine thrust alone.
3. **JATO Ignition:** When reaching $\sim 80 \ km/h$ (or immediately at brake release for some configurations), rocket motors are fired — usually by a **pyrotechnic squib** igniting the solid propellant grain.
4. **Augmented Acceleration:** Combined thrust ($T_{\text{engine}} + T_{\text{JATO}}$) accelerates the aircraft rapidly.
5. **Burnout:** After $t_b$ seconds (typically 10–15 s), propellant is exhausted; rocket pods are typically **jettisoned** or remain inert.
6. **Rotation and Climb:** At $V_{LOF}$ the pilot rotates; JATO thrust is no longer useful (and is often shut down to avoid dangerous over-thrust).

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of Why Rockets (not Turbojets) are Used

- **Rockets carry their own oxidizer** → work at zero forward speed, while turbojets need $V > 0$ to compress incoming air.
- **High thrust-to-weight ratio** ($T/W \approx 50:1$ for solids vs. $4:1$ for turbojets) → compact, lightweight boosters.
- **Short-duration high-thrust** → ideal for the take-off phase only.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 4**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|:-:|:--------:|:--------|:--------------|
| 1 | Specific impulse | $I_{sp} = \dfrac{T}{\dot{m} \cdot g}$ | seconds (s) |
| 2 | Total impulse | $I_{total} = \displaystyle\int_0^{t_b} T(t) \, dt$ | N·s |
| 3 | Propellant mass | $m_p = \dfrac{I_{total}}{g \cdot I_{sp}}$ | kg |
| 4 | Ground run (no JATO) | $s_{TO} = \dfrac{1.44 \, W^2}{g \, \rho \, S \, C_L \, (T - D)}$ | metres |
| 5 | Ground run (with JATO) | $s_{TO}^{JATO} = \dfrac{1.44 \, W^2}{g \, \rho \, S \, C_L \, (T + T_{JATO} - D)}$ | metres |
| 6 | Thrust augmentation factor | $\tau = \dfrac{T + T_{JATO}}{T}$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Ground run reduction ratio | $\dfrac{s_{TO}^{JATO}}{s_{TO}} = \dfrac{T - D}{T + T_{JATO} - D} \approx \dfrac{1}{\tau}$ (approx) | dimensionless |
| 8 | Lift-off speed | $V_{LOF} = 1.2 \, V_{stall}$ | m/s |
| 9 | Stall speed | $V_{stall} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2W}{\rho \, S \, C_{L,\max}}}$ | m/s |
| 10 | HLD lift augmentation ratio | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{C_{L,\max}^{\text{flap}}}{C_{L,\max}^{\text{clean}}}$ | typically 1.8 – 2.8 |
| 11 | Approach speed reduction | $\dfrac{V_{app}^{\text{flap}}}{V_{app}^{\text{clean}}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\varepsilon}}$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | Drag divergence (flap) | $C_{D,\text{flap}} = C_{D,0} + \Delta C_{D,\text{flap}}$ | — |
| 13 | Plain flap $\Delta C_L$ | $\Delta C_L = \eta_f \, \Delta\alpha_{\text{eff}} \, C_{L,\alpha}$ | — |
| 14 | Flap effectiveness | $\eta_f = \dfrac{\Delta C_L}{\Delta C_{L,\text{ref}}}$ | $\leq 1.0$ |
| 15 | Slot momentum coefficient | $C_\mu = \dfrac{\dot{m}_s \, V_s}{q_\infty \, c}$ | boundary layer control |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical notation rule:** In LaTeX inline math inside the table above, the magnitude of a quantity (e.g., the angle of attack change $\Delta\alpha$) uses the \Delta symbol, NOT the vertical pipe $\vert$, to keep Markdown table syntax intact.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Military operations** (e.g., F-15, F/A-18 carrier launches, B-52 heavy-weight take-offs): JATO/RATO allows loading more fuel and weapons.
- **Space Shuttle Solid Rocket Boosters (SRBs):** Industrial-scale JATO; provided 71.4% of lift-off thrust.
- **Civil STOL aircraft** (e.g., de Havilland Canada Dash 7, Fokker F-27): rely on high-lift devices rather than rockets for short-field performance.
- **Modern airliners** (Boeing 787, Airbus A350): use **slatted Fowler flaps** for high lift without rocket augmentation — economically preferred.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Reduction in Ground Run Due to JATO

**Starting point:** The aircraft's equation of motion on the runway (during ground roll, $L \approx 0$ at low speeds, so the weight is fully supported by the gear):

$$T - D - \mu W = \frac{W}{g}\frac{dV}{dt}$$

Using $V \, dV = a \, ds$ (where $s$ is the ground run), we get:

$$V \, dV = \frac{g}{W}(T - D - \mu W) \, ds$$

Drag at low speeds can be approximated using the rolling-friction form (drag negligible below rotation speed, since $V$ is small):

$$V \, dV \approx \frac{g}{W}(T - \mu W) \, ds$$

**Step 1:** Integrate from $V = 0$ to $V = V_{LOF}$, and from $s = 0$ to $s = s_{TO}$:

$$\int_0^{V_{LOF}} V \, dV = \frac{g}{W}(T - \mu W) \int_0^{s_{TO}} ds$$

**Step 2:** This yields:

$$\frac{V_{LOF}^2}{2} = \frac{g}{W}(T - \mu W) \, s_{TO}$$

**Step 3:** Solving for $s_{TO}$:

$$s_{TO} = \frac{W \, V_{LOF}^2}{2g(T - \mu W)}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $V_{LOF} = 1.2 \, V_{stall}$ and $V_{stall} = \sqrt{2W/(\rho S C_{L,\max})}$:

$$V_{LOF}^2 = (1.2)^2 \cdot \frac{2W}{\rho S C_{L,\max}} = \frac{2.88 \, W}{\rho S C_{L,\max}}$$

**Step 5:** Insert into $s_{TO}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
s_{TO} &= \frac{W}{2g(T - \mu W)} \cdot \frac{2.88 \, W}{\rho S C_{L,\max}} \\
&= \frac{1.44 \, W^2}{g \, \rho \, S \, C_{L,\max} \, (T - \mu W)}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the standard KTU form; in many textbooks the rolling friction $\mu W$ is absorbed into the drag term $D$ to give $s_{TO} = 1.44 W^2 / [g \rho S C_L (T - D)]$, exactly matching Cheat Sheet entry #4.

**Step 6:** With JATO augmentation, $T \rightarrow T + T_{JATO}$:

$$s_{TO}^{JATO} = \frac{1.44 \, W^2}{g \, \rho \, S \, C_{L,\max} \, (T + T_{JATO} - \mu W)}$$

**Step 7:** Take the ratio:

$$\frac{s_{TO}^{JATO}}{s_{TO}} = \frac{T - \mu W}{T + T_{JATO} - \mu W}$$

Defining the thrust augmentation factor $\tau = (T + T_{JATO}) / T$, and assuming $D \ll T$ (typical of take-off with high thrust-to-weight):

$$\frac{s_{TO}^{JATO}}{s_{TO}} \approx \frac{1}{\tau}$$

**Result:** A 50% thrust boost ($\tau = 1.5$) approximately cuts ground roll by 33% — a massive operational benefit.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Propellant Mass Required for a Given Burn Time

**Given:** A JATO rocket must deliver thrust $T_{JATO}$ for burn time $t_b$.

**Step 1:** Specific impulse definition:

$$I_{sp} = \frac{T}{\dot{m} \, g} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{m} = \frac{T}{g \, I_{sp}}$$

**Step 2:** Assuming constant thrust (a good approximation for most solid rockets):

$$\dot{m} = \text{const} \quad \Rightarrow \quad m_p = \dot{m} \cdot t_b = \frac{T \, t_b}{g \, I_{sp}}$$

**Step 3:** For thrust-time profile $T(t)$ (e.g., tail-off burning), use:

$$m_p = \int_0^{t_b} \dot{m}(t) \, dt = \frac{1}{g \, I_{sp}} \int_0^{t_b} T(t) \, dt = \frac{I_{total}}{g \, I_{sp}}$$

**Example numerical evaluation:**

Let $T_{JATO} = 30,000 \ N$, $t_b = 12 \ s$, $I_{sp} = 230 \ s$.

$$m_p = \frac{30{,}000 \times 12}{9.81 \times 230} = \frac{360{,}000}{2256.3} \approx 159.6 \ kg$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that the JATO rocket delivers $30 \ kN$ for $12 \ s$ using only $\sim 160 \ kg$ of propellant — an extraordinary mass efficiency compared to a turbojet that would weigh tonnes for the same purpose.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Reduction in Stall Speed Using High Lift Devices

**Step 1:** Stall speed (clean configuration):

$$V_{stall,\text{clean}} = \sqrt{\frac{2W}{\rho \, S \, C_{L,\max,\text{clean}}}}$$

**Step 2:** Stall speed (with flaps deployed):

$$V_{stall,\text{flap}} = \sqrt{\frac{2W}{\rho \, S \, C_{L,\max,\text{flap}}}}$$

**Step 3:** Take the ratio:

$$\frac{V_{stall,\text{flap}}}{V_{stall,\text{clean}}} = \sqrt{\frac{C_{L,\max,\text{clean}}}{C_{L,\max,\text{flap}}}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\varepsilon}}$$

**Step 4:** Where $\varepsilon$ is the lift augmentation ratio (Cheat Sheet entry #10):

$$\varepsilon = \frac{C_{L,\max,\text{flap}}}{C_{L,\max,\text{clean}}}$$

**Typical numerical example:** If $\varepsilon = 2.0$:

$$\frac{V_{stall,\text{flap}}}{V_{stall,\text{clean}}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.707$$

**Result:** A 30% reduction in approach speed — translating directly to safer landings and shorter landing distances.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Plain Flap Lift Increment (Thin-Airfoil Theory Approach)

**Step 1:** The plain hinged flap increases the effective camber of the wing. The basic thin-airfoil lift slope is:

$$C_{L,\alpha} = \frac{2\pi}{\sqrt{1 - M^2}}$$

**Step 2:** For a flap deflection $\delta_f$, the additional lift coefficient (per unit span) is given approximately by the **Helmbold-Diederich approximation** for small flap chord ratios $c_f / c$:

$$\Delta C_L = \eta_f \cdot \left(\frac{c_f}{c}\right) \cdot \delta_f \cdot C_{L,\alpha}$$

**Step 3:** Where $\eta_f$ is the flap effectiveness factor ($\eta_f < 1$ for finite-span wings due to three-dimensional relief), and $\delta_f$ is the flap deflection in radians.

**Step 4:** The total maximum lift coefficient becomes:

$$C_{L,\max,\text{flap}} = C_{L,\max,\text{clean}} + \Delta C_L$$

This relationship is used in preliminary aircraft design to size the flap area and deflection to achieve a target $\varepsilon$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture: JATO/RATO Integration with Aircraft

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Pilot Cockpit Control Panel] -->|Arm Command| nodeB[JATO Master Arm Relay]
    nodeB -->|Squib Fire Signal| nodeC[Pyrotechnic Igniter]
    nodeC -->|Ignition Pulse| nodeD[Solid Propellant Grain]
    nodeD -->|Combustion Gases| nodeE[Convergent-Divergent Nozzle]
    nodeE -->|Thrust Jet T_JATO| nodeF[External Rocket Pod Mounted on Aircraft]
    nodeF -->|Reaction Force| nodeG[Aircraft Airframe]
    nodeG -->|Combined Thrust T + T_JATO| nodeH[Runway Ground Run Reduction]
    nodeI[Burnout Sensor] -->|t gt t_burnout| nodeJ[Pod Jettison Circuit]
    nodeJ -->|Mechanical Release| nodeF
    nodeK[Engine Thrust T] --> nodeG
    nodeL[Aerodynamic Drag D] -.->|Opposes| nodeG
    nodeM[Rolling Friction muW] -.->|Opposes| nodeG
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block-level functional diagram shows the **signal and force flow** during a JATO-assisted take-off, mapping the cockpit initiation to the final thrust augmentation and burnout/jettison sequence.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: High Lift Device Deployment Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Take-off / Landing Phase Initiation] -->|Command Signal| nodeB[Flight Control Computer]
    nodeB -->|Hydraulic Actuation| nodeC[Leading Edge Devices]
    nodeB -->|Hydraulic Actuation| nodeD[Trailing Edge Flaps]
    nodeC --> nodeC1[Slats Extend and Form Slots]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[Krueger Flap Deploys]
    nodeD --> nodeD1[Plain Flap Hinge Down]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[Slat Tracks Translate Rearward]
    nodeD --> nodeD3[Slot Opens Between Flap and Wing]
    nodeC1 --> nodeE[Combined C_L_max Augmentation]
    nodeC2 --> nodeE
    nodeD1 --> nodeE
    nodeD2 --> nodeE
    nodeD3 --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF[Reduced V_stall and V_app]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Shorter Take-off and Landing Distances]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Improved Low-Speed Safety Margin]
```

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Flap Type Comparison Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[High Lift Devices Classification] --> nodeB[Trailing Edge Flaps]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Leading Edge Devices]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Boundary Layer Control]
    nodeB --> nodeB1[Plain Flap: Camber Increase Only]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[Split Flap: Camber plus Sharp TE]
    nodeB --> nodeB3[Slotted Flap: Slot Delays Separation]
    nodeB --> nodeB4[Fowler Flap: Area plus Camber plus Slot]
    nodeB --> nodeB5[Triple Slotted Flap: Maximum Performance]
    nodeC --> nodeC1[Fixed Slat]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[Movable Slat]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[Krueger Flap]
    nodeD --> nodeD1[Vortex Generators]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[Suction Slots]
    nodeD --> nodeD3[Blowing Jets]
    nodeB5 --> nodeE[Highest C_L_max Achieved 2.8 to 3.5 plus]
    nodeD3 --> nodeF[Active Circulation Control]
```

---

### 4.4 Schematic: Take-off Distance Geometry (Side View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[V equals 0] -->|Ground Roll s_TO| nodeB[V equals V_LOF]
    nodeB -->|Transition s_TRANS| nodeC[V equals 1.1 V_stall]
    nodeC -->|Climb-out Distance| nodeD[Obstacle Clearance Height 35 ft]
    nodeD -->|Continued Climb| nodeE[Second Segment Climb]
    nodeE -->|Cruise Climb| nodeF[Initial Cruise Altitude]
    nodeG[JATO Active Phase 10 to 15 s] -.->|Augments T| nodeA
    nodeH[Flaps at Take-off Setting] -.->|Raises C_L_max| nodeB
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The JATO phase is active **only during the initial ground roll** (node A → node B). Once airborne, the rocket pod is typically jettisoned. The flap setting during this phase is the partial take-off setting (e.g., Flaps 5°–15°), not the full landing configuration.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper - Set A]**

**Q1. Define JATO. Why are rockets (and not turbojets) used as the auxiliary propulsion unit for assisted take-off?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Jet Assisted Take-Off (JATO) is an auxiliary propulsion system in which rocket motors (or, in some installations, water-injected turbojets) are attached to an aircraft to provide supplementary thrust during the initial take-off ground roll, thereby reducing the required runway length.

**Why rockets (1.5 marks):**
1. **Self-contained oxidizer:** Rockets carry their own oxidizer, allowing them to deliver full thrust even at zero forward airspeed, whereas turbojets require forward motion to ingest and compress air.
2. **High thrust-to-weight ratio:** Solid rocket boosters achieve $T/W$ ratios of $\sim 50:1$, vastly higher than turbojets ($\sim 4:1$), making them compact and lightweight.
3. **Short-duration, high-thrust profile:** Ideal for the 10–15 second take-off phase, after which the rockets are jettisoned.

---

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper - Set B]**

**Q2. List any four high-lift devices used on subsonic transport aircraft and state the aerodynamic purpose of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Device | Aerodynamic Purpose |
|:-------|:--------------------|
| (a) Plain Flap (1 mark) | Increases wing camber → raises $C_{L,\max}$ |
| (b) Slotted Flap (1 mark) | Allows high-energy air from below to energize boundary layer over flap → delays flow separation, allowing higher $\alpha$ before stall |
| (c) Fowler Flap (1 mark) | Increases wing planform area AND camber AND introduces a slot → maximizes $C_{L,\max}$ |
| (d) Leading Edge Slat (1 mark) | Forms a leading-edge slot that directs high-energy air over upper wing surface → delays stall, raises $C_{L,\max}$ |

Alternative acceptable devices: split flap, triple-slotted flap, Krueger flap, vortex generators.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — JATO/HLD Questions:**
> - **Common mistake 1:** Using the wrong stall speed (e.g., using $V_{LOF}$ instead of $V_{stall}$ in the ground run derivation). **Penalty: −2 marks.**
> - **Common mistake 2:** Forgetting to specify the **assumptions** (e.g., constant thrust, negligible drag, level runway). **Penalty: −1 mark.**
> - **Common mistake 3:** In HLD questions, confusing $C_L$ (any $\alpha$) with $C_{L,\max}$ (at stall). The augmentation ratio $\varepsilon$ is defined at the MAXIMUM, not at an arbitrary angle. **Penalty: −2 marks.**
> - **Common mistake 4:** Writing the augmentation ratio as $\varepsilon = C_{L,\max}/C_{L,\text{clean}}$ instead of $\varepsilon = C_{L,\max,\text{flap}} / C_{L,\max,\text{clean}}$. **Penalty: −1 mark.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]*

**(a)** Derive the expression for the take-off ground run of an aircraft, with and without JATO augmentation. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A military transport aircraft weighing $W = 80,000 \ kgf$ at take-off has a wing area $S = 150 \ m^2$, maximum lift coefficient $C_{L,\max} = 1.6$, and is powered by four turbojet engines each delivering $T = 30 \ kN$ of static thrust at sea level ($\rho = 1.225 \ kg/m^3$). The rolling friction coefficient is $\mu = 0.025$ and the drag at take-off is $D = 15 \ kN$. To reduce the ground run, two JATO rockets are fitted, each delivering $T_{JATO} = 50 \ kN$ for 12 seconds.

Compute:
- (i) The take-off ground run **without** JATO.
- (ii) The take-off ground run **with** JATO.
- (iii) The percentage reduction in ground run. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

(See Section 3.1 of these notes for full derivation. Summary of key steps expected:)

- **Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the equation of motion during ground roll: $T - D - \mu W = (W/g)(dV/dt)$.
- **Step 2 (1 mark):** Use the kinematic identity $V \, dV = a \, ds$ to convert to distance form.
- **Step 3 (1 mark):** State the assumption $L \approx 0$ during the initial low-speed roll (lifting off the brakes) and treat $T, D, W$ as constants.
- **Step 4 (2 marks):** Integrate from $V=0$ to $V = V_{LOF} = 1.2 V_{stall}$ and substitute $V_{stall}^2 = 2W/(\rho S C_{L,\max})$ to obtain:
$$s_{TO} = \frac{1.44 \, W^2}{g \, \rho \, S \, C_{L,\max} \, (T - D)}$$
- **Step 5 (1 mark):** Repeat with $T \rightarrow T + T_{JATO}$ to get the JATO-augmented form.
- **Step 6 (1 mark):** State all assumptions explicitly: level runway, no wind, constant thrust, drag absorbed or negligible, $\mu =$ const, instantaneous lift-off at $V = V_{LOF}$.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $W = 80,000 \times 9.81 = 784,800 \ N$
- $S = 150 \ m^2$
- $C_{L,\max} = 1.6$
- $\rho = 1.225 \ kg/m^3$
- $T_{\text{total,engine}} = 4 \times 30 = 120 \ kN = 120,000 \ N$
- $D = 15 \ kN = 15,000 \ N$
- $\mu W = 0.025 \times 784,800 = 19,620 \ N$ (this is the rolling friction contribution)
- $T_{JATO} = 2 \times 50 = 100 \ kN = 100,000 \ N$

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Calculate $V_{stall}$ and $V_{LOF}$:

$$V_{stall} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 784,800}{1.225 \times 150 \times 1.6}} = \sqrt{\frac{1,569,600}{294}} = \sqrt{5338.78} \approx 73.07 \ m/s$$

$$V_{LOF} = 1.2 \times 73.07 \approx 87.69 \ m/s$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute $s_{TO}$ without JATO. Using the rolling-friction form:

$$s_{TO} = \frac{W \, V_{LOF}^2}{2g(T - D - \mu W)}$$

$$= \frac{784,800 \times (87.69)^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times (120,000 - 15,000 - 19,620)}$$

$$= \frac{784,800 \times 7689.66}{19.62 \times 85,380}$$

$$= \frac{6.034 \times 10^9}{1.675 \times 10^6} \approx 3603 \ m$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute $s_{TO}$ with JATO. Net accelerating force becomes:

$$T_{total} - D - \mu W = 120,000 + 100,000 - 15,000 - 19,620 = 185,380 \ N$$

$$s_{TO}^{JATO} = \frac{784,800 \times 7689.66}{19.62 \times 185,380} = \frac{6.034 \times 10^9}{3.637 \times 10^6} \approx 1659 \ m$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Percentage reduction:

$$\% \text{ reduction} = \frac{3603 - 1659}{3603} \times 100\% = \frac{1944}{3603} \times 100\% \approx 53.96\%$$

**Result:** JATO augmentation reduces ground run by **~54%**, from 3603 m to 1659 m.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — *[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]*

**(a)** Explain with neat functional diagrams the working principle of a **Fowler flap** and a **leading-edge slat**. Discuss how each contributes to the augmentation of $C_{L,\max}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An aircraft weighing $W = 50,000 \ N$ has a wing area $S = 80 \ m^2$ and a clean-wing $C_{L,\max,\text{clean}} = 1.4$. The aircraft operates at sea level ($\rho = 1.225 \ kg/m^3$). On landing approach, the pilot deploys full flaps, achieving a lift augmentation ratio of $\varepsilon = 2.4$.

Compute:
- (i) The clean-wing stall speed.
- (ii) The flap-deployed stall speed.
- (iii) The reduction in approach speed, expressed as a percentage. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Functional Description (7 marks):**

**Fowler Flap (3.5 marks):**
- The Fowler flap is a multi-element trailing-edge device that **translates rearward along tracks** as it deflects downward.
- This translation **increases the effective wing planform area** (typically by 10–20%) — unique among trailing-edge flaps.
- Simultaneously, the flap introduces a **slot** between the wing trailing edge and the flap leading edge, allowing high-energy air from below to flow over the flap's upper surface.
- **Effect on $C_{L,\max}$:** The area increase directly scales the maximum lift, the slot delays separation (allowing higher $\alpha$ before stall), and the increased camber further raises $C_L$.
- Result: $C_{L,\max}$ increases by 80–110% over the clean wing.

**Leading-Edge Slat (3.5 marks):**
- The leading-edge slat is a small auxiliary aerofoil positioned just ahead of the wing leading edge, which **extends forward and down** to form a **slot** between the slat and the main wing.
- The slot directs a thin sheet of **high-energy, undisturbed air** over the upper surface of the main wing.
- This **re-energizes the boundary layer**, delaying the onset of leading-edge separation.
- **Effect on $C_{L,\max}$:** Allows the wing to operate at much higher angles of attack before stall — typically adding 0.4–0.8 to the $C_{L,\max}$ value.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $W = 50,000 \ N$, $S = 80 \ m^2$, $C_{L,\max,\text{clean}} = 1.4$, $\varepsilon = 2.4$, $\rho = 1.225 \ kg/m^3$.

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Clean-wing stall speed:

$$V_{stall,\text{clean}} = \sqrt{\frac{2W}{\rho S C_{L,\max,\text{clean}}}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 50,000}{1.225 \times 80 \times 1.4}}$$

$$= \sqrt{\frac{100,000}{137.2}} = \sqrt{728.86} \approx 27.0 \ m/s \approx 97.2 \ km/h$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Flap-deployed $C_{L,\max}$:

$$C_{L,\max,\text{flap}} = \varepsilon \times C_{L,\max,\text{clean}} = 2.4 \times 1.4 = 3.36$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Flap-deployed stall speed:

$$V_{stall,\text{flap}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 50,000}{1.225 \times 80 \times 3.36}} = \sqrt{\frac{100,000}{329.28}} = \sqrt{303.69} \approx 17.43 \ m/s \approx 62.7 \ km/h$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Percentage reduction in approach speed:

$$\% \text{ reduction} = \frac{27.0 - 17.43}{27.0} \times 100\% = \frac{9.57}{27.0} \times 100\% \approx 35.4\%$$

**Result:** A 35.4% reduction in approach speed — substantial improvement in landing safety margin.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4 (KTU 2024)**

**Jet/Rocket Assisted Take-Off:**
- [x] JATO = Jet Assisted Take-Off; RATO = Rocket Assisted Take-Off (often synonymous; "jet" refers to exhaust jet, not turbojet)
- [x] Rockets used because they carry their own oxidizer (work at $V = 0$) and have high $T/W$
- [x] Specific impulse formula: $I_{sp} = T / (\dot{m} \cdot g)$ — remember the factor of $g$
- [x] Total impulse: $I_{total} = \int T \, dt$; propellant mass: $m_p = I_{total} / (g I_{sp})$
- [x] Ground run formula: $s_{TO} = 1.44 W^2 / [g \rho S C_{L,\max} (T - D)]$ (KTU standard form)
- [x] With JATO, replace $T$ with $T + T_{JATO}$
- [x] Lift-off speed: $V_{LOF} = 1.2 \, V_{stall}$
- [x] JATO burn time typically 10–15 seconds; pods jettisoned after burnout
- [x] Real examples: Space Shuttle SRBs, carrier-launched fighters, B-52 heavy take-offs

**High Lift Devices:**
- [x] Purpose: increase $C_{L,\max}$ to reduce $V_{stall}$ and approach/take-off speeds
- [x] Lift augmentation ratio: $\varepsilon = C_{L,\max,\text{flap}} / C_{L,\max,\text{clean}}$ (typically 1.8–2.8)
- [x] Stall speed reduction: $V_{stall,\text{flap}} / V_{stall,\text{clean}} = 1 / \sqrt{\varepsilon}$
- [x] **Plain flap:** hinged trailing edge → increases camber only
- [x] **Split flap:** trailing edge deflected down, leaving upper surface intact → camber + sharp TE
- [x] **Slotted flap:** introduces slot → delays separation, allows higher $\alpha$
- [x] **Fowler flap:** translates rearward → increases wing **area** + camber + slot (best of all worlds)
- [x] **Triple-slotted flap:** three slots → maximum $C_{L,\max}$ augmentation (used on large transports like B-747)
- [x] **Leading-edge slat:** forms a slot at LE → re-energizes upper-surface boundary layer, delays stall
- [x] **Krueger flap:** hinged LE flap that increases LE camber → alternative to slat
- [x] Penalty of flaps: increased drag and pitching moment (down on tail)

**Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
- [x] Don't confuse $C_L$ at an arbitrary $\alpha$ with $C_{L,\max}$ — augmentation is defined at the MAXIMUM.
- [x] Don't forget the gravitational constant $g$ in specific impulse equations.
- [x] Always state assumptions explicitly in derivations (level runway, no wind, constant thrust, etc.).
- [x] Use SI units consistently (N, m, s, kg, m/s) — KTU strictly penalizes mixed units.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Range and Endurance of Airplanes: Charts for Piston and Jet Engine Aircrafts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET753 - AEROSPACE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Range and Endurance of Airplanes: Charts for Piston and Jet Engine Aircrafts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Range and Endurance

In the context of KTU PEMET753 Module 4, two of the most critical performance metrics for any fixed-wing aircraft are its **Range** and **Endurance**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Range (R):** The total horizontal distance an aircraft can travel from a point of take-off (or cruise start) with a full fuel tank until the fuel is completely exhausted. It is measured in **nautical miles (nm)** or **kilometers (km)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Endurance (E):** The total time an aircraft can remain airborne on a given fuel load under specified flight conditions. It is measured in **hours (hr)** or **minutes (min)**.

The cruise segment of flight is where these two performance parameters are optimized, assuming steady, level, unaccelerated flight (i.e., $L = W$ and $T = D$).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car from your hometown to another city:

- **Endurance** is the answer to *"How many hours can I keep the engine running on this tank of petrol before it runs dry?"*
- **Range** is the answer to *"How many kilometers can I travel on this same tank?"*

In an aircraft, endurance is dominated by **how efficiently the engine burns fuel per unit of time** (fuel flow rate), while range is dominated by **how efficiently the aircraft converts fuel energy into useful distance** (i.e., aerodynamic efficiency combined with fuel efficiency).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (KTU Syllabus Highlight):**
> The mathematical formulation for **Range** and **Endurance** differs fundamentally between **piston-engine (propeller-driven) aircraft** and **turbojet/turbofan (jet-engine) aircraft**. This is because:
> - For **piston aircraft**, power is approximately constant with speed at altitude → fuel flow depends on **power (P)** and brake specific fuel consumption **c**.
> - For **jet aircraft**, thrust is approximately constant with speed at altitude → fuel flow depends on **thrust (T)** and thrust specific fuel consumption **c_T**.

### 1.3 Why Are Charts Used?

Because the Breguet equations contain complex aerodynamic terms (such as $C_L^{3/2}/C_D$ and $\sqrt{C_L}/C_D$), and because the optimum $C_L$ for maximum range/endurance depends on aircraft weight, altitude, and configuration, engineers use **standardized performance charts** to:

1. Visually identify the optimum **angle of attack** ($\alpha^*$) or **airspeed** ($V^*$) for any mission.
2. Compute $R$ and $E$ as a function of the **weight ratio** $W_0/W_1$.
3. Trade-off between range-optimized and endurance-optimized flight conditions.

These charts are called the **Breguet Performance Charts** or **Range/Endurance Polars**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Aerodynamic and Propulsion Relations

For **steady, level, unaccelerated flight**:

$$ L = W \quad \text{and} \quad T = D $$

The total drag coefficient (assuming parabolic drag polar):

$$ C_D = C_{D_0} + K C_L^2 $$

where:
- $C_{D_0}$ = zero-lift drag coefficient (parasitic drag)
- $K = \frac{1}{\pi \, e \, AR}$ = induced drag factor
- $AR = b^2/S$ = aspect ratio of the wing

The corresponding **lift-to-drag ratio** is:

$$ \frac{L}{D} = \frac{C_L}{C_D} = \frac{C_L}{C_{D_0} + K C_L^2} $$

The **maximum L/D** occurs at:

$$ C_L^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{C_{D_0}}{K}} \quad \text{and} \quad \left(\frac{L}{D}\right)_{\max} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{K C_{D_0}}} $$

The true airspeed at any $C_L$ is:

$$ V = \sqrt{\frac{2W}{\rho \, S \, C_L}} $$

### 2.2 Piston-Propeller Aircraft — Governing Equations

For a piston-engine aircraft, the shaft power output is **assumed constant** at a given altitude and RPM. The fuel flow rate is:

$$ \frac{dW_f}{dt} = c \cdot P_{shaft} $$

where $c$ is the **brake specific fuel consumption (BSFC)** in **lb/hr/hp** (or kg/kW/hr in SI).

The propeller efficiency $\eta_p$ relates thrust to power:

$$ T \cdot V = \eta_p \cdot P_{shaft} \implies P_{shaft} = \frac{T \cdot V}{\eta_p} = \frac{D \cdot V}{\eta_p} $$

So the rate of weight change is:

$$ -\frac{dW}{dt} = c \cdot P_{shaft} = \frac{c \cdot D \cdot V}{\eta_p} = \frac{c \cdot V}{\eta_p} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_D $$

$$ \boxed{-\frac{dW}{dt} = \frac{c}{\eta_p} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho S C_D \cdot V^3} $$

### 2.3 Jet Engine Aircraft — Governing Equations

For a jet engine, the **thrust** is assumed constant, and the fuel flow rate is:

$$ \frac{dW_f}{dt} = c_T \cdot T $$

where $c_T$ is the **thrust specific fuel consumption (TSFC)** in **hr⁻¹** (or 1/s in SI).

The rate of weight change is:

$$ -\frac{dW}{dt} = c_T \cdot T = c_T \cdot D = c_T \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_D $$

$$ \boxed{-\frac{dW}{dt} = c_T \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_D} $$

### 2.4 The Breguet Equations (Compact Formula Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet — Memorize the four Breguet Equations below:**

| # | Aircraft Type | Endurance (E) | Range (R) | Optimum Parameter |
|---|---------------|---------------|-----------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Piston-Propeller** | $E = \dfrac{\eta_p}{c} \cdot \dfrac{C_L^{3/2}}{C_D} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\rho S}} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{W_0}{W_1}\right)$ | $R = \dfrac{2\eta_p}{c} \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{C_L}}{C_D} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\rho S}} \cdot \left(\sqrt{W_0}-\sqrt{W_1}\right)$ | Max $C_L^{3/2}/C_D$ for $E$, max $\sqrt{C_L}/C_D$ for $R$ |
| 2 | **Jet (Turbofan/Turbojet)** | $E = \dfrac{1}{c_T} \cdot \dfrac{C_L}{C_D} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{W_0}{W_1}\right)$ | $R = \dfrac{2}{c_T} \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{C_L}}{C_D} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\rho S}} \cdot \left(\sqrt{W_0}-\sqrt{W_1}\right)$ | Max $C_L/C_D$ for both $E$ and $R$ |

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Units |
|--------|---------|---------------|
| $W_0$ | Initial (take-off) weight | N or lbf or kg |
| $W_1$ | Final (empty + reserve) weight | N or lbf or kg |
| $\eta_p$ | Propeller efficiency | dimensionless (~0.8) |
| $c$ | BSFC (piston) | 1/hr or lb/hr/hp |
| $c_T$ | TSFC (jet) | 1/hr or 1/s |
| $C_L$ | Lift coefficient | dimensionless |
| $C_D$ | Drag coefficient | dimensionless |
| $\rho$ | Air density at cruise altitude | kg/m³ or slug/ft³ |
| $S$ | Wing reference area | m² or ft² |

### 2.5 Optimum Lift Coefficients (Parabolic Drag Polar)

Using $C_D = C_{D_0} + K C_L^2$, the maxima of the three functions are found by calculus:

| Function | Optimum $C_L$ | Maximum Value |
|----------|---------------|---------------|
| $\dfrac{C_L}{C_D}$ (max $L/D$) | $C_L^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{C_{D_0}}{K}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{K C_{D_0}}}$ |
| $\dfrac{C_L^{3/2}}{C_D}$ (piston endurance) | $C_L^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{3 C_{D_0}}{K}}$ | $\dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{K C_{D_0}}} = 1.299 \cdot (L/D)_{\max}$ |
| $\dfrac{\sqrt{C_L}}{C_D}$ (jet range) | Numerical, $C_L^* \approx 1.69 \sqrt{\dfrac{C_{D_0}}{K}}$ | Slightly less than piston optimum |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering insight:** Notice that the optimum $C_L$ for endurance and range are **different**. This means the **best endurance speed** is *lower* than the **best range speed**. Pilots select $V_{E_{\max}}$ for loitering (e.g., search and rescue, surveillance) and $V_{R_{\max}}$ for ferry flights and airline routes.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Piston-Propeller Endurance

**Step 1 — Start with the fuel flow equation in terms of weight:**

The fuel consumed per unit time equals the rate of change of aircraft weight:

$$ \frac{dW_f}{dt} = -\frac{dW}{dt} $$

**Step 2 — Relate fuel flow to power and SFC:**

For a piston engine, fuel flow (weight per time) = SFC × Power:

$$ -\frac{dW}{dt} = c \cdot P_{shaft} $$

**Step 3 — Substitute propeller power relation $P_{shaft} = TV/\eta_p$:**

$$ -\frac{dW}{dt} = \frac{c \cdot T \cdot V}{\eta_p} $$

**Step 4 — Apply cruise condition $T = D$:**

$$ -\frac{dW}{dt} = \frac{c \cdot D \cdot V}{\eta_p} $$

**Step 5 — Substitute drag equation $D = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_D$:**

$$ -\frac{dW}{dt} = \frac{c}{\eta_p} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho S C_D \cdot V^3 $$

**Step 6 — Substitute true airspeed from $L = W = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_L \Rightarrow V = \sqrt{2W/(\rho S C_L)}$:**

$$ V^3 = \left(\frac{2W}{\rho S C_L}\right)^{3/2} $$

So the equation becomes:

$$ -\frac{dW}{dt} = \frac{c}{\eta_p} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho S C_D \cdot \left(\frac{2W}{\rho S C_L}\right)^{3/2} $$

**Step 7 — Simplify algebraically:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
-\frac{dW}{dt} &= \frac{c}{\eta_p} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho S C_D \cdot \frac{(2W)^{3/2}}{(\rho S C_L)^{3/2}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{c}{\eta_p} \cdot \frac{C_D}{C_L^{3/2}} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{(2W)^{3/2}}{(\rho S)^{3/2}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{c}{\eta_p} \cdot \frac{C_D}{C_L^{3/2}} \cdot \frac{2\sqrt{2} \cdot W^{3/2}}{2 \cdot (\rho S)^{3/2}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{c}{\eta_p} \cdot \frac{C_D}{C_L^{3/2}} \cdot \frac{W^{3/2} \cdot \sqrt{2}}{(\rho S)^{3/2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Separate variables and integrate from $W_0$ to $W_1$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
-\frac{dW}{W^{3/2}} &= \frac{c}{\eta_p} \cdot \frac{C_D}{C_L^{3/2}} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2}}{(\rho S)^{3/2}} \, dt \\[6pt]
\int_{W_0}^{W_1} W^{-3/2} \, dW &= \frac{c}{\eta_p} \cdot \frac{C_D}{C_L^{3/2}} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2}}{(\rho S)^{3/2}} \int_{0}^{E} dt \\[6pt]
\left[-2 W^{-1/2}\right]_{W_0}^{W_1} &= \frac{c}{\eta_p} \cdot \frac{C_D}{C_L^{3/2}} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2}}{(\rho S)^{3/2}} \cdot E \\[6pt]
2 \left(W_0^{-1/2} - W_1^{-1/2}\right) &= \frac{c}{\eta_p} \cdot \frac{C_D}{C_L^{3/2}} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2}}{(\rho S)^{3/2}} \cdot E
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9 — Solve for E:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= 2 \left(W_0^{-1/2} - W_1^{-1/2}\right) \cdot \frac{\eta_p}{c} \cdot \frac{C_L^{3/2}}{C_D} \cdot \frac{(\rho S)^{3/2}}{\sqrt{2}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\eta_p}{c} \cdot \frac{C_L^{3/2}}{C_D} \cdot \frac{2 (\rho S)^{3/2}}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{W_1} - \sqrt{W_0}}{\sqrt{W_0 W_1}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\eta_p}{c} \cdot \frac{C_L^{3/2}}{C_D} \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho S} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{W_1} - \sqrt{W_0}}{\sqrt{W_0 W_1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For small fuel fractions (typical cruise), $\sqrt{W_0 W_1} \approx W_0$ and this simplifies. **However, the canonical Breguet form** is obtained by integrating $V$ instead of $t$ (i.e., for range). To obtain the Breguet *endurance* form, we must use:

$$ dt = -\frac{dW}{c D V/\eta_p} \implies E = \int_0^E dt = \int_{W_0}^{W_1} \frac{-\eta_p \, dW}{c D V} $$

Using $V = \sqrt{2W/(\rho S C_L)}$ and $D = W \cdot C_D / C_L$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \int_{W_0}^{W_1} \frac{-\eta_p \, dW}{c \cdot (W C_D / C_L) \cdot \sqrt{2W/(\rho S C_L)}} \\[6pt]
&= \int_{W_0}^{W_1} \frac{-\eta_p}{c} \cdot \frac{C_L}{C_D} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho S C_L}{2 W}} \cdot \frac{dW}{W} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\eta_p}{c} \cdot \frac{C_L^{3/2}}{C_D} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho S}{2}} \int_{W_0}^{W_1} W^{-3/2} \, dW
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 10 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \frac{\eta_p}{c} \cdot \frac{C_L^{3/2}}{C_D} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho S}{2}} \cdot \left[ -2 W^{-1/2} \right]_{W_0}^{W_1} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\eta_p}{c} \cdot \frac{C_L^{3/2}}{C_D} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho S}{2}} \cdot 2 \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{W_0}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{W_1}} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 11 — Final Breguet Piston Endurance form** (combining terms and noting $\sqrt{2} \cdot 2 / \sqrt{2} = 2$):

$$ \boxed{E_{\text{piston}} = \frac{\eta_p}{c} \cdot \frac{C_L^{3/2}}{C_D} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2}{\rho S}} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{W_0}{W_1}\right)} $$

*(Note: the exact closed form contains a $1/\sqrt{W}$-dependence; the $\ln(W_0/W_1)$ form is the common engineering approximation valid when the bracketed expression is re-interpreted via the standard Breguet integral identity for $V \cdot dt$.)*

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Jet Engine Range

**Step 1 — Start with thrust and fuel flow:**

$$ -\frac{dW}{dt} = c_T \cdot T $$

**Step 2 — Apply $T = D$ and drag equation:**

$$ -\frac{dW}{dt} = c_T \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_D $$

**Step 3 — Range is $dR = V \, dt$:**

$$ dR = V \cdot dt = V \cdot \frac{-dW}{c_T \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_D} = \frac{-2 \, dW}{c_T \rho V S C_D} $$

**Step 4 — Substitute $V = \sqrt{2W/(\rho S C_L)}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
dR &= \frac{-2 \, dW}{c_T \rho S C_D \sqrt{2W/(\rho S C_L)}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{-2}{c_T \rho S C_D} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho S C_L}{2W}} \cdot dW \\[6pt]
&= \frac{-2}{c_T} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{C_L}}{C_D} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1}{2 \rho S W}} \cdot dW
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Integrate from $W_0$ to $W_1$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \int_{W_0}^{W_1} \frac{-2}{c_T} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{C_L}}{C_D} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \rho S}} \cdot W^{-1/2} \, dW \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2}{c_T} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{C_L}}{C_D} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \rho S}} \cdot \left[ 2 W^{1/2} \right]_{W_0}^{W_1} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2}{c_T} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{C_L}}{C_D} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2}{\rho S}} \cdot \left( \sqrt{W_0} - \sqrt{W_1} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Breguet Jet Range:**

$$ \boxed{R_{\text{jet}} = \frac{2}{c_T} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{C_L}}{C_D} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2}{\rho S}} \cdot \left( \sqrt{W_0} - \sqrt{W_1} \right)} $$

### 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Jet Engine Endurance

$$
\begin{aligned}
dt &= \frac{-dW}{c_T D} = \frac{-dW}{c_T \cdot (1/2) \rho V^2 S C_D} \\[6pt]
E &= \int_{W_0}^{W_1} \frac{-dW}{c_T \cdot (1/2) \rho V^2 S C_D}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $V^2 = 2W/(\rho S C_L)$ and $D = W C_D / C_L$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \int_{W_0}^{W_1} \frac{-dW}{c_T \cdot W \cdot (C_D/C_L)} = \frac{C_L}{c_T C_D} \int_{W_0}^{W_1} \frac{-dW}{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$ \boxed{E_{\text{jet}} = \frac{1}{c_T} \cdot \frac{C_L}{C_D} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{W_0}{W_1}\right)} $$

### 3.4 Summary — The Three Critical Functions Plotted on Charts

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Chart Recognition Table** (you will see these in exam plots):

| Curve Plotted | Symbol | Aircraft Type | Y-axis meaning | Optimum $C_L$ |
|---------------|--------|----------------|----------------|---------------|
| $C_L^{3/2}/C_D$ vs $C_L$ | Endurance Function | Piston | Multiply by $\eta_p/c \cdot \sqrt{2/(\rho S)}$ to get $E$ per ln-ratio | $\sqrt{3 C_{D_0}/K}$ |
| $\sqrt{C_L}/C_D$ vs $C_L$ | Range Function | Piston **and** Jet (same form) | Range multiplier | ~ $1.69\sqrt{C_{D_0}/K}$ (jet) |
| $C_L/C_D$ vs $C_L$ (or $L/D$ vs $\alpha$) | Aerodynamic Efficiency | All | Common aerodynamic polar | $\sqrt{C_{D_0}/K}$ |

The **Charts for Piston and Jet Engine Aircrafts** (the title of this KTU Module 4 topic) are precisely these:

1. **The Aerodynamic Polar**: $C_D$ vs $C_L$ (or $C_L/C_D$ vs $C_L$).
2. **The Endurance Chart**: $C_L^{3/2}/C_D$ vs $C_L$ — used for piston.
3. **The Range Chart**: $\sqrt{C_L}/C_D$ vs $C_L$ — used for both, but with different constants.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — How Range/Endurance Charts Are Used

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Define Mission Requirements] --> B{Engine Type?}
    B -->|Piston-Propeller| C[Use BSFC c and Propeller Efficiency η_p]
    B -->|Turbojet Turbofan| D[Use TSFC c_T Only]
    C --> E[Compute Aerodynamic Polar C_D vs C_L]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Generate Three Curves]
    F --> G1[Curve 1: C_L over C_D vs C_L for Jet E and R base]
    F --> G2[Curve 2: C_L^3/2 over C_D vs C_L for Piston E]
    F --> G3[Curve 3: sqrt C_L over C_D vs C_L for Piston and Jet R]
    G1 --> H[Find Optimum C_L Star from dF over dC_L = 0]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I[Compute Optimum Airspeed V Star from L = W]
    I --> J[Insert into Breguet Equations with W_0 W_1]
    J --> K[Final R and E Values]
    K --> L[Cross Check with Flight Test Data]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Comparison of Piston vs Jet Breguet Equations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PISTON[PISTON PROPELLER AIRCRAFT]
        P1[Engine Power P approx constant] --> P2[Fuel Flow = c × P]
        P2 --> P3[Propeller Coupling T × V = eta_p × P]
        P3 --> P4[Range R = 2 eta_p over c × sqrt C_L over C_D × sqrt 2 over rho S × sqrt W0 minus sqrt W1]
        P3 --> P5[Endurance E = eta_p over c × C_L^3/2 over C_D × sqrt 2 over rho S × ln W0 over W1]
    end
    subgraph JET[JET ENGINE AIRCRAFT]
        J1[Engine Thrust T approx constant] --> J2[Fuel Flow = c_T × T]
        J2 --> J3[Direct Thrust Drag Balance T = D]
        J3 --> J4[Range R = 2 over c_T × sqrt C_L over C_D × sqrt 2 over rho S × sqrt W0 minus sqrt W1]
        J3 --> J5[Endurance E = 1 over c_T × C_L over C_D × ln W0 over W1]
    end
    P1 -.Contrasts.-> J1
    P4 -.Same Range Function.-> J4
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Aerodynamic Polar Generating the Breguet Charts

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Flight Test or Wind Tunnel Data] --> B[Tabulate C_L vs alpha]
    A --> C[Tabulate C_D vs alpha]
    B --> D[Plot Parabolic Drag Polar C_D = C_D0 + K C_L^2]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Compute C_L over C_D]
    D --> F[Compute C_L^3/2 over C_D]
    D --> G[Compute sqrt C_L over C_D]
    E --> H[Aerodynamic Efficiency Chart]
    F --> I[Piston Endurance Chart]
    G --> J[Range Chart for Both Engine Types]
    H --> K[Optimum C_L Star at sqrt C_D0 over K]
    I --> L[Optimum C_L Star at sqrt 3 C_D0 over K]
    J --> M[Optimum C_L Star at approx 1.69 sqrt C_D0 over K]
    K --> N[Insert into Breguet Equation for Final R or E]
    L --> N
    M --> N
```

### 4.4 Diagram — Effect of Weight Ratio on Range and Endurance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Aircraft Initial Weight W_0] --> B[Fuel Burn During Cruise]
    B --> C[Aircraft Final Weight W_1]
    C --> D{Compute Two Ratios}
    D --> E[Weight Ratio W_0 over W_1 for Endurance]
    D --> F[Weight Difference sqrt W_0 minus sqrt W_1 for Range]
    E --> G[Endurance E grows with ln W_0 over W_1]
    F --> H[Range R grows with sqrt W_0 minus sqrt W_1]
    G --> I[Logarithmic Growth]
    H --> J[Square Root Growth]
    I --> K[Diminishing Returns for Heavy Fuel Loads]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Engineering Compromise Limit Useful Fuel Fraction]
```
